oxygen levels being a factor and to elaborate a bit further on what I'm talking about here one obvious variable that needs to be factored in is ocean scavengers things like sharks crabs and small fish will tend to pick apart a body pretty quickly depending on the animal activity there can be a pretty drastic variance in a body's decomposition and just recently in another study on body decomposition cells ultimately leading to a preservational effect on the body for example in 2011 after a couple nearby earthquakes in Switzerland a body was found floating in Lake Brien the investigation estimated the body was as fresh as 6 months old but as it turned out adaper ended up creating a massive cocoon on the Torso of the body and x-rays later showed that it preserved much of the internal portions of - What’s it like growing up on a boat? | Living Differently: Kind: captions Language: en Imagine this is your home!&nbsp; Meter-high waves and no privacy.&nbsp; The Schwörer family has been living&nbsp; like this for the last 25 years. It is a lot of responsibility&nbsp; with so many people on board. Six children and their parents traveling around&nbsp; the world on a sailing ship — may sound dangerous… We were once chased by pirates. … but it’s also breathtakingly beautiful.&nbsp; So what is family life on a boat like? Welcome! Tromsø, Norway. That's where this&nbsp;&nbsp; Swiss family has dropped anchor&nbsp; from their 15-meter sailboat,&nbsp;&nbsp; “Pachamama” — literally “Mother&nbsp; Earth” in the language of the Incas. Now, I can show you how we&nbsp; live all together on this boat. So, this is our living&nbsp; room. We have the big table. Right across from the kitchen. Here is the fridge. Here are all the teas and coffees. Here we are cooking.&nbsp; And that is maybe interesting,&nbsp; because this oven can move.&nbsp; If we have a lot of wind and&nbsp; waves, we still can cook.&nbsp; So, the spaghetti or the&nbsp; soup is not flipping over. So here is one kid cabin. Here, we have another cabin. Alegra and Salina are sleeping here.&nbsp; They have a toilet, and of course&nbsp; everywhere is space to store things. So here we would have a shower&nbsp; actually, take this off and shower. 8 a.m., time for breakfast. Meals are taken on the enclosed deck.&nbsp; Every aspect of life for this family of&nbsp; eight takes place on just 20 square meters.&nbsp; The ship offers little opportunities for privacy. The kids actually never complained about&nbsp; having their own room or their own bed&nbsp;&nbsp; or maybe a little bigger bed - maybe&nbsp; because they just grow up like this. All of the children were born during their travels&nbsp; and are now between seven and 20 years old.&nbsp; Instead of growing up with roads, cars, or movies,&nbsp; they are growing up with animals and nature. Dario and his wife Sabine embarked&nbsp; on their unconventional life in 1999,&nbsp;&nbsp; setting off on a voyage around the world. The certified mountain guide wanted to climb the&nbsp;&nbsp; highest peak on every continent, relying solely&nbsp; on his own strength and the power of nature.&nbsp; His goal was to inspire&nbsp; people to protect our planet. To finance this expedition was&nbsp; really difficult in the beginning.&nbsp; Nowadays, we are lucky in a way that we have&nbsp;&nbsp; sponsorship and also people who&nbsp; think that we do good work and donate. The Schwörers had not planned to&nbsp; be living on the boat 25&nbsp;years later - now with children.&nbsp; Every family member pitches in. Alegra and Noé are busy hoisting the sails.&nbsp; Today they're heading into nearby fjords. Andri pilots the boat. He's 19&nbsp; years old, the second oldest. At seven years old, Vital is the youngest.&nbsp; The family has sailed round&nbsp; about 260,000 kilometers so far. When we need a doctor, therefore, I'm&nbsp; really lucky that I have an excellent wife.&nbsp; She's a nurse, an excellent nurse. We have also a small operation set,&nbsp;&nbsp; and we have a really good medication box. The children only go to school when&nbsp; their parents have work to do ashore.&nbsp; On board, they are homeschooled. This is allowed in many Swiss cantons.&nbsp; Math is on today's schedule. The older children,&nbsp;&nbsp; such as 14-year-old Alegra, learn&nbsp; using a digital school program.&nbsp; It allows Swiss students&nbsp; to study anywhere, anytime. That is really taking a lot pressure off&nbsp; me, because they have all the subjects&nbsp;&nbsp; online and they have their tasks they have to do. Don't the children miss their friends from school? Yes, I miss my friends from school.&nbsp; It's bad to have to leave them and say&nbsp; goodbye all the time but on the other&nbsp;&nbsp; hand you can sail around and experience&nbsp; not just one friend but many friends. Now it's time to go ashore. We try to go every day to do some sports,&nbsp;&nbsp; because you cannot be on the boat for&nbsp; such a long time, and this is like a must. The Schwörers are going climbing. Not only is this good exercise for muscles&nbsp;&nbsp; that are neglected while sailing, but it's also&nbsp; important for strengthening the family's bonds. The climbing for children, I think,&nbsp; is something like a life school,&nbsp;&nbsp; because you learn a lot. You&nbsp; learn about responsibility.&nbsp; And so, they have to trust each other.&nbsp; So, we build trust when we climb. Gathering plastic from the sea is&nbsp; also part of their life school.&nbsp; So far, they have fished a total of 85,000&nbsp; kilograms of trash out of the oceans. We take microplastic samples.&nbsp; And on the starboard side we take&nbsp; eDNA, where we can define then what&nbsp;&nbsp; kind of species are in the water. So, it's all about biodiversity. They send the samples to&nbsp; institutes to be researched. Microplastic comes over the fish and the birds&nbsp; into our food chain and we see already in some&nbsp;&nbsp; papers that it causes some cancer. So, it all comes back. It's Karma. And so, the Schwörers visit&nbsp; schools to urge teenagers&nbsp;&nbsp; and children to take action on climate change.&nbsp; They have spoken to around 175,000&nbsp; children in more than 100 countries. But their expedition is not without its risks.&nbsp; In 2017, they were caught in&nbsp; a violent storm off Iceland.&nbsp; Their boat was severely damaged—fortunately,&nbsp; the family was rescued unharmed. They have even been stalked by hungry polar bears.&nbsp; And in the South Pacific,&nbsp; pirates attacked their boat: So, we told them, 'Ah, great that you're coming.&nbsp; We like locals and we just have&nbsp; lunch. You're more than invited'.&nbsp; And the children went directly&nbsp; to them and asked them questions,&nbsp;&nbsp; and that was breaking their hearts. So, they became friendly. It takes a lot of courage and&nbsp; good fortune to undertake such&nbsp;&nbsp; a journey—and a strong sense of family.&nbsp; The question is: how long do&nbsp; they intend to continue sailing? I do it with all my heart, and it's&nbsp; my passion, and it makes totally sense to&nbsp;&nbsp; me to do something for the children of&nbsp; this planet, for the next generation.&nbsp; So, I have no other idea at the&nbsp; moment to do something better. Life on the world's seven seas— does&nbsp; it sound like something you'd be into?&nbsp; Tell us in the comments!

- Couple Shares Unfiltered Reality of Sailboat Life – Including Costs, Challenges & More: Kind: captions Language: en big wave we felt like we wanted some extra Adventure so we bought this boat and we've been living aboard fulltime for about a year and a half at this point since we left the UK in August 2022 we've now sailed about 3,000 nautical miles which feels like a really long way we've obviously had our share of great experiences but also some really terrifying ones we've grounded the boat we've hit rocks we flood the engine with salt water we ripped to sail we've suffered with seasickness a lot and I had a really severe level of sailing anxiety so living on a Sailing Boat isn't always super [Music] easy we decided to move move on to a boat because we were sort of ended up in a bit of a rut at home so we lived in the northwest of England and we'd bought a house and we' renovated it learned lots of new skills but we felt like we wanted some extra Adventure so I'd been watching a lot of YouTube videos on house renovations and then the YouTube algorithm suggested sailing YouTube channels and then one day I turned around to flick and suggested should we buy a boat and I was like no I didn't even really think about it I was like absolutely not this is not in my life plan i' never really sailed before either but Joe had some sailing background and had sailed at most of his life and we didn't talk about it again for I think it was a few months and meanwhile I was sort of reflecting and being like oh maybe actually this could be the adventure that we've been looking for but I wanted to set some boundaries as to how we did it so I wanted to make sure that we didn't sell our house like I didn't want to get off the housing ladder um I wanted to not quit my job I'd been freelancing for like 5 years I think at this point and I didn't want to lose any of that that I'd spent time building up and I wanted to make sure that if we did it we did it in in a sustainable way where we weren't just burning through our life savings so I went to Joe and said like okay these are what I'm thinking like yeah we could do it but if we did it this way and that's kind of where it started so we bought this boat nundes back in March 2022 nundes is a 1989 Gypsy 402 she's 12.4 m long about 3.8 M wide and she's got a draft which is how deep she sits in the water of about 2 m we paid about the equivalent of $48,000 on nundes which is quite cheap for a 40ft boat but then we did have a lot of money to spend on her to make her fit for full-time modern living so one of the first big jobs was to do a total rewire on nundes so we now have three 200 amp lithiumion phosphate batteries which gives us 600 amp hours of Total capacity what this means for us is that we can run electrical cooking equipment so we got an induction Hub and an air fryer and that is our only way of cooking on the boat we do not have gas anymore to charge those batteries there's a few methods the first method is we can charge the batteries off of the engine via an alternator and the second way is the solar panels we have 875 wats of solar panels and they are our primary charging source and the third option which is more of a backup option for when there's multiple days of no sun is we've got got a 2,200 W petrol generator after we' finished all of the electrical wiring we then moved on to the wet side of things where we basically replum the boat and we replaced all of the very old hose and we installed two water tanks these are flexible bladder type things and we have 400 L of capacity we um make sure that our water is divided into different sources because when you're living off grid if you have one massive 400 lit tank and if that gets compromised so you get like a saltwater leak into it then you end up with your entire water supply that's compromised whereas ours is split into a 200 L tank or 120 L tank and then we have about 80 l in Jerry cans so we then have multiple different sources that we can always draw upon if we did have any problems with water we also did things like we created an entirely new navigation panel so a brand new chart plotter battery monitor monitoring system new switchboards VHF radio all the works and we had to service the engine there were some parts that were very corroded and damaged and one of the other large upgrades we we finished with was the one thing that we didn't do ourselves and that was the stainless steel stern Arch which acts as both our dingy transport and also a home for all our solar panels or most of them it ended up costing us around $3 $35,000 it becomes very hard to distinguish between what was an upgrade and what was sort of a mandatory maintenance job so since we left the UK in August 2022 we've now sailed about 3,000 nautical miles so we left from um p pth in the UK and we sailed directly from Plymouth down the coast of Spain and Portugal we crossed over to Morocco we went to there for 3 months and then we sailed to jalta and then to Sardinia and then from Italy to Greece and we are now just by Athens the lifestyle of living sailing traveling working on a sailboat sounds pretty cool right but it's not all like blue skies and flat Seas it's can be quite stressful I compare it a lot to a roller coaster you have the most incredible highs and amazing days but then everything's extremes right so you also have the most the lowest of blows I've personally ever experienced and most stressful times so if you ask us one day how we find this lifestyle we might be like it's amazing it's so fun like we love it and then you ask us the next day and we'll be like it's awful take home this is Dreadful um so we've obviously had our share of great experiences but also some really terrifying ones we went through A Series of Unfortunate Events I would say so from losing our main cell and one of the like most treacherous or inFAMOUS stretches of water to we grounded the boat we hit a rock we lost our prop shaft like all these bad things happened over quite a condensed period of time and for me personally it left me feeling quite broken and I think think my um my mental health was quite affected and I anxiety where it got to the point where we would be in a Marina and the idea of like leaving the marina the next morning or something would have me just crying on the stairs to Joe being like I don't want to go um and when we did leave I'd then be lying down below in a full anxiety fit I guess totally useless so it meant that Joe was single-handedly sailing a lot of the Atlantic coast it actually took 3 months of us stopping sailing in Morocco for me to be able to reset my mindset and come at it with a more positive approach but you know it's very easy to look at this lifestyle and be like oh my God how incredible but it's definitely not always easy um it's important I think that people understand that when you are a liver board sailor you're totally self-reliant so you are the Cook you're the cleaner you're the plumber you're the engineer you're the electrician but you're also the first responder to a medical emergency you're the Lifeguard you're everything else so yeah I think that that adds a different level of stress maybe to the experience you very much have to be resilient and self- rign because even if you were to call for Rescue by the time the rescue boat might get to you it might already be too late your vat might be on the rocks and sinking or already sunk so you have to be able to fix all these problems yourself and it's really important that you do we knew that there would be sailing related challenges but we didn't realize how uncomfortable it would be in the peak of Summer on the Sailing Boat where you have no airon and no real way to cool off so we were in Sardinia in this horrible Heat Wave earlier this year and I think it got up to 48° and it was roasting you would jump in the sea and it wouldn't really cool you down and we had this prolonged period of just heat for weeks and that was probably the biggest surprise that I didn't really anticipate is you end up sort of just kind of going into a hibernation mode you just sort of stay down below out the sun you occasionally jump in the sea to cool off a little bit but then you don't really get to do anything like it's very hard to explore some of the places when the temperatures are that hot it's also interesting I thought that when we first got the bathe I'd be on the bow sunbathing the whole time I'm not I'm hiding from the Sun the whole time CU they just so over exposed to it in reality you just spend like 90% of your time hiding from the Sun some people say that living on a bow is expensive and some people say it's very cheap so which one is it well it's an impossible question isn't it it's sort of how long is a piece of string everybody lives to certain standards and has certain expectations for us personally we'd probably spend around $1,500 to $2,000 a month between us and that would cover groceries eating out at restaurants or bars and occasionally buying some treats like new fishing tackle or a Speare gun and things like that so day-to-day costs they can vary a lot we find that it's definitely cheaper than being on land and the principal reason for that is that you're no longer paying rent or you're no longer paying a mortgage in addition to that you've ditched all of the other ad hoc land bills like your council tax or utility bills for gas electricity and all those things you don't have a car now so you don't have to pay for insurance or fuel and things like that sure we've got to buy fuel for the boat but that's generally a lot less than what you would spend on fuel for a car having said that there are some usually pretty strong barriers to entry for the Boating world the fact that you need to have the money in the first place to buy the boat and once you bought the boat generally speaking you're going to have to spend more money on it to get it ready for full-time cruising and so that's probably the trickiest part of the budget balancing is is finding that initial part of cash to actually get into or make the step into this lifestyle once you're in it it is for us at least cheap people also live a lot cheaper than we do and obviously there's people who live in a lot more of an expensive way than we do a great way that we find of cutting down our cost is avoiding marinas so we really invested at the start in our anchor and chain setup so that we feel confident in a lot of strong wins and things at anchor if you were to sit in a marina it would trash your monthly budget and there's just no way we'd be able to afford it so we've been at anchor for well over 6 months this season and in that time we've spent I think it's 5 days in a marina total so how do we earn money whilst we live on a boat fulltime well we're very fortunate in that we still own the house that we used to live in we now rent this out and we receive an income off of that after paying for various fees and mortgage costs in addition to that we also both work part-time whilst on the boat so I work part-time as an accountant I've set up a small um business just me on my own doing the accounts and tax advice for other small businesses so I'm a a marketing consultant I specialize also in small businesses particularly within retail and helping them grow through digital marketing in addition to that we're on YouTube at saling NZ and we're able to do all of them this because we have a stable internet connection so we now have starlink on the boat which enables us to have super fast internet wherever we are in the world it doesn't matter how remote a Anchorage you're sitting at you will always have internet so staring costs €1 a month for us but the pricing is different depending on where you are in the world on board with us we have our 3-year-old vizer called Walter he has been an absolute dream when it came to U moving to being a boat dog the biggest challenge is getting them to pee or do their business on the boat so when we had Walter as a puppy we taught him to go to the L on Astro to for like fake grass and when we moved down to the boat we moved the artificial grass onto the bow of the boat and he immediately pretty much adapted he's absolutely fine when we're sailing but if it's particularly bad and he we want to be in the cockpit with us then we'll clip him into the cockpit he also has a live jacket that he wears or buyan a I guess it is but most of the time he'll stay down below so that then he's safe and not going to be rolling around too much he's quite a high energy dog so he needs a lot of exercise but actually he gets more exercise than he did when we were in Manchester and we were both working full-time he's also around us a lot more than he was back then and I think overall he's generally just a lot happier yeah the best part about this lifestyle is by far the People You Meet it's this cruising Community this community of other Sailors so when you come across another cruiser with a problem on their boat we're obviously always wanting to help and it's the same it's reciprocal so whenever we've had our problems the cruising Community the way it pulls together to sort of help you in your of lows is just amazing we've also met people that we would have never have met normally in our day-to-day lives from different countries different cultures over the last like year year and a half I've had lots of different scenarios playing out in my head but I think ultimately we will just keep checking in with each other and see if we're still enjoying it and as long as the answer to that is yes then we'll continue cruising um I do think at some point we'll definitely move back to land life but will'll definitely try and take as much that we've learned from this experience in particularly in terms of being self-sufficient and take that back to land life and try and apply it in kind of every way that we can yeah I think we'd quite like to set up an off-grid Homestead style environment for ourselves - 14 Years On A Self Sufficient Sailboat (How It’s Possible) ⛵️ Sailing Vessel Delos Ep. 441: Kind: captions Language: en (bright upbeat music) - For the last 14 years, we have lived on a 53 foot sailboat, and sailed around the whole globe. - And most of it has actually been in places like we are right now. This is Tahanea Atoll, it's uninhabited. There's no stores, there's no food, there's no people, there's no communications, there's nothing. And in today's video, we are going to talk about the systems and the technology behind the scenes that make it possible to stay out here off the grid, not only to survive, but also to do it comfortably. When we're out here away from the civilization, we rely entirely on ourselves, our boat, and the systems that we've set up. We need to be our own electrician, plumber, mechanic, and much more. And while we can't control what curve balls come our way, we can set ourselves up as best as possible to adapt to the challenges that this lifestyle presents. But for all the adversity we face out here, the payoff is like nothing else. - I love not knowing what's around the next corner. Beaches with no footprints, crystal clear water, just the boss nothingness. It's so, so special, and it's very rare to find anywhere else, unless you go with your own boat. - It takes a lot to make it happen, and it's a huge challenge, and I love it. (waves splashing gently) (gentle music) (keyboard typing) (waves continue splashing) When I think about taking a sailboat off grid for a long period of time, there's four things that pop into my mind that we've learned over the years are the most important. Number one, electricity, number two, food storage, number three, the ability to make your own fresh drinking water, at number four, communications and entertainment. So today we're gonna be talking about how we've set Delos up over the years to really improve for systems. You know, Delos is quite an old boat. She is a year 2000, so she's now 23 years old, and I have owned Delos for, what is it like 14 or 15 of those years? So it's been quite a long time. When I first got Delos, she didn't have very many of these systems. I mean, she had electricity, she had a desalinator, but over the years I've improved her. In fact, when you look at the original owner's manual for AMEL, it states that you should run the generator four hours a day. So like two hours in the morning, and then two hours in the afternoon for a- - [Kazza] Really? - total of four hours per day. Yeah. - That's a lot. - It is quite a bit. Over the years, my goal has been to wean us off that generator usage, and we're there. We're gonna dive into some of the details, how we've set up the systems, and how it all works, and what's possible. (gentle upbeat music) (lightbulb buzzing) It goes without saying, that power is one of the most critical pieces of the puzzle when it comes to thriving out here. We use it for necessities like our navigation systems and making fresh water, for comforts like our fridges, freezers, and fancy flushing toilets, (toilet flushing) for work, like powering our laptops on our satellite internet, and for luxuries like an ice maker, espresso machine, and our trusty smoke machine and strobe light. Before we dive into the nitty gritty of our setup, I want to quickly explain the basics behind how it actually works. The solar panels on Delos convert sunlight into electricity. The electricity generated first goes into a charge controller, whose job it is to make sure that the batteries get the right voltage and don't get overcharged, which could damage them. The electricity is stored in lithium batteries, which provide direct current electricity to power most of the boat's systems. (gentle piano music) Things like our lights, fridges, water pumps and navigation systems all run on DC electricity at either 12 or 24 volts. The power in your house, however, is alternating current at much higher voltage, like 110, or 220 volts depending on where you live. The job of the power inverter is to convert the DC supplied from the batteries into AC that can power all of our appliances, our induction stove, bread maker, electric hot water kettle, toaster, computers, and anything else we plug into the outlets runs on AC power. And lastly, before we go further, let's quickly talk about how we will be quantifying the amounts of power we generate and use. (gentle piano music ends) So I'm talking about things in terms of kilowatt hours because I think it's a much more useful term than amp hours, which are normally used in boats, and I thought it would be cool to relate it to some common household things, so you can know just how much energy a kilowatt hour is. So, this is our hot water boiler. You might have this in your house. This takes two kilowatts when it's running, 2000 watts. So if you were to run this for an hour, it would take two kilowatt hours. The water maker takes about 1,400 watts, so 1.4 kilowatts. So if you run that for an hour, that would be 1.4 kilowatt hours, and the toaster is about 800 watts. it would be eight tenths of a kilowatt hour, 0.8 kilowatt hours. Now you probably never run the hot water kettle or the toaster for an hour, but over time, if you're making a cup of tea, and then you're doing toast, and then you run the stove, and then you're running the water maker, and then you're flushing toilets, ice machine on, refrigerators on, like all of it day after day, it just accumulates all these small bits of electricity over time, or power consumed over time accumulate into all the energy uses for the boat. - As this week's video is about how we live in very remote places for long periods of time. I think it could be cool to talk about another aspect of this. So as you see in our videos, this sailing lifestyle comes with lots of very high highs and very low lows. Like a few weeks ago when we were hard ground, it was really stressful. I'm quite an anxious person, and the best thing that I have done for my mental health over the years, is to talk to someone. These are three SunPower Maxeon 410 watt panels, for a total of 1,230 watts back here. They're great panels, not a lot of shading. You know, we get some shading from the wind generator, and from the Starlink antenna on the other side, but these guys pump out the majority of our power. I went ahead and pulled the history from the charge controller for the last 29 days, which includes like days like today, cloudy days, sunny days, and everything in between. So it's not just the rated power, it's what you actually get in a real world environment, which I think is pretty cool. So over the last 29 days, we generated 132.3 kilowatt hours of energy. That means 4.6 kilowatt hours on average per day just from these panels. And then what I did, is I divided the wattage into the total amount of power produced, to get the watt hours per rated watt of solar. So just because it says 400 watts, doesn't mean that it's making 400 watts because of a ton of variables. But that number actually comes out to be 3.7 watt hours per watt of solar. So we're gonna compare that number to the other panels. So this is the second set of panels we're gonna talk about. These are the SunPower Flex Panels available from SunPoweredyachts.com. Also the big ones on the back I just showed you, also from Lyle and Katie @ sunpoweredyachts.com. They're awesome. They're very, very knowledgeable. So if you're looking for your panels, go ahead to the link. I think we'll just pop it below, our affiliate link, and they'll definitely source you out. They're sailors, and their cruisers, and super cool people. Okay, so these bigger panels are 170 watts each. We have two of those. And the smaller ones are a hundred watts each. So the total rated power there, is 540 watts between all four panels. The only problem is, you get a lot more shading because of the location. So you can notice how there's this shade across from the rigging, we've got the boom in here, we've got the mast. Right now, it's like high sun. The sun is directly above us, so the shading is at a minimum, but these get a lot more shade than the back, so we get less efficiency out of them. So it's a total of 540 watts in total. And over the last 29 days, we got 46.7 kilowatt hours, or 1.6 kilowatt hours on average per day. These are 2.9 watt hours per watt, so it's a little bit lower. I think the flexible panels by nature are a little bit less efficient, and because of the mounting location, they have a lot more shade affecting them. So that's why they're actually putting out less power per watt, but still getting 1.6 kilowatt hours per day. That's quite a bit of energy for otherwise space that we just wouldn't be using anything for it. Okay, so now let's look at the third setup here. Okay, so this is the third group of panels. We have two more of the Sun Power 100 watt panels on the four deck. And then we have two of these longer ones that we're trying out from a place called Bouge RV. They're 200 watt panels. And so together, the solar power on the four deck is 600 watts. We get, over the 29 day average, we get 31.1 kilowatt hours, or 1.07 kilowatt hours on average per day. And if we divide up that by the 600 watts capacity, we get 1.8 watt hours per rated watt of capacity. So these produce the least amount of power per rated watt of solar panel. I think there's a lot of shading up here, because the boom goes right over it. Like right now we have laundry hanging out. The dinghies up here, sometimes the main mast shades at all the rigging, but still it's adding just over a kilowatt hour per day. So if we add all those up, we get on average over 29 days, sunny, rainy and cloudy weather, and sailing and everything, we get 7.27 kilowatt hours per day in perfectly free renewable solar energy, which is actually enough to run our boat. We only run the generator now maybe about once a week for a couple hours to fill dive tanks. Or if the weather is terrible, and we don't get any solar for a few days, then we need to run the generator. That's our fallback plan. - [Kazza] That's pretty amazing. - It's pretty amazing. Shopping for food might not sound that tricky, but it's definitely an art that's tougher than it looks. Shopping in foreign countries can be full of surprises and adventure. The quantities needs to last for months at a time, and can be staggering, and often require multiple trips. Transportation between land and water means lots of loading and unloading multiple times. And then of course stashing it all away in the limited space on the boat is a huge job in itself. We try to get as much fresh fruit and vegetables as we can possibly fit, which we store in one of our two fridges, in the hanging nets, or in whatever extra room we can find. This was our hole before leaving Tahiti about three weeks earlier, and this is what it looks like now. - [Brian] We got the fresh stuff department. - [Kazza] The fresh stuff. - [Brian] We still have a few things. - [Kazza] We still have a few things. (Kazza laughs) - [Brian] That stuff's starting to get weird after three weeks. But these things do well. Onions, garlic keep well. The fruit and apples are keeping well. - Yeah. And it's a few things that I do to like kind of keep things fresh. Like I always go through it. If something goes bad, I remove it or cut it away. Like we have a stash of eggs. - Oh, show me the eggs. - Yeah. So if they're not refrigerated in the store, you don't have to refrigerate 'em. But we keep them like this, and every like week or so I turn them. It basically prevents the yolk from getting stuck on the shell, or on the outside of the egg, and that's when it starts going bad. So if you turn them, the yolk doesn't get stuck on the side as fast, and it lasts longer. One other thing too, is that I'm very strict with like keeping potatoes, and onions, and apples separately. The more separately you can keep stuff, the longer it lasts, basically. - [Brian] I think the gas from some things as they ripen can make other- - things ripen quicker. - I bet other people know way more about this, and maybe can educate us. (Caribbean music) - So for me, I personally love fresh water. - [Brian] You love fresh water? - I love fresh water. (laughs) - What do you use it for? - I mean drinking obviously. - Obviously. - Like we could not be out here without drinking water. I mean the water maker's super critical. - You can catch rain. We have in the past when the water maker's broken. - But, it's not dependable enough. And also, you know, being able to wash clothes. - Uses quite a bit of fresh water, but it's a lifesaver. - Yeah, that's really amazing. - The toilets. They flush with salt water. - So we don't use, we don't flush fresh water down the toilet. - So yes, drinking, bathing, washing. (bubbles popping) We use a machine called a water maker, or desalinate that turns salt water into fresh drinking water, using a process called reverse osmosis. But first, let's look at plain old osmosis. It's a naturally occurring process in which a liquid spontaneously passes through a membrane. The membrane allows some molecules like water to pass through, but other molecules, like the majority of salts, are unable to pass through. The flow of liquid through such a membrane occurs naturally to try and even out the salt concentrations between the two sides. In reverse osmosis, we're applying pressure to the salt water side, to force it through the membrane in the reverse direction, letting the pure water pass through, and stripping the salt and other impurities on the other side. And voila, just like that, we've created fresh, drinkable salt-free water. So there's a pump in the engine room back there, and it basically puts high pressure water through a couple of tubes, it strips off the salt, and then the fresh water goes into our water tank, which is in the keel. I can actually go and turn it on. I'll show you how it works. So the pressure comes up, we turn on the high pressure pump, we wait about 30 seconds for the system to stabilize. You'll hear it turn on pretty soon. We turn up the pressure to 800 PSI, and boom. Now we're making fresh drinking water right from the sea. Pretty cool. And it's all being done off of the batteries and the solar power right now. (brassy upbeat music) Last crucial part is what I would categorize as like communications and entertainment. - And it's changed over the years. Like when so much, when we first left, we had the SSB radio. That was game changer first us. - [Kazza] And it was before Starlink came out. - Before Starlink. - So it was like, yeah. - But now we are in the Pacific, and we do have Starlink. - The service runs us about 250 bucks a month. - And for our business, it's- - It's absolutely crucial. - Yeah. Otherwise, like we couldn't- - I mean we are in the middle of nowhere, and in order to do that and still work, and still maintain our business, we need connectivity. - 🚫 Top 5 Reasons NOT to Live on a Sailboat (Harsh Realities of Boat Life): Kind: captions Language: en We here at Practical Sailor love the idea of living on a sailboat. In fact, all of us here have at one or more points in our lives lived on a sailboat or do right now. But it wouldn't be very practical of us to gloss over the downsides, the hardest parts, the outright awful moments that you'll have living on a boat that'll make you want to scream and move back in with your mom and dad. So, I pulled everyone and today the top five things you need to know before and what to expect when you do live on a sailboat. This is the bad stuff. [Music] Number one, the very obvious, but maybe you didn't think of it. Everything is a compromise on a boat as compared to landlife. For starters, most boats don't have a clothing washer and dryer. Both appliances would use way too much electricity or water to make sense on a boat, so you'll likely be laundry matting it. And speaking of the lack of electricity and water, your internet isn't as simple as your house internet. The phone or cable guy can't help you with a high-speed reliable connection on your boat. Now, Starlink has made this easier, but it's not perfect and not as good as the fiber line you might be used to with the one gig up, one gig down at home. Also, you'll have enough trouble parking the boat somewhere reliably. So, don't expect to have a car. Most sailboat liverboards don't even own a car because there's simply nowhere to keep it. So, get used to your bicycle or walking. And the compromises don't end there. It's much harder to have a family pet on the boat. Some people do it, but it's a lot harder. Kids, if you have kids, they might not like the boat at all. It's a lot harder to have people over for dinner. Older family members that are less mobile may not be able to visit you at all because it's hard to get on and off the boat from the dock. You may not have a freezer, so grocery trips will be a lot more frequent and without a car, a lot more difficult. And speaking of compromises, reason number two not to live on a sailboat, showers and sleeping. In my college days, I could sleep anywhere at any time and shower with a garden hose. But as I get older, I find a good night's sleep to be a key part of my happiness. Sailboats notoriously have oddshaped beds, and because of the nature of a boat, they tend to move around a lot. I recently went camping on land camping with some friends and watched for an hour as they set up their camping trailer, raising and lowering jacks and placing a level on each end of the trailer, adjusting the jacks, and then leveling the sides for and aft. Do campers have a four and a leveling and adjusting and I thought to myself, "Wow, I'm so used to sleeping on a sailboat that had never occurred to me that it should be level. I don't think my boat's ever been level. My bed on that boat has never really stopped moving, let alone been level and damp. A lot of the time, a lack of electricity means you won't be running the AC. So, bed will be hotter than you want it to be with humidity and sometimes damp feeling. And there's only so much you can do about that. If you need to sleep in a cool and dry room, your live aboard life might just be spent at a marina so that you have all the power you need to run the AC. And then the showers. Boat water tanks are only so big and water heaters are pretty small on boats and the power to heat the water is in such demand that your shower routine will be the shortest it's ever been. A couple minutes in and out is fairly normal on a boat. Reason number three, everything breaks. I don't care if your boat's brand new or you just did a $100,000 refit. Everything on a boat breaks. From the mundane oil changes and impellers to the stitching in the sails, the VHF fuse that keeps blowing. The BGE pump hose came loose again. The toilet is back feeding seawater into the bowl. The igniter for the stove won't work and you have to light it with a lighter. And the electric winch hasn't been electric in months. Salt water and salty air makes things a lot worse. Rigging is rusting. The bright work needs to be oiled or treated constantly. The birds spent the night on the lifelines again, and you have to scrub the deck as soon as you wake up before the sun bakes everything into the glass work. And the dinghy has 6 in of water in it from the rain. An old salt once told me if 80% of the boat is working, it was time to go have an adventure. 80% because 100% was impossible. Can you imagine an 80% functional house if that was good enough? Everything on a boat breaks all the time. Reason number four, along with everything breaks, everything's expensive. Most of us get into boat life to try to save a little bit of money, but boats are definitely not cheap. The engine 20 grand. Rigging 10 to 15 grand. If you get a cat, it's 20. Sails are about 10 grand. Line, that's what we call any rope on a sailboat once it has a purpose, a line. Most line has to be poly no or low stretch, and that can be several dollars a foot. There's a boat supply store near where I live, and they just did new landscaping and a water feature in their front yard. That should tell you everything you need to know about the marine parts industry. Everyone overcharges on everything, and the one taking the brunt of that joke is the sailor. It's sad, but it forces us to be resilient and inventive and resourceful. We often make our own parts or adapt other things for our boat's use. And reason number five, the one you likely didn't even think of and the thing I hated most about living on a boat. The weather is not your friend. Sometimes it's wonderful, but the weather dictates everything in your life. If the wind will be blowing from the west tonight really strong, you'll need to find somewhere to hide from it before you go to bed. If tomorrow the wind switches and comes from the east really, really strong, you'll need to find somewhere to hide from that. If there's a storm or a tide running the opposite direction that you want to go, it's going to be blowing 40 knots for a few days, so hunker down in somewhere and hide. Maybe you have somewhere you have to be in a week, but you can't go because the waves are 10 ft high. The weather will truly dictate every aspect of your life on a boat. The weather comes first ahead of your own agenda. any expectations that anyone else has of you to be somewhere on a given day or a given time, your plans, your destination goals, even what the customs of a new country wants you to do, the weather will always come first. And if you defy it, you could likely lose your home. The weather is the mighty equalizer in the boating world. And as much as you wish you could control it, if it would just stop blowing 35 knots through the rigging so you can get even 2 hours of sleep so you can work tomorrow, but it won't. You have absolutely no choice on the weather. It controls you. Now, all of this is not to say you shouldn't live on a boat. It can be the most rewarding lifestyle that is out there. You get the same million-doll view anyone else does for a fraction of the price. You aren't tied to one location and free to go wherever you want. Most of us head south. And when things break or you're looking for cheaper ways to fix things, people like us here at Practical Sailor love to help you out. We've got decades of articles specifically for you. Like this one helping you with navigation lights for your dinghy or this one helping you find a fuelefficient RPM on your engine to save you some money. I'll link both of those in the description. We've got decades of these things and now we have this YouTube channel aimed at doing the same thing, making your life on board easier. So, please hit the subscribe button and give this video a thumbs up if you like sailboats and leave a comment if you're a live aboard and tell me what was your number one complaint about living on a boat. I can't wait to hear from you guys in the comments.

  14. @PumpernickelBread25 [1 likes]: I thought sailors slept in hammocks and took baths in the sea.
  15. @davidsowens [1 likes]: 6years full-time liveaboard and counting. I will never live on the land ever again.  My wife doesn't quite fully share the enthusiasm of yacht life as I do but she still enjoys it. So she housesits over the humid summer months for North Queensland, Australia. While I live aboard. It seems to work for us. She broke her leg recently and I had to leave my sanity for 3 months and go live on the land albeit in some gorgeous homes and help my wife. Despite the niceties and big cinema TV's, one for of us. It meant nothing to me. I longed to be back on the yacht again. The sea breezes running the through the yacht the gentle rocking of the waves. Despite our 48' ketch big smaller than a house it was driving the streets seeing houses stacked almost on top of each other where you can hear your neighbours breathing. It was the quiet and space I missed most the most. Yacht life has its Pros and Cons for sure. To be a fulltime live aboard space is a premium. Renting a yacht before buying a yacht will give you some idea of how much space you think you will need. For 2 people in my humble opinion 45'-50' is a great size. There're times you will need space from each other. Never buy a yacht with out a survey report. Growth below the water line prevents you knowing what is taking place. Getting an above water survey, as is where is, first, allowed us the get comprehensive insurance on the promise we would get a hull inspection at a later time. Fortunately the seller had a survey report. The above and below survey and lift and hold can be possibly negotiated with the seller by being prepared to split the cost. Better to spend a little than find out you have more serious problems later. Rigging is important. I do all my own rigging. By replacing 2 cables at a time. I buy the cable eyelets screws get eyelet end for top of mast swaged then measure against the old cable allowing for stretching. A tensioning tool is required to get the correct tension on the cables. A rigger can give you correct tension advice based on the number of cable core strands. On a yacht 12v electrical core should only ever be tinned silver copper never brown copper. Someone asked for help with their faulty Bow thruster I replaced a 70mm cable on a popular proprietary built yacht. The manufacture had cheapened out and used standard copper line that corroded out the line. I've seen copper cabling used on windlass cabling when users have dropped their anchor only to find the winch has stopped. Again the line has corroded out. This can be a horrible nightmare to replace on production yachts as the cabling is not accessible being hidden behind fibreglass panelling and its been bunched up cable tied to other surrounding cabling during the yacht build. Inspect ports then have to be cut out to get access to the cabling.  A working windlass and yacht engine are non negotiables. Due to sea moisture all 12volt wiring requires heat shrink with glue to seal at both ends of the cable. Mercury makes a sealing spray wax coating you apply on the windlass electrical connections anywhere you have electrical connections exposed to salt air this will protect the connections against corrosion. I brought a Sailrite sewing machine to make my own breathable rope bags and other canvas work. Particularly with repairing sails. Being self taught I do my own electrical 12v and 240v work. In addition to all engine repairs and plumbing. It will save you many thousands if you can do the work yourself. Particularly in this age. At the risk of stereotyping, the middle age trade mindset is not about repeat business. Its about screwing the customer for all your can. I have found rule pumps to be the most reliable for me. Our fibreglass tender has side console steering with a battery electrical start for the engine under our seat. I run a Rule LoPro 900 GPH Bilge Pump off our battery, It supports timer and sensor function for pumping out rainwater. The timer works best running every 15mins. When it detects water it runs until bilge is empty. We have since installed a collapsible Bimini cover over our tender so it keeps most of the water out now. We are fully off grid I run 1.3Kw of solar using Victron products. Their smart solar regulators and a Phoenix 3000watt inverter /120amp charger. We have an 800amp lithium bank which has been brilliant punching way above it weight. We run our 2.5amp Dakin air-condition off this. The wife is happy running our medium size microware, air fryer and a number of other kitchen utilities of this. The trick to owning a yacht is being organised by planning ahead documenting everything you do. You can refer to later when needed. In addition being vigilant by maintaining situational awareness at all times of your surroundings whether anchored or on passage.  with an ever changing sea state. Finally I'm an avid evangelist of OpenCPN. A free downloadable software application running on mac or PC that can utilise the plotter Wi-Fi network using the plotters NMEA 0183 network sentence strings to import into OpenCPN masthead winds depth GPS, etc. Most yachties carry global maps for openCPN might cost you a carton of beer at worst. The other is Predict Wind and the Datahub. The data hub uses your NMEA 2000 network to upload wind speed direction boat speed and more to your phone or tablet as an app. Very useful if your own the land.  There is a lot more one could share but for another time.

- The Harsh Truth About Living on a Boat (That No One Tells You): Kind: captions Language: en in today's video I'm going to explain why I believe it's a bad idea to attempt living on a steel boat that's 40 ft or smaller I'll do this by sharing some of my experiences from the past 4 years and explaining the reasons why I seemingly abandoned my first boat if you've been following this channel from the beginning you'll know that it was my dream to live fulltime on a boat to start living that dream I bought this 40t steel boat little did I know at the time that it would turn into a nightmare living situation there are two main reasons why I believe that a boat that's approximately 40 ft or smaller is not well suited for full-time living the first reason is obviously the space or rather the lack thereof now don't get me wrong a boat trip is fun even on smaller boats but only for a limited period of time once you start living on the boat full-time with all your belongings even just the Bare Essentials it gets crammed very quickly and so I found myself on a never-ending boat trip and slowly but surely the novelty wore off I think this issue is largely related to the shape and layout of my boat in particular since it's an older boat it's relatively narrow with a living area not exceeding 2.5 M for the most part but what makes it even worse is that the kitchen living room and bedroom are all on separate floors meaning I have to go up and down those tiny stairs constantly frequently hitting my head or my knees in the process and once again I'm specifically referring to the shape and layout of my boat so a similarly sized boat with a better layout could be more suitable for example if all the rooms were on the same level the space issue wasn't even the biggest problem I told myself that this was the life I chose and that I would eventually get used to it but then winter came around and that's the second reason why I had to abandon my first boat suddenly it wasn't just about being crammed or constantly hitting my head or knees I was also cold and oh how cold it could get the icy winter air sipped in through the gaps in the doors and the cold got in through the weak incomplete insulation but rest assured I didn't throw the towel at the first drop in temperature for the record I spent three entire burlin Winters on that boat and it's in those long dark winter nights that things get really difficult as soon as the temperature started to drop I quickly realized that most boats aren't designed for long-term living in harsh weather especially one with single glazed windows and the only partially insulated steel hull it also didn't help that on a boat at anchor I was completely on my own there was no warmth from the ground on neighboring apartments and my only source of heat was a small diesel heater the kind designed to warm up a car in Winter before a trip it became clear that this to was poorly suited for the frosty temperatures that often take hold here in Europe during winter it simply wasn't powerful enough to heat up an entire boat when General winter decided to show off everything he had in store for us I would have loved to install a better heating system ideally a wood stove but again the size and layout of my boat just didn't allow for it there was simply no way way to install a wood stove in any reasonable location mainly due to the fact again that the three rooms are on different levels but also because of the size of the rooms a wood stove needs at least 40 to 50 cm of clearance around the front and where do you find that in such small spaces as we have on this boat and so I was stuck with that damn diesel heater which on top of everything guzzled copious amounts of fuel not only did it break my back from constantly holding Jerry cans of diesel but it also put a serious strain on my wallet and because I was burning close to 100 L of diesel each week the interior of the boat ended up smelling like a trucking rest stop but despite all this I still tried to hold out I told myself that winter would eventually end and things would get better in reality however I was forced to wear my big winter jacket indoors my feet were always cold no matter how many layers of socks I wore and my fingers were so stiff that typing on my laptop became a struggle if I were alone it might have been okay I'm a tough guy and what doesn't kill you makes you stronger right but I wasn't alone my wife and our pet dog were shivering right next to me and so we were stuck in a giant freezer barely able to live a normal life I tried to play it cool to hide my own despair but sooner or later I had to Face Reality this sucked this was not what I had imagined something needed to change and so change I did I got a different boat alt together one that first of all was big enough to accommodate two people comfortably and secondly its shape and size gave it a much better chance of staying warm in the winter with the living area above the water line however the biggest Improvement on our current boat is the heating system we now have a wood stove specifically a wood pallet stove which is not only far more powerful than that little diesel heater but also cost the fraction to keep it running and so the story of my first boat as a whole had a happy ending for us I managed to live my dream even if that first boat wasn't even part of it anymore and of course I didn't actually abandon it while I was building the cabin on my barge boat I kept working on my first boat when ever I could I built the bathroom the galery and even started paneling the rear cabin all of it at the expense of important work that needed to be done on the barge boat this just goes to show that my determination to finish the boat is still there and I hope we can finally complete it by this coming summer and so there you have it I know this is probably not what many of you were expecting and I'll admit I'm guilty of procrastination and mismanaging my time and money but looking at the bigger picture getting this type of boat in the first place was a mistake if the goal was to live on it full time had I known that back then I probably would have started building a proper house boat from the beginning but at the time I simply didn't know any better so it is what it is I still hope you enjoyed this little update on my first boat or rather an explanation of why it's not featured as prominently on my channel anymore and I know that many of you have been upset with some of my choices but I hope today's video helps put your minds at ease

- How to Network: Networking Etiquette Tips for Professionals: Kind: captions Language: en business networking love it or hate it it is critical for your Career Success you need to know how to do it and you need to know how to do it well in this video you're going to learn how to improve your networking skill at networking events so you can come across as somebody who is poised confident and warm these tips will help you build your professional reputation and make valuable Connections in business it's up to you what you do with those connections afterwards if you like the sound of what you're going to learn then hit the like button subscribe to my channel for more content like this and let's get straight into it one of the most basic tips that most people Overlook when it comes to networking and going to networking events is to find out who is going to be there the purpose of this is to go into the networking event prepared and with a strategy so you know exactly who to talk to at networking events you have limited time you have limited time to meet people and to have conversations you don't want to waste your time talking to somebody who can have no impact whatsoever ever on your career so you need to be a lot more strategic about who you talk to at networking events now I know what you're thinking you're probably saying to yourself well that's really shallow Cara you never know who you're going to meet at a networking event you should keep all opportunities open that is true to some extent but from my experience when you go to a networking event you go there with a purpose you go there to meet people who can connect you to a new job if you're a job hunting you go there to meet new clients if you aren't running your own business or if you're in an organization and you need to look for clients you go to those networking events with a purpose in mind if you fill your time the whole time you spend at that networking event talking to this person talking to that person it is very likely you will never achieve the purpose you had of going to that networking event you will walk away from that event not having achieved anything not having met anyone of significance of importance who can help you in your career here I know this through my experience going to many networking events as a business person that you really need to have strategy you need to be prepared you need to find out who is going to be there it doesn't mean you need to spend all of your time talking to people on your list who is going to be there leave some time to open yourself up to New Opportunities if you want but please do go with some type of preparation to at least meet two maybe three people who can help you in your career or help you achieve the goals that you want to achieve now how do you find out who is going to be at the event well a lot of the time the events are posted on Eventbrite on Facebook or even on LinkedIn they are posted on this on those platforms and you can actually see on the event page who is interested in going or who is actually going to be attending those events so take a look at the pages where those events are posted online if that fails you can always ask the event organizer they may or may not tell you who will be attending the event but there's no harm in asking right arriving early gives you a fantastic advantage to see who is entering the room and to start conversations with the people you want to talk to so stand near the door or near the bar both of these areas are great Vantage points to see who is entering in the room and to be able to approach them as soon as they enter another advantage of arriving early is that you are already there when people enter the room now when people enter the room you have to understand that they usually go through a period of feeling a bit nervous a bit stressed a bit uncomfortable because they don't know who to talk to they don't know what the first thing is that they should do when they enter the room you have the advantage of being able to approach them to start a conversation to introduce yourself to help them feel more comfortable and more relaxed at the event which of course will help you give a good first impression and help that person to remember you more if you are at a networking event looking for someone to talk to looking to enter an already established conversation you need to know the right way to do it sometimes groups will be very closed off people will be standing very close together it will be very difficult for you to enter that conversation and start to talk to someone other times groups will be very open and there'll be lots of opportunity for you to enter the group and to enter the conversation as well how can you tell the difference how do you know which is an easy group to approach or a difficult group to approach well you need to look at the body language of the people in the group if they are standing close together if there is not much space at all in between the people in the group then that means they are very closed to anyone else entering the group it's going to be very difficult for you to enter the group and to start talking to anyone in that conversation I recommend you avoid those types of groups on the other hand if there is a group where people have gaps in between them or if they're using very open body language maybe they are not directly facing the other person they're slightly angled away from the other person this is an indication that they are open they're opening up the group for other people to approach them for other people to enter the conversation and start talking when you see groups like this at networking events whether it's one person whether it's three people or more than three people this is your opportunity to approach them and to enter the conversation and meet new people you can learn a lot just by reading someone else's body language if you liked what you have learned so far in this video then please hit the like button subscribe to my channel for more videos like this every week and let's continue on using a business card case is one of my favorite tips to share with emerging leaders when it comes to networking events a business card case will absolutely make make you look polished and professional like no one else does if you're wondering what a business card case is it is a small pouch where you store your own business cards and also the business cards of other people this ensures that both your business cards and the other person's business cards remain neat organized and secure a business card case is something I use religiously for every networking event or a business function that I go to I have been using a business card case ever since I was working in Japan it is a tip that I picked up over there and I absolutely love how it can really make you look a lot more polished a lot more confident and a lot more organized as well which definitely helps you give a positive first impression when you meet people for the first time there are so many different types of business card cases out there it's really important you find one that matches both your personality and also your leadership brand I definitely recommend that you get a business card case but if you want want to start with some options that could be really helpful for you I have linked to some options in the description below I do need to let you know those links are affiliate links so if you do purchase from them I will earn a small commission at no cost to you the next tip for networking events is to avoid eating now I know at networking events there are some amazing food there and it is really tempting to wanna eat all the food that is served to you or presented to you or offered to you at the networking event but you need to go back to the purpose of why you are at the event you are there to meet people you are there to have conversations how can you have a conversation if you have a mouthful of food you won't look polite if you're talking with your mouth full you won't feel comfortable and you won't feel confident either I recommend you eat before the event that way you won't be tempted by all the delicious food that's offered to you and you can focus on your main purpose of going to the event which is to meet people when you start talking to people at a networking event I recommend you start the conversation by asking non-threatening questions what do I mean by non-threatening questions these are questions that are really easy to answer they're not too prying they're not too interrogative in nature you see when you meet someone at a networking event they will be nervous just like you they're likely to be stressed when you start a conversation with them they'll probably be very wary about who you are or what you want what your intentions are they might be worried that you're going to try to sell them something immediately or try to get them to say yes to some kind of request you need to help them relax you need to bring down their defense mechanism you need to get them comfortable talking to you and you do this by asking non-threatening questions have you been to this event before how did you find out about this event and the dreaded question what do you do you I know a lot of you are probably thinking that is so boring it is so typical to be asked that question at a networking event but it is extremely effective in getting the other person to talk to you and getting the other person to feel relaxed around you why because they expect you to ask them this kind of question if you start to ask some questions out of left field questions they don't expect they will start to become cautious they'll put up their defense mechanism they won't be able to relax with you and they probably won't want to continue the conversation with you either how can you meet people who can help you in your career if you're not able to talk to them if you're not able to have an in-depth conversation with them so you need to find ways to bring down their natural defense mechanism get them relaxed to get them to talk to you and you do this by asking non-threatening questions and the handshake you give conveys a lot about who you are as a professional I've had bad handshakes I'm sure you have had bad handshakes at some stage in your career as well they definitely make you notice there are definitely memorable but they don't help you to remember that person in a positive way you don't want people to have this reaction of you you don't want people to think this of you any less of you because you gave a bad handshake I want you to be able to connect with people I want you to be able to get people to like you at networking events this is how you can build a relationship with that person networking events are the first point of contact with someone if they don't like you at their first point of contact then you have no hope of developing a relationship with them so it's really important that you give a positive impression and you give a good handshake or the right handshake to convey the right impression there are many different types of handshakes there is the dead fish handshake where the other person has a slightly clammy hand and it feels a little moist there is the queen handshake where the other person offers you just their fingers instead of their entire hand to shake then there is the limp handshake where they offer you their entire hand but there is no strength to their shake you want to use a strong confident handshake so that is exactly the impression people form about you and here's how you do it you need to make sure you have web to web contact when you shake the other person's hand this is the first step you need to wrap your hand around the other person's hand firmly then you need to shake up and down two to three times then you can let go a handshake is generally the only physical contact you're going to have with someone at a networking event so you really want to make it count we haven't talked at all about body language but body language is extremely important for networking events it's extremely important to help you look more confident and look more poised and professional when you meet people I do have a two-part series on my Channel about body language which I will link to in the description below or you can click on this video right here if you want to watch the first part of this two-part series before you go if you - How To Get A Yacht Job In The Caribbean (Fast!) | Maritime Career: Kind: captions Language: en So, you would like to work on a yacht in the Caribbean, turquoise waters, sun for a good portion of the year, and a lifestyle that feels more like a dream than a job at times. But where do you start? What qualifications do you need? And most importantly, how do you actually get hired? Hello sailors, Marcus here and welcome back to the CCV Rumés channel. In this video, I am going to break down exactly how to land your first yacht job in the Caribbean from the documents you will need to the ports you should visit and how to stand out from hundreds of other green crew. And now without further ado, let's sail into it. Why the Caribbean? Each year, a large number of superyachts cross the Atlantic from the Mediterranean to the Caribbean for the Northern Hemisphere winter season. Between November and May, places like Antigga, St. Martin, and St. Thomas become hubs for some of the most luxurious yachts in the world. This means more jobs, shorter contracts, and plenty of opportunities for new crew members to make a start on their maritime careers. If you missed the Mediterranean summer season, the Caribbean is the perfect second chance to get your foot in the door. Documents you need before you take the next flight. Let's talk about the must-h have documents for working on yachts. STCW basic safety training. This is your entry ticket to the industry. It covers firefighting, sea survival, first aid, security awareness, and personal safety. Without it, no yacht can legally hire you. PNG1 medical certificate issued by an MCA, Maritime Coast Guard Agency approved doctor. It confirms you are medically fit to work at sea. Seaman's discharge book beneficial for professionally logging sea time. Valid passport and visas. Many Caribbean islands are British overseas territories or US jurisdictions. So check your passport requirements. Example visas required are the US B1B2 for cruising in US waters and European Shenhen visa as some Caribbean countries are also EU European Union dependencies. If you do not have any of the documents ready yet, complete them before heading out. Most crew agencies or captains will not even consider your application without them. If you have other maritime certificates or qualifications, ensure to provide these as well. Choosing your island base. There are three main Caribbean hotspots for yacht crew to base themselves in. Antigga, English Harbor, known for the Antigga Charter Yacht Show and one of the best places for dock walking. St. Martin, Simpson Bay, and Isle Doul Marina, the Caribbean's yacht capital. It is where many yachts base between charters. St. Thomas, US Virgin Islands. More American flagged vessels. Perfect if you already have a US visa, green card, or passport. Other jurisdictions such as St. Lucia, Dominica, and British Virgin Islands are also great places to base yourself for job searching. Always be flexible and open to job openings for each base, even if you're on another island. Are you finding this video helpful so far? If so, then be sure to leave a like and sail into that subscribe button so I can create more weekly content like this for you. Thank you so much for the support. Finding accommodation. Wherever you arrive, you'll need to stay somewhere crew friendly. Also, it needs to be somewhere close to marinas and crew hangouts. Look for crew houses. For example, a place like the crew house in Antigga. They are not fancy, but they provide the basic necessities. They are also full of people with the same ambitions, all networking, sharing job leads and prepping resumes. Expect to pay around 40 to $60 US each night or a weekly rate if you stay longer. Your resume and first impressions. Now, let's talk about your most powerful asset and first impression for any captain or recruiter, your yacht resume. Unlike a normal resume, your yacht resume needs to include a professional headsh shot, smiling, uniform style polo with a marina or yacht background, nationality, age and location, STCW, ENG1 and other certificate details, any hospitality or mechanical skills, hobby or water sports experience. Remember, your resume should look clean, presentable, maritime focused, and ready to hand out physically and digitally. Dock walking and networking. Here is where you start to make valuable connections through dock walking. This means walking along the marinas in the early morning, handing out your resume to yacht crew, and introducing yourself to captains. Please note, conduct research on which marinas allow for dock walking, as well as other regulations for being on a marina site. Best times between 6:30 a.m. and 9:30 a.m. before yachts get busy. Dress code. A clean polo shirt, shorts, and closed shoes. Look professional. Do not be discouraged. You are likely to face rejection at times, and that is normal for the beginning. It can take days or even weeks before you land a day work or junior deckhand job. Keep going and show up with your head held high. Once you do land that first part-time job, it can lead to your first full-time job. Next up, networking is everything. Go to crew bars and engage with people in the crew houses. Be professional, friendly, ask questions, and let people know you are looking for work. You can also start networking before you relocate so you have a head start and people you already know to ask for advice, crew agencies and online jobs. While you are dock walking, you can also register online to different job platforms. Examples include Yacht Spot, Blue Water Crew, Crew Seekers, and Facebook groups for yacht jobs in the Caribbean area. Make sure your online profile matches your resume. And take as much time as you need to make sure your profile is professional and matches all requirements. When applying, ensure to check your emails daily as yacht jobs come and go fast and responses may need to be answered quickly. Conclusion and final notes. Each individual will have a different journey into getting a yacht job in the Caribbean. It all comes down to experience, passport nationality, prior connections, and timing of the job application. Some will get hired quicker than others, but that does not mean the ones who didn't get hired are not good enough. Positions can be very competitive and someone else may have had something that gave them the edge. Bottom line, ensure to do your research on the base you are looking to go to, budget for staying, and how many resume copies you plan to give out. You can start networking early and job applying before you head out. Once in the Caribbean base, keep showing up with a positive attitude and get talking with other professionals who have experience, knowledge, or even know someone who is hiring. New crew members can stand out by doing the following. Conducting their own research. Early networking and networking with professionals. Showing up prepared and documents ready. Not distracted by rejections. Positive mindset 24/7. Well written and designed resume. Showing up each day. dressing smart and to the dress code. Not being afraid to accept voluntary work. It counts.
