- Podcast Episode: Is Starlink Any Good for Yacht Connectivity?: Kind: captions Language: en Welcome back to the deep dive. Okay, we've gathered up all those sources you sent over and today we're setting sail into well the future of staying connected at sea. Our mission is a deep dive into Starlink maritime, specifically how it's changing things for highv value yaching because let's face it, for years getting online offshore meant uh insane bills and speeds that felt ancient. >> Yeah. Like dialup days sometimes. >> Exactly. And for a modern yacht needing good nav data, safety comms, plus, you know, guests needing to work or stream, >> those old limits just didn't cut it anymore. >> And that's precisely the gap Starlink was built to fill. It's SpaceX's low Earth orbit satellite system, LEO, we call it. The maritime version basically extends that fast connection way offshore, fundamentally changing how viable it is to be online once you're past cell tower range. >> Okay, let's unpack that. We already have satellite internet at sea, right? Those big white domes you see everywhere. So what's the um the fundamental tech difference here? Why is Starling such a big deal for yachts? Now >> it really boils down to altitude physics. Basically the older systems, what we generally call via use geostationary or go satellites. These guys are parked way way out there like 22,000 m up. >> 22,000 miles. Wow. >> Yeah. So your data has a massive round trip just to get there and back. That takes time. Starlink on the other hand uses this huge constellation of Lao satellites, thousands of them orbiting just a few hundred miles up. >> Much closer. >> Exactly. And that proximity is the whole technological edge. >> So closer satellite. >> What does that actually mean for someone say in the pilot house trying to remote into their office server. >> It means a massive improvement in what we call latency. That's the lag, right? The delay for your signals round trip. With the old GEO VSet, that huge distance means latency is often 600 milliseconds, maybe even more. >> Half a second or more delay. Ouch. >> Yeah, try having a natural conversation on a video call with that kind of delay. It's painful. Starlink, because it's so much closer, cuts that right down. We're usually talking well under 100 milliseconds, sometimes much lower depending. >> Okay, that's a genuine night and day difference. And that explains why things like Zoom calls, using remote desktops, accessing cloud apps, things vital for work from boat suddenly become actually usable at sea. That just wasn't really the case before. >> Absolutely. And it's not just low latency. The speeds are genuinely useful, too. Looking at the sources, yaw operators are seeing downloads often in the 40 to 150 megabits per second range, sometimes higher. >> And uploads because that's key for calls and sending files. >> Uploads are typically decent, too. to say 8 to 25 melodies plus. That's enough bandwidth for several people browsing, streaming HD, or even 4K all at once. >> Let's quickly touch on the hardware because the Starlink antenna looks totally different from the old dome, >> right? No big spinning dish. It's a flat rectangular thing called a phased array antenna. And that's crucial because those LEO satellites are zipping across the sky really fast. The phased array steers its beam electronically, no moving parts inside to track those satellites instantly and then hand off the connection seamlessly as they go overhead. >> So that complex tracking is built in maintaining the link. >> Exactly. It's key to keeping that connection stable and the latency low. >> It's quite fascinating when you stack up speed, latency, and the cost per megabyte. Even with the higher maritime plan pricing, Starlink seems to come out ahead of the traditional options. Yeah, generally speaking, you're getting a lot more performance for your data dollar, >> but uh nothing's perfect, especially when you're operating in the middle of the ocean. The sources were pretty clear. You can't expect flawless 150 megs, 247 everywhere. Let's talk about those trade-offs. What makes the service dip? >> Okay, the main challenges kind of fall into three buckets. First off, weather. Really heavy localized rain, what we call rain fade or thick squalls, can definitely degrade the signal, sometimes interrupt it. It just has trouble punching through all that water. >> Okay. Weather makes sense. What's the second one? >> Physical obstructions. This is a big one. On a boat with masts, radars, antennas everywhere. The Starlink antenna needs a really wide, clear view of the sky. The sources suggest 140° or more ideally >> to see as many satellites as possible. >> Right. If the yacht's own mast or maybe a cliff face in a fjord or even a tall building in port blocks that view, you're going to get dropouts. >> And the third challenge, >> congestion. Simple as that. Starlink's capacity isn't infinite. In really busy maritime areas, think popular harbors, cruise ship routes speeds can noticeably slow down because lots of users are sharing the same satellite beams, >> right? More demand on the network. >> Now, it's worth mentioning the maritime plan costs more than the residential one. partly because it gives you priority traffic over fixed users that helps mitigate congestion somewhat but doesn't eliminate it in really crowded spots. >> Yeah, makes sense. Okay, so given those potential issues, why do some high-end commercial operators according to what we read still keep their old VSAT systems? >> Ah, that brings us to the concept of the SLA, the service level agreement. While Starlink generally offers much higher potential speeds, the legacy GEO VSA providers have mature global SLAs's >> meaning >> meaning a contract that guarantees a certain minimum level of bandwidth or a certain percentage of uptime usually with financial penalties if they don't meet it. >> Ah so it's about guaranteed minimum. >> Exactly. For some operations where 100% guaranteed mission critical uptime is paramount, that contractual guarantee, even if the speed is slower, might still be preferred over Starlink's higher, but potentially more variable speeds. >> Okay, so Starlink is faster, generally better, but not absolutely bulletproof 100% of the time. Which leads to the really practical question for yacht captains and managers. If you know Starlink can have these brief dips, rain fade, maybe a satellite handoff lag, congestion, how do the best operators guarantee that office at C level of reliability their owners or charter guests demand? >> And this right here is the core takeaway from all the source material we looked at. The answer overwhelmingly is the hybrid setup. >> Hybrid meaning not relying on just one connection. >> Precisely. Relying solely on Starlink or any single connection at sea isn't really viable anymore for truly critical needs. You absolutely have to build in redundancy. >> So what does that hybrid approach look like on the boat? >> It typically means pairing Starlink Maritime with good quality high-speed LTE or 5G cellular for when you're near shore or even in port. But the real magic happens in the network management system. That sophisticated router or firewall device on board >> the router. How so? That device is configured for seamless failover. So let's say the Starling signal dips for just a second. Maybe a heavy rain cell passes over or it's doing a tricky satellite handover. The managed router instantly automatically reroutes all your active internet traffic over to the stable LTE connection >> without dropping the connection. >> Exactly. Your critical voice call, your remote desktop session, it all stays active. It effectively masks those brief Starlink dips that would otherwise kill a sensitive application. That's what delivers reliability for business use. >> So, it's not just about buying the Starlink dish. It's about architecting the entire onboard network properly like you would in a smart office building. And that demands a really specific physical installation, too, right? The sources really hammered at this point. >> They absolutely did. Getting the installation right is critical for optimal performance. The antenna needs to be mounted high up clear of any physical blockage, you know, away from other domes, life raft pods, antennas. >> And they specifically mentioned mounting it above any radar beams. Why is that so important? >> Yeah, that's crucial. Radar emits powerful microwave energy. If the Starlink antenna is mounted at the same level or too close, that radar energy sweeping past could potentially interfere with or even damage the sensitive electronics inside the phased array. So you could basically fry your Starlink terminal or just degrade its performance every time the radar spins >> pretty much. So clear sky view high up and away from radar interference and keep it clean. Salt spray, bird droppings, that can affect performance too. >> Okay, good physical setup. But then there's managing the data flow itself, right? This gets us into QoS. >> Absolutely essential. Quality of service or QoS is non-negotiable in this kind of hybrid setup. Think of your yacht's internet pipe like a highway. If you just let everyone merge on at full speed whenever they want, you get traffic jams. Gridlock >> makes sense. >> QoS is like having intelligent traffic lights and dedicated lanes. You program the network management system to prioritize traffic. Critical data navigation updates, VIP calls for the captain. Maybe that remote desktop connection for the owner always gets priority. The fast lane >> and the less critical stuff. Secondary traffic like guests streaming movies, big background downloads gets automatically throttled back or delayed slightly if the network gets busy with priority traffic. Without QoS, one person starting a massive game download could easily choke the connection and kill the captain's vital weather feed. >> Right. You need to control the flow. >> You have to. >> You know, that real world feedback we saw really sums it up. People consistently say Starlink is night and day better than the old satellite systems, finally enabling those low latency applications. But they also confirm the caveats. Rain can slow it down. Speeds drop in crowded ports. It underlines that the solution isn't just Starlink. >> No, it's Starlink integrated intelligently. It's Starlink plus smart network management. >> So, let's try and bring this to a final verdict. Based on everything we've reviewed, Starling Maritime clearly represents a huge leap forward. It offers a really compelling mix of speed, low latency, and relative simplicity, making it arguably the new baseline standard for most private yachts. >> I agree. And connecting that to the bigger picture, its value is truly maximized when you stop viewing it as just a standalone dish and start seeing it as the primary high performance pipe within a properly managed redundant network. That implementation, the smart router, the QoS settings, the seamless LTE 5G failover, that's what transforms Starlink from really good most of the time into consistently excellent for demanding real world use at C. >> Well said. Which leads us to a final thought for you to consider. Given that modern work, safety, and even guest expectations increasingly demand continuous, reliable high-speed connectivity offshore, will this engineered hybrid setup the combination of Starlink, cellular backup, and sophisticated QoS soon become the absolute non-negotiable standard for all high-v value yacht operations, regardless of the added complexity and cost. Something to think about. Join us next time for another deep dive.

It doesn't take a gallon of copper paint to cover an inch. 

The only way it costs $18000 per month to wash the teak deck is of the crew is using pick axes. Teak decks are washed each day with water...not champagne. They use special soap, not liquid gold. They use mops, not fur coats. $18000 per month is a bald faced lie. 

Again, boats are expensive. The bigger the more expense...just like a house. However, this is typical journalism....if you don't know... just make it up. Journalism is just story telling. This was a story...a false story

Delos right now we have 500 liters and when we fill our pickup it's enough water to last us for almost a month this water maker is capable of making 200 liters or approximately 50 US gallons an hour nice and we have a lot of crew on board one other thing that I really love with dolls is that we have so much food storage this is all food and stuff and if you here inside the cell phone we have - They Bought a Yacht With My Life Savings – I Did This Instead: Kind: captions Language: en My phone buzzed on the kitchen counter. Another notification. I'd been ignoring them all morning. Still too weak from the surgery to care about whatever was happening on social media. But this one kept buzzing again. Again, a group chat I hadn't checked in weeks. I picked it up, squinting at the screen. OMG, Sarah, is that your yacht? Marcus, you're living the dream. Wait, how did you afford that? Lol. My chest tightened, not the surgery pain. Something else. I opened Instagram with shaking hands. Found Sarah's profile and there it was. Posted 6 hours ago. My daughter in a white bikini and oversized sunglasses. My son beside her in swim trunks. Both of them grinning like they just won the lottery. Champagne glasses raised in a toast. Behind them, a gleaming 50-foot yacht. The name painted across the hall in gold letters, "New horizons. Location, Bahamas." The caption read, "Finally living the life we deserve. Clinking glasses sparkles. # blessed #new beginnings #smart investments. I stared at that photo, at their faces, at that yacht, and something cold began spreading through my veins. Smart investments. Three weeks ago, I'd signed power of attorney papers before my triple bypass surgery. Standard procedure, my lawyer had said, "Just in case." Sarah had held my hand. Marcus had promised to take care of everything. My hands were trembling now as I opened my banking app, checked my investment account, the one I'd been building for 23 years, the one that had $817,000 in it last time I looked. The screen loaded. Balance $4,322.19. I stopped breathing. Scroll down to recent transactions. Transfer. Transfer. Transfer. Transfer. Seven large transfers over the past 2 weeks. All to an account I didn't recognize. All authorized by power of attorney. The last transfer was dated yesterday. $127,000. Memo. Chun family trust account final distribution. My life savings gone and my children were on a yacht in the Bahamas drinking champagne celebrating the life they deserved. The phone slipped from my hands and clattered onto the floor. I should tell you how we got here. How a mother who sacrificed everything, who worked three jobs to put her ungrateful children through private school, who skipped meals so they could have new clothes, ended up betrayed by the very people she'd given her life to protect. But the real story, the one that kept me up every night after that moment, started 23 years earlier with a decision I made that I thought would secure their future forever. My name is Margaret Chen. And for 47 years, I believed I was doing everything right. When my husband David died of a sudden heart attack in 2001, I was 32 years old with two children under five. Sarah was four, Marcus was two, and I was a part-time receptionist with $1,700 in our joint account. David's life insurance barely covered the funeral costs. His parents blamed me for his stress, for his death, and cut us off completely. My own parents had passed years before. We were alone. I remember sitting on our apartment floor that first week, bills scattered around me like fallen leaves, watching my children sleep on the couch we' bought at a garage sale. Sarah had her thumb in her mouth. Marcus was clutching his father's old baseball cap, and I made a promise to them, to David's memory, to myself, I would give them everything. They would never struggle the way I had. They would have opportunities, education, security. They would never feel the cold grip of poverty that I started working. Not just one job, three, sometimes four. Mornings, I answered phones at a law firm. Afternoons, I cleaned houses. Evenings, I waitressed at an upscale restaurant downtown. Weekends, I did data entry from home. I slept 4 hours a night. I wore the same three outfits for 2 years. I cut my own hair in the bathroom mirror. I ate pasta and canned vegetables while my children had fresh fruit and organic milk, but I was strategic about it. Every spare dollar went into a high yield savings account, then into index funds, then into carefully researched stocks. I taught myself about investing using library books and free online courses. By the time Sarah turned 10, I'd saved $50,000. By Marcus' high school graduation, it was 200,000. By last year, when I turned 55, I'd reached $817,000. My children never knew about it, not the full amount. I told them we had some savings for emergencies, nothing more. I wanted them to stay humble, to work hard, to appreciate what they had, and they did seem grateful. At least I thought they did. Sarah became a marketing manager at a tech startup. Marcus went into finance. They had good jobs, nice apartments, respectable lives. I was so proud. I plan to tell them about the money on my 60th birthday. I had it all worked out in my head. A family dinner, a heartfelt speech about sacrifice and love, and then the reveal for 100,000 for each of them. Enough to buy homes, start businesses, secure their own children's futures. It would be my final gift. Proof that every sacrifice had been worth it. But life had other plans. 6 months ago, I started having chest pains, severe ones. My doctor ordered tests, found blockages, scheduled surgery. Triple bypass, he said, but nothing to worry about. I was strong, healthy despite my age. Sarah and Marcus came to the hospital the day before the procedure. Sarah held my hand and cried. Marcus promised he'd take care of everything while I recovered. We love you, Mom, Sarah whispered. We're so lucky to have you. I signed the power of attorney paperwork that evening. Standard procedure, my lawyer said. Just in case something goes wrong during surgery, someone needs to handle your affairs. I listed both children as co-agents. They were successful, responsible adults. Why wouldn't I trust them? The surgery went well. I woke up groggy but alive. Tubes in my chest, everything wrapped in a fog of morphine and exhaustion. Sarah and Marcus visited every day that first week. They brought flowers, magazines, Sarah's homemade soup. They seemed attentive, concerned, perfect children doing their duty. I came home on day nine. Sarah had hired a nurse to check on me daily. Marcus had filled my refrigerator. They'd cleaned my apartment, put fresh sheets on my bed, left encouraging notes on the bathroom mirror. I felt guilty for ever doubting their love, even in my darkest moments of single parent exhaustion. But then came the phone call that changed everything. 3 weeks post surgery, still recovering, I received a call from my bank's fraud department. There had been unusual activity on my investment account. The representative said several large transfers. Did I authorize them? My hands went numb. What transfers? Ma'am, it appears someone with power of attorney authorization moved $812,000 to an external account over the past 2 weeks. The final transfer was completed yesterday. The room tilted. That's impossible. That money, that's my entire life savings. I understand, ma'am, but the authorization was legitimate. Signed by your designated agents. We're required to process transfers made by power of attorney holders unless you file a formal. I hung up. My chest hurt. Not from the surgery, but from something much deeper. Something tearing open inside my soul. I called Sarah first. It went to voicemail. Then Marcus. Also voicemail. I called Sarah's work. She's taken the week off. Her assistant said cheerfully. Went on vacation with her brother. something about a surprise trip. Isn't that nice? That's when I opened Instagram and that's when I saw the photo was posted 18 hours earlier. Sarah in a white bikini and oversized sunglasses, Marcus in swim trunks, both of them grinning like lottery winners. Behind them, a gleaming 50-foot yacht named New Horizons. The location tag said Bahamas. I scrolled through the comments. Friends congratulating them. Someone asking how they afforded it. Marcus had replied, "Let's just say we made some smart investments pay off." Smiling face with sunglasses. Smart investments. My investments, my money, my life. I sat there for 3 hours, phone in my lap, trying to understand, trying to find an explanation that made sense. Maybe it was a mistake. Maybe they'd borrowed against my account temporarily, planning to pay it back. Maybe there was a good reason. But then I checked my email and found the messages I'd been too drugged to notice earlier. Seven emails from Sarah sent over the past 2 weeks, all with the subject line, important estate documents. Please review. I deleted them thinking they were spam. I open them now. They were transfer authorization forms, professionally formatted, covered in legal language. Each one transferring a portion of my investments to something called Chin family trust account. And at the bottom of each form, my electronic signature harvested from the power of attorney documents. They'd forged them. Technically legal because of the POA, but morally, criminally, they'd stolen from their own mother while she was recovering from heart surgery. But I still didn't understand why. They had good jobs. They didn't need my money. I called Sarah again. This time she answered, "Mom, how are you feeling? We were just thinking about you." Her voice was bright, cheerful, slightly slurred. Day drinking on my stolen money. Sarah, I said quietly. I need you to tell me what happened to my investment account. Silence long enough that I could hear ocean waves in the background. Mom, I we can talk about this when we get back. You stole from me. My voice cracked. You stole everything I saved. It's not like that. Her tone shifted. Defensive now. You don't understand. Marcus and I, we've been talking and we realized, "Mom, you're not getting any younger. You had a major surgery. What if something had happened? All that money would have just gone to the state or taxes or whatever. We're your children. It's basically our inheritance anyway. Inheritance. I couldn't breathe. I'm not dead, Sarah. I know, but she covered the phone. Muffled conversation. Marcus's voice in the background. When she came back, her tone was harder. Look, Mom, you've always been so secretive about money. We didn't even know you had that much saved. And honestly, after everything we've done for you, taking care of you, visiting you in the hospital, managing your recovery, we deserve this. You deserve this. Yes. She said it like it was obvious. Do you know how hard it's been having to put our lives on hold to take care of you? Marcus missed a major client meeting. I had to delay a promotion opportunity. We've sacrificed a lot, Mom. This is just compensation. Something inside me crystallized into ice. Where did the money go, Sarah? We made investments. Smart ones. The yacht is just part of it. We're starting a charter business in the Bahamas. It's going to be huge. You should be proud of us. Proud. The word tasted like poison. Yeah. I mean, God. Mom, you worked yourself to death your whole life. And for what? To die with a pile of money in the bank. At least we're living. At least we're making something of ourselves. You should be thanking us for I hung up, but the real twist was still coming. I sat there numb trying to process what had just happened. Then my phone buzzed. A text from Marcus. Mom. Sarah's upset. You need to understand. We were going to tell you eventually. The POA thing was Dad's idea anyway. He left instructions. Dad's idea. David, my husband, dead for 23 years. What the hell did that mean? I went to the closet and pulled out the box of David's things I'd kept all these years. Photos, letters, documents. I'd gone through it a hundred times. But now I was looking for something specific. And I found it. An envelope yellowed with age addressed to Sarah and Marcus to be opened on your mother's decline or death. My hands shook as I tore it open. Inside was a letter in David's handwriting. Dated 3 days before he died. To my darling children. If you're reading this, your mother is either gone or failing. I want you to know the truth about the money she's been saving. Before I met your mother, I was engaged to another woman. Her name was Elizabeth Chen. We were going to be married, start a family, but she got pregnant before the wedding. And when I found out the baby wasn't mine, she'd been having an affair with my business partner, I left her. I was angry, devastated. I made a decision I've regretted every day since. Elizabeth had a younger sister, Margaret, your mother. She was 19, working as a waitress, trying to put herself through college. She'd always had a crush on me. I knew that. And in my anger and pain, I used her. I seduced her, got her pregnant with Sarah, and then I married her. Not because I loved her, but because I wanted to hurt Elizabeth to show her I could move on, replace her, start the family she'd stolen from me. By the time Marcus was born, I'd fallen in love with Margaret for real. But I never told her the truth about why I married her. I let her believe it was fate, love at first sight, destiny. The guilt has eaten me alive. I'm dying, kids. Heart disease. The doctors give me 6 months. I'm not going to tell your mother. She's already struggling, already sacrificing so much. But I've set up something for her. Insurance policies, investments. When I'm gone, she'll have enough to survive, to raise you well. But here's what you need to know. Margaret will save every penny. She'll work herself to death. She'll sacrifice everything because that's who she is. Someone who believes love means suffering. She learned that from me. Don't let her do that forever. When the time comes, when she's declining, when she can't fight anymore, take the money. Use it. Live the lives she never let herself live. Don't waste it on guilt or obligation. She'll be angry, but she'll be free from the burden of holding on to something that was built on lies. Take care of each other. And remember, you don't owe your mother your lives just because she gave up hers. That was her choice. You get to make yours. Love always. Dad, I read it three times. Then I threw up. Everything I thought I knew about my life was a lie. David hadn't loved me. Not at first. I was a revenge plot, a replacement, a consolation prize. and he'd left instructions for our children to rob me when I was at my most vulnerable, calling it freedom. But that wasn't even the worst part. The worst part was realizing I'd done exactly what he predicted. I'd worked myself to death. I'd sacrificed everything. I'd built my entire identity around being the mother who gave and gave and gave until there was nothing left. And my children had learned from him, not me. They'd learned that I was something to be used, that my love was an obligation, not a gift, that they were entitled to everything I'd built because I'd been stupid enough to build it for them. I spent 3 days in that apartment, not eating, barely sleeping, reading David's letter over and over. Then I made some calls. First to my lawyer. Is there any way to reverse the transfers with power of attorney authorization? Unlikely. You'd have to prove fraud or incompetence. But if you sign those POA documents willingly, what about pursuing criminal charges? Elder abuse, possibly financial exploitation, but you'd be taking your own children to court. It would be messy, public, expensive, and there's no guarantee you'd win. Second to the bank. Can you freeze the yacht purchase? It's already processed, ma'am. The yacht is titled in your son's name. Third, to a private investigator. I need you to find out everything about my children's finances. Everything. That's when the final twist revealed itself. Sarah and Marcus didn't have good jobs anymore. They both been fired 6 months before my surgery. Sarah for embezzling company funds. A small amount swept under the rug, but enough to blacklist her in the industry. Marcus for insider trading. not enough evidence to prosecute, but enough to destroy his reputation. They weren't successful. They were desperate, and they planned to steal from me the moment they found out about my surgery. The private investigator found emails, texts, conversations between them dating back 8 months, right after they'd found David's letter in a safety deposit box I didn't know existed. We deserve this more than she does. She's going to die eventually anyway. Why wait? Dad was right. She's wasted her whole life. We can actually do something with the money. They'd engineered my trust, the hospital visits, the soup, the clean department, all of it calculated to make me sign that power of attorney without suspicion. And I had because I loved them. Because I'd given my whole life to them. Because I still believed somewhere deep down that love was enough. It wasn't. I didn't take them to court. I didn't file charges. I didn't try to get the money back. Instead, I did something David's letter never accounted for. I disappeared. I sold my apartment, the furniture, everything I owned. I took the $18,000 I had left in my checking account. I changed my phone number. I closed my social media accounts. I moved to a small town in Montana where nobody knew my name. And I started over at 55 with almost nothing. I started over. I got a job at a local bookstore. Minimum wage, no benefits, but honest work. I rented a studio apartment above a coffee shop. I bought secondhand clothes and cooked simple meals and walked everywhere because I couldn't afford a car. And for the first time in 47 years, I felt free because I wasn't working for anyone else anymore. I wasn't sacrificing for children who saw me as a resource to be exploited. I wasn't carrying the weight of David's guilt or his manipulations. I wasn't performing the role of good mother or devoted widow. I was just Margaret finally. Sarah called six times in the first month, then Marcus. Then both of them frantic when they realized I'd vanished. They filed a missing person's report. The police found me within 3 days. I wasn't hiding from the law, just from them. She doesn't want contact with you, the officer told them. She's of sound mind and making her own choices. There's nothing we can do. They tried to visit. I refused to see them. They sent letters. I burned them unopened. They hired a lawyer to contest my disappearance to claim I was mentally unfit. The evaluation came back clear. I was perfectly sane. just done. The yacht business failed within 8 months. They'd overextended themselves, underestimated costs, and crashed spectacularly into debt. Last I heard from my private investigator. Yes, I kept him on retainer with my meager wages because I wanted to know they'd filed for bankruptcy. Sarah was back to living with roommates. Marcus was working in a call center. They lost everything, including me. People ask me sometimes if I regret it. Walking away from my children, letting them destroy themselves, choosing loneliness over family. I tell them the truth. I don't have children anymore. I have two strangers who share my DNA and taught me the hardest lesson of my life. Love is not sacrifice. Sacrifice is not love. And sometimes the most loving thing you can do is let people face the consequences of who they choose to be. I work at the bookstore. I have coffee with my co-workers. I read novels I never had time for before. I take walks in the mountains and watch sunsets without thinking about anyone's future but my own. And sometimes late at night I think about David's letter. About how he was wrong. About how I'm not free because I gave up the burden of money I never got to keep. I'm free because I finally learned that I was never anyone's martyr to burn. I was just a woman who loved the wrong people too much. And now I'm a woman who's learning slowly to love herself. The money is gone. My children are gone. The life I built for 47 years is gone. But I'm still here. And for the first time, that feels like enough. Last week, a letter arrived at the bookstore. No return address. Sarah's handwriting. I held it for 3 days before opening it. Inside was a single sentence. Mom, we're sorry. We were wrong. please come home." And beneath it, a check for $42,000. All they had left. I looked at that check for a long time, thought about what it represented. Thought about forgiveness, second chances, the complexity of love and betrayal. Then I tore it into pieces, and threw it in the trash. Because some bridges aren't meant to be rebuilt. Some endings are the only honest beginning, and some mothers need to save themselves instead. If this story spoke to your heart, don't let it fade. Subscribe to The Silent Parents, where every emotion finds a home.

- 5 Reasons NOT to buy a live aboard sailing boat  - (Watch before you buy!): Kind: captions Language: en after three years living on a sailing boat full time sharing the very best bits of our adventures with you all in this video we want to try and balance out some of our overly romanticized depictions of life living on a sailing vote and share with you our five reasons not to buy a lever board sailing boat reason number one everything breaks out of all the reasons not to buy a liveaboard sailboat everything breaking was the reason we were the most unprepared for we're stuck for the foreseeable even if you put your boat on the heart and fix absolutely everything that's broken it won't be long before something has broken again if you want to live on a boat full time then you're gonna have to embrace this reality and spend your time learning to become a mechanic a plumber an electrician and a general shipwriter and everything else in between there's so much to learn and that's just the boat itself we haven't even touched on the skills you need to take her off and navigate to new and potentially dangerous places [Music] reason number two money boats consume an ungodly amount of money even if you do learn to do most of the work yourself the reality is that much of the equipment you'll need is expensive from broken hatches to replacing life rafts new engine parts replacing sails new rigging lift outs it all adds up and fast we're constantly hearing stories of people giving up life on the water because they were just totally unprepared for how much ended up costing the good news is that life on a boat can work out much cheaper if you're prepared to live a bit more frugally the way that we manage to make a small amount of money last us is by spending half a year living out at anchor always trying to scout out somewhere new to drop our hook and stay for free it's not always possible to stay out of expensive marinas but the more we can avoid them the longer we can make our money last if you're lucky enough to be able to work remotely from your laptop and the liverboard life could be a great fit for you but if you're more like us and very hands-on workers then at some point you're likely going to need to head back to shore and find some work for us we choose to tie up for the winter months and find jobs where we rebuild the sailing kitty and perform all the repairs and upgrades to the boat ahead of setting sail again as the warmer months arrive now some of you may be wondering hey but you guys are youtubers doesn't that give you an income too well yes we are and yes it does but for 99 of video makers out there the money you make from youtube is enough to maybe keep some beers in the fridge and of course hank's treat jar nicely topped up we are also very lucky that our humble little channel has the support of an incredibly generous group of patrons and this additional income helps repair and replace our constantly failing filming equipment which doesn't always play so well with the harsh salt water environment that comes with living on a sailing boat it also helps pay for the odd luxury stop in an expensive marina from time to time we're forever grateful to our patrons for all they do it's lovely to think that others are enjoying these adventures enough to support their future production reason number three the trade-offs giving up some of those land creature comforts in almost all cases moving on to a sailing boat is going to involve some downsizing serious downsizing and this is especially true if you're looking to move into an affordable monohull boat depending on how you intend to use your boat it could mean giving up mod cons like a freezer washing machine dryer hot water big tv reliable internet connection space for your many clothes and toys however with some careful planning the addition of solar panels some lithium batteries and a decent inverter and you can actually still enjoy many of the everyday creature comforts to be found on land even if you're living at anchor hot water but beware as lovely as life on a sailing boat can be with these amazing upgrades know that they don't come cheap if you'd like to install enough lithium solar a decent inverter with say a water maker too this is the kind of upgrade package which can easily set you back five figures even if you're doing the work yourself reason number four being well rested if you've decided to embrace the more frugal existence and avoid expensive marinas by living on your anchor then be prepared to never sleep quite as deeply and peacefully as you once did on land this reality hits you the hardest when you first start sleeping at anchor your biggest fear being that you haven't set your anchor properly and it drags while you're asleep or worse yet when you're not even on your boat at all there are things you can do to mitigate anchoring catastrophes and give you back a little peace of mind check over all of your ground tackle each year as part of your annual maintenance buy a modern oversized anchor that's easy to set set up multiple anchor alarms which alert you the moment your boat drifts outside a predetermined area and if you really want to be thorough you can even dive in and make sure your anchor is set correctly even if you take every precaution possible the chances are you will still sleep with at least a little part of your brain staying awake and alert listening out for new sounds or sensations in the boat's motion truth be told it gets better as time goes on and you grow more comfortable with anchoring but at first it certainly takes a little getting used to but don't let us totally put you off the idea of living on your anchor because what you may give up slightly in terms of rem sleep you more than make up for when you wake up in some of the most tranquil beautiful breathtaking locations which very often you have all to yourself reason number five your pets may hate it while it's true that most people don't take their family pet sailing it also seems to be the case that a high number of people who live on a sailboat full time do so with their little furry companions we got really lucky with hank he gets on great aboard cadoa he has no trouble getting around the boat he's very relaxed when we're on the move and often just found asleep well that is until we're joined by some of the amazing wildlife to be found with a life on the water in which case he's fascinated by all the weird and wonderful creatures he gets to meet one of the great pleasures we have is being able to take hank to so many more varied walks and he'd likely have ever ventured with in a more conventional life he's a happy dog whose life is rich with adventure and variety but of course there is a catch several in fact firstly despite our best efforts hank refuses to go to the toilet on board the boat so we have to carefully plan our voyages to ensure we can get him to shore at least twice a day normally first thing in the morning and again in the evening hank gets on fire when we're underway but we are super selective about when we move our home it's safe to say that as such we're very much fair weather sailors because even with hank's fantastic temperament on board anything too boisterous just it just wouldn't be fair on him so it seems as if we've worked a life on the water around our specific dog's needs but there's still a major catch especially when you choose to live at anchor because come wind rain or shine we need to get hank to show no matter what and that often means doing so in conditions that you'd much rather not venture out in is that something you could imagine doing for 10 plus years if not then that's a fifth good reason not to buy a live aboard sailing boat i hope this video has provided some balance to the lifestyle that's otherwise all too easy to romanticize thanks for watching see you next time

I think the ability to to deliver what we deliver is is is interesting, but I think what I get most out of the job itself is the lifestyle that affords me outside of when I'm actually working. So that's something to remember is that you might not necessarily do this job for fulfillment with with the work itself. Although it's it's not definitely not unfulfilling, but the lifestyle out of work is is truly exceptional in my opinion. So that's first criteria. Second one is fair compensation and financial stability. Now against any other job in the world, I again I think yaching comes out number one on this one. And of all these six criteria, I think this is the strongest. So with yaching, when you're on the yacht, because they pay, you don't have to pay rent. You're not paying taxes. For me, I don't pay taxes because Brits get seafares exemption deduction no matter where I am in the world. As long as I'm a British citizen, I'll never pay tax. It's not the same for every country. I know US citizens and Australian citizens, it's not the same. I think for Aussies, it's only if they're on Aussie flag boats, though. So, there's that to consider. No taxes, no expenses for food, uh, no travel expenses for the most part, depending on what contract you're on. So, you're in a really good uh position financially and even me, I'm not the best at saving. It's almost impossible not to save in yaching because you're earning so so much money and there'll be periods where you're on a trip where you just have won't have the opportunity to spend it. Um so, for that for that reason, it has to be one of the best um careers for financial stability. I actually did some calculations recently and if if um if you factored me earning my salary but working in London and um paying rent prices in London and living costs in London and taxes in the UK, the amount I would be earning in pounds in the UK is somewhere between 140 and £170,000 right now. So although my actual salary is not that, it just puts in perspective the lifestyle that you can live on what is still a very good salary when you don't have those um expensive uh living costs. So it's it's truly incredible on that front and that's even without adding on charter tips as well. Work life balance and flexibility. So this is very interesting with yaching. It can be just balls to the wall. you're working basically 12 to 16 hours a day, all within the MLC labor laws, which if they're not if you're on a boat that isn't following those laws, then I would suggest to leave. That's always my suggestion if they're if it's no good leave. Um, but yeah, you can be you can be um working really really long days on trip, but then when you're off trip, then if you're on a good boat, you should be really working 40 to 45 50 hours a week. I mean, you don't commute, so that that time's all spent working. Um, you don't have to add in the commute time on a normal job, which could end up adding 10, 20 hours onto your working week. And so, for that part, you're working 8 to 5, Monday to Friday. You're getting the weekends off in a beautiful location. I think that gives you a great lifestyle. Personally for me, I'm going to be off the boat every night, whether I'm going to be doing salsa, whether I'm going to be doing jiu-jitsu, going to the gym, just going out and seeing the place that I'm in. It does give you an incredible quality of life. If you think you're working eight, nine hours a day, you still got another eight, seven hours left of the day when you're done, having done no commute, you probably won't feel that tired because you haven't had that stress of the commute and all of these other things that are going on. Um, so for that part, the work life balance for me is is brilliant. Now, there are caveats to that. I've been on boats where uh one in particular in Italy where I was working there for 3 months and we would work Monday to Saturday, 12 hours a day. I kid you not, 7:00 till 7:00 and then every other Sunday we were working for four or five hours. So we would work and that was in shipyard. So then we were going to go on trip and work even more. And uh I was still early on in my career when I got this job. I think it was my third or fourth job and so I didn't really know any different. Personally for now like I saw guys turning up to that job me like for day work or like long for like temp work like this is terrible I'm leaving. I thought ah I thought this was terrible too. But at the time obviously you're limited in options so you kind of have to do it or you know look for another job and it is difficult early on to find jobs in yaching with no with little to no experience. So I stuck it out for a little bit longer but ultimately it didn't work out and I honestly think that if if a yacht is doing that it's a huge red flag. Huge red flag. Like if if you can you should leave. You shouldn't be working more than 45 50 hours a week in a shipyard period. And that's max there. I've known some people that work four or five days a week 9 till 2 in a shipyard period. So if you're in a shipyard period, you shouldn't be shouldn't be working those kind of hours. So the final point and the final summary on work life balance is it is what you make it. How you spend your time outside of the yacht is going to determine how much you have that balance. And then how you choose and maintain your jobs is also going to determine that balance. You know, don't be a mug. Yes, there is a bit of scarcity in jobs in yacht, but it's not as much as people uh fear-monger in the industry. There's an abundance of jobs. It is very difficult to get jobs in the beginning. So, stick it out if you can. But if it's really if it's, you know, if it's really stressing you out, it's probably not worth it. I would say go find another job. And if they're working to the bone, you to the bone, go find another role. So, growth and learning opportunities. So while I was in the Navy, I I learned a lot for sure. I was in a I was a naval officer was in a management position and I did learn a lot. But I would say I've learned the same in yaching. The only difference is is when I started I was learning something very different cuz I was come from a managerial background and self-employed background and now I was doing all of these very very basic repetitive repetitives tasks. What I didn't appreciate though was I was building a skill set where I was able to add value and I was also doing the job that I was eventually going to basically help other junior crew members learn which actually was really nice for me personally coming from a background where I didn't really have that um experience working my way up and then was just put at the top with just like academic knowledge rather than knowledge of actually doing the job. you get this huge imposter syndrome and I would say in this case it's not a good kind of imposter syndrome where you just you're just reaching you really you really don't know what you're doing that much whereas on here I can be very sure of myself and certain that if someone's doing a job whether they're doing it well or not and then if I can help them uh improve on that one so personally for me I've gone down the small boat route this is a 40 meter and that's how I plan to structure my career I'll never probably never go above 45 that's the way I want to do it the advantages of that in the early stages is as you get a lot more responsibility early on. Personally for me, if I was on a 60 m now, I'd probably be bosen if that because they probably want more longevity and more specificity on size of boat and not like that on small boats. And it's not to their detriment. Small boats sometimes get a bad rep for being less professional. That's absolutely not true. Um, this is one of the most professional boats I've ever been on. And we have a great time as well when we're not working, even though it's a small boat. and I get a lot of responsibility um despite only having 3 years experience because if there's only six crew you're inevitably going to take on a lot more a lot more responsibility. Now if you don't want responsibility then maybe big boats are for you but personally I don't want to live my life without responsibility. Responsibility for me especially for me where I am in my life uh it gives me purpose. So growth and learning opportunities yaching has loads um you're really going to be learning from one end the sort of academic knowledge managerial side and the other end just like the actual hands-on part of the job and where they meet in the middle. So, there's a really good balance of that and I can't see many jobs where it serves the same. Most jobs are like very hands-on or very managerial and they don't really have that mixing and I think it's a really wonderful job for having having that uh balance in that one. Positive relationships and culture. I would say that this one is very controversial because I think on a boat you have a tendency for it to go really well or really not well because you're living in a tin can you're all crammed together. You you you sleep, eat, and work all in the same place. So this takes some getting used to and even for me coming from a Royal Navy background on a ship of likeund well actually 250 people that is a different environment to be on board with six. It's a it's a much more um tough environment to learn and and and learn how to how to get on well with this with this environment. Okay. There's a lot of nuance about sharing a cabin with someone. Dos and don'ts of that which you just pick up naturally. Um and having respect for the for for your cabin mate for example. That can be a real difficult thing especially if you don't get on with your cabin mate. Most people probably start there. I think that's probably one point of friction in terms of relationships. The other is probably if you don't get on with someone in your chain of command. So for me as a dehandan, my chain of command is going to be the bosen, then the chief mate, then the captain. If any one of those people I have frequent interactions with and we don't see eye to eye, it's definitely going to cause friction. I mean, the best way you can avoid this is really just to get on and and do the job. Um, you do get people come on and question a lot of things when they actually don't know anything. Um, yeah, and I think just to divide as well professional and personal relationships very quickly is is important. someone's just asking you to do something on deck. I see it I do see it as a military command like they're above you. You should follow the hierarchy and you're going to be in that position one day. And it is very frustrating when you just want people to get on and do things if they're just questioning what what you're your authority and what you're saying. So, and then when it's personal, use your personal time for your for your own for what you want. If if if you want to spend time on your own, then you should more than well feel entitled to do that. Um so, as I said, yes, it can it can go wrong. Um, and I I think it can go wrong a lot quicker um than, you know, normal normal careers. I've done normal jobs. It's generally not that fiery, but on yachts, it can get fiery quite quickly, and I think it does take a um a good strength of character to learn how to how to manage those situations. Um, so for me personally, yes, it can de develop incredibly strong positive relationships very quickly. I mean, after 2 weeks in here, you probably spent more time with your close friend than you did in the last three three years with the people that you're around. So, in that way, it can be great. And if you're not going to get on that well, you know, if you're not going to be best friends, you can't be best friends with anyone, then you just develop this ability to be to be amicable with people, which is great. And you don't necessarily have to develop that ability in other jobs. um you know it depends but it's definitely not the same as on your own. And finally autonomous um and control. So I would say that for yaching this is the absolute lowest point of all of these six criteria. And what's funny is it's probably the one I value the most personally. So it's definitely something I'd had to give personal sacrifice to as a characteristic of of yaching. um you basically don't have control or or any any uh um autonomy about what you're doing. You'll in terms of leave on this on this uh contract, I get 40 days leave a year. That's quite low. Uh industry lowest is probably like 27 if they're just going off the flag state, which most boats do if they're a small boat. And that's definitely a downside to small boats. However, when you're when you're on a certain salary, if you take all that time off, you're probably going to spend the majority of what you've earned. So, there is that to consider. And for me personally, when I'm on here, I get all my bills paid for. I'm in a beautiful spot most of the time. And so, that's actually quite a good deal. I don't really need rotation. I don't really need that much leave. And if I do take leave, I'm just going to go spend money. It's something you want to get when you're senior, when you've got a family, when your when your salary is much higher, and when that time off is really going to be um useful to you. But early on, I wouldn't I wouldn't care too much about that. And that does play into the the autonomy and control that you're going to have. And the other factor is is the program. The program changes a lot. You're going to have no control over that. I mean one of the reasons why that happens is you're serving you know basically one person the owner if you're if you're a private boat or charter guest which you know these people are very demanding and they can change things all the time and as as they should be able to you're offering a luxury service they should have flexibility so the program can change quite quickly trips can get extended and cancelled uh shipyards periods can get extended because work doesn't get done in time so it can all it can all uh change so you need to be very adaptable when it comes to yaching and probably lacks the most in that one. So, you know, if you don't mind giving up full control over your life to uh to the to the program of the boat, then you'll be absolutely fine. And for me personally, although I value autonomy and control, it's been okay to give up give up that because everything else makes up for it. And sometimes it's just nice having all the decisions made for you and when you're going to be. Um, personally, you do have control before you take the role on where the boat's going to be based. I accepted the job on this boat because I wanted to be in Australia. I wanted to immigrate into Australia and I knew the best way to do that would be to get a job in Australia. Now, there aren't many jobs in Australia. So, I was very fortunate. It just came up this year cuz I decided this year I wanted to do that. It took about probably 6 months of looking for jobs for this one to come up and feel very fortunate. It's been a it's been a great role. So control what you can control, but once you're on board, um there isn't much you're going to be able to control about where you're going and what you're what you're going to be up to. And if you're not happy with it, then you can always leave. Um people won't tell you to do that, but I will encourage it a lot. There's an abundance of jobs out there, and you can always find another one. So if you're not happy with the control that's being put on you and what you've been told to do, um then you can always you can always find another job. One benefit I definitely don't want to leave out that I haven't mentioned yet is one thing that people are really struggling to do in Western world now is to save for a deposit for a house because rent is so high and so are mortgages. Now if there is a better way um to do that than yaching then you'll have to tell me because I can't find one. But you can save a housing deposit in yaching very very quickly. it'll probably only take you 12 months whereas some people might spend 10 years to do that same thing. So saving for a housing deposit is a huge huge advantage to boats and I do feel like some people have that as possibly they even know the number one reason around why they would actually start to get into yaching because it's so effective for doing that. I haven't really spoken about the downsides of yaching and I think because one one reason is that it's very dependent on what kind of boat you're on. I've experienced a lot. As I said in the beginning of this video, I would say that one of the downsides is if you're moving around boats a lot like I did, it's very difficult to build long-lasting friendships. It will be in general because crew rotation is high whatever boat you get on. So, even if you stay on a boat for a long time, you're probably going to experience a lot of crew rotation. If you're also moving around uh bases a lot, then it's going to be hard to build a network. Personally, when I was in Italy, for example, I had a great time. definitely found it difficult to build a network because there weren't many people my age there. You know, Italian isn't my first language. So, I think going to a going to a country where English is the first language. You're going to be in the same base a lot. You're probably going to be able to avoid the downside of not being able to build uh long-lasting relationships, which for for me personally has been it's been fun to meet a lot of people, but it does get tiring after a few years, especially now I'm at the age of 32. I want to build like strong foundational friendships. I don't want to be moving around all the time. I want a good balance of both. So for that reason it can be difficult but again it's it's what you can make of it. Now I can't speak from my own personal experience of what it's like on other and other industries on this topic but sexual violence does exist in yaching. Um, it is a very strange environment because people want to be in yaching so badly that they are willing to transgress their moral compass far more than they would in another role because of the supply and demand. There's a lot of people that want to do yaching and there aren't that many jobs. So, this leads into very strong power dynamics uh especially on bigger boats. And I would say look if you feel uncomfortable on the boat and you don't feel protected by your superiors then you then you should leave. You should leave. I know of a few exper experiences of other people that have been pretty horrific. I don't know what it's like compared to other industries but there have been awful cases of sexual violence in yachin. I don't know if it's a really common thing. It does exist and I would say look if you feel unsafe on a boat then you should leave and if anything does happen you need to report it to your superiors. I've I know in in some cases that there's been no comeuppance in in that situation. They've turned a blind eye to it. Then that's what your DPA is for. You designate a person ashore. I've made a report to a DPA before. They did nothing about it. So that's really frustrating because they should be your last line of defense. And in that case, if it was something that was illegal, then you need to report it to the police. Um for the most part, I think you're unsafe and I think it's a very very small percentage of cases where this does happen. But it does happen. It's a strange environment. you sleep in it, you know, next to all of these people and um you don't necessarily get the best people in yacht, but you can get some of the greatest. It's very polarizing industry. I think I've met the greatest people I've ever met in yacht and and also the worst. So, you know, it's definitely an adventurous and it's definitely a great experience. Um but just be wary of being in an environment where you don't necessarily feel safe. It might not be um about the topic I just mentioned. It might just be in your working routine. their working practices aren't safe, you know, raise it to them and if they don't if they don't um listen to you, then again, like I said, you can leave. You know, that's always always a choice. One more downside of yaching is for for me personally, I found it can be a bit lonely at times. Um, but I would say living on your own can be lonier and I think it's just depends on how you frame the experience. Um, you know, you can be lonely on your own and you can be lonely surrounded by other people. I think it's obviously easier if you're surrounded by crew that you really get on with, but you know, make the most of the solitude. You you've got kind of no responsibilities in terms of bills to pay and all of that stuff. So, you've got you haven't got those worries. So, when you do have time alone, you know, you can kind of switch off. You can I I read a lot. I listen to podcasts. One of the best positive things for me personally is that when I'm working, when I'm like rinsing down the boat or doing deck work and you know, I can put an earphone in, I can listen to a a podcast, I can learn while I'm working. And if you actually think about in knowledge work jobs, I'm going on a tangent here, but in knowledge work jobs, when you're working, you have to focus so much. So you know when it comes to loneliness you probably can be lonely in in other in other roles more than this. Um and it can be lonely because you're away from home but I would just say build a base wherever you go and make the most of it. And uh when you do have time on your own when you're working fill it with podcasts and fill it with good music if if you're working on your own which I do a lot cuz I'm on small boats. But, you know, uh, all in balance, I I would say that it's probably the most sociable job that I can think of that I've had or that I know of. Um, you're always surrounded by people, so you're never really on your own as such. It just depends on whether you feel lonely in their company. And for the most part, I I I have haven't had that experience. And I have definitely been far more lonely when I've been uh ashore or or just taking a break from yaching. So, overall, very very good. Her&nbsp; sweeping exterior lines give her a refined,&nbsp;&nbsp; regal bearing wherever she glides&nbsp; into port. At over 200 feet long,&nbsp;&nbsp; Lionheart dominates any marina or&nbsp; anchorage, dwarfing lesser craft. Number 16 - Rising Sun Measuring over 450 feet long, she offers immense&nbsp;&nbsp; interior volume spread across 7 spacious decks.&nbsp; This golden avenue castle was originally built in&nbsp;&nbsp; 2004 by Lürssen Yachts of Germany for Oracle CEO&nbsp; Larry Ellison at a cost exceeding $200 million. She has&nbsp; entertained A-list celebrities and business&nbsp;&nbsp; moguls around the world thanks to owner&nbsp; David Geffen's many Hollywood connections. Number 15 - Octopus Microsoft co-founder Paul Allen&nbsp;&nbsp; spent a reported $200 million constructing this&nbsp; 413-foot giant in 2003. She remains one of the&nbsp;&nbsp; largest personal superyachts ever launched,&nbsp; weighing over 10,000 gross metric tons. Octopus requires a full-time crew of&nbsp; 57 just to maintain her many complex&nbsp;&nbsp; systems and tend to pamper guests. She&nbsp; offers abundant space for 26 esteemed&nbsp;&nbsp; passengers across 13 lavish VIP suites.&nbsp; Facilities aboard include a music studio,&nbsp;&nbsp; full-size basketball court, glass bottom&nbsp; swimming pool, movie theatre, spa,&nbsp;&nbsp; helipad and a garage housing multiple&nbsp; helicopters and a personal submarine. Paul Allen insisted on an environmentally&nbsp; friendly hybrid propulsion system for Octopus,&nbsp;&nbsp; utilizing liquified natural gas and diesel. Her&nbsp; eight generators produce enough power for a small&nbsp;&nbsp; town! Clearly no expense was spared designing&nbsp; and engineering Allen's personal megayacht. Though Paul Allen passed away in 2018, his&nbsp; estate has kept Octopus in flawless condition,&nbsp;&nbsp; still cruising the world. Guests wanting an&nbsp; especially luxurious escape can charter her&nbsp;&nbsp; for over $2 million per week. She&nbsp; has been spotted in locales from&nbsp;&nbsp; the French Riviera during the Cannes Film&nbsp; Festival to Australia's Great Barrier Reef. Whether one desires fine dining beneath&nbsp; the stars, an underwater excursion,&nbsp;&nbsp; or basketball on the tide-compensated&nbsp; court, Octopus brings your wishes into&nbsp;&nbsp; reality. Each space provides optimal&nbsp; views outward through vast windows. Number 14 - Lady Moura The beloved yacht of Saudi&nbsp;&nbsp; billionaire Nasser Al-Rashid. This 344-foot regal&nbsp; vessel was custom built by Dutch firm Blohm+Voss&nbsp;&nbsp; and delivered in 1990 after nearly 6 years of&nbsp; meticulous construction. At the time, Lady Moura incorporated&nbsp; numerous technological firsts like&nbsp;&nbsp; her automated retractable roof and internal&nbsp; floodable garages to store tenders, jet skis&nbsp;&nbsp; and other watercraft. - Eco-friendly Cleaning: Busting 3 Myths for Greener Boating | Sailors for the Sea: Kind: captions Language: en hi my name is Shelly and I'm with Sailors for the sea powered by Oceana did you know that some cleaning products can damage fish tissues or persist in the environment and end up in our food chain some cleaning products can also add excess nutrients into our waterways including nitrogen phosphorus which may lead to algal blooms which can also be harmful for our Coastal ecosystems it can be a daunting task to find the right non-toxic cleaning product for your boat but with the proper knowledge and research you can find one that actually works and is less impactful for the environment today I'll be sharing three myths about cleaning products first most boaters believe that soaps that are made for boats can end up going directly into our waterways however most boat cleaning products are like household cleaners and need to go through wastewater treatment facilities where the majority of contaminants and chemicals are removed before the water goes back into rivers lakes in the ocean second many boaters think that words like eco-friendly natural biodegradable non-toxic are regulated actually there are no federal requirements or specific standards that manufacturers must follow to make these claims additionally manufacturers are not required to list ingredients on their containers or make them public which can make finding a non-toxic cleaner challenging finally some voters believe the more product you use the better the cleaning power however increasing the soap to water ratio doesn't make a difference and in fact using less product can actually do the job better so what can boaters do to protect our waterways from potentially harmful cleaning products first rinse your boat with fresh water after every trip so you can go longer between washing with soap products next follow the manufacturer's instructions regarding dilution levels and use the least amount of product necessary finally if possible clean your boat and boat parts on Shore so the soap does not drain directly into the water some Arenas have designated washdown areas if you're interested in more EcoSmart tips for your boat head to our website at sailorsforthec.org Green tips to download our full green boating guide thank you for protecting our oceans and waterways

  1. @NaptownPete: As I have retired from my lifetime professional offshore racing career I've found a new calling, to lead the charge with environmentally responsible products and how best to use them with my company, TerraMarine Advanced Environmental Solutions. My main product, De-Oil-It (DOI) breaks down hydrocarbons resulting with each atom or molecule being surrounded with a positive charge called colloidal micelle, and the elements repel each other.
It's a perfect science. We are on the EPA National Contingency Plan for treating oil spills as a surface washing agent. Completely non-toxic to animal or plant life. But the DOI breaks down more than hydrocarbons (C-4 to C-40) but many other forever chemicals. We also have a solid but growing customer base in Florida treating Harmful Algae Blooms (HAB) as the DOI kills the two PbTx-2 and PbTx-3 brevotoxins and the nitrates the algae feeds upon.
We are working on our white paper, at great cost with laboratory certifications, to prove the same colloidal micelle process works on PFAS.

Now Kiyra you're doing much more than working on a superyacht and providing services. You do have a bit of a reputation a growing reputation as being an influencer in your industry, You have founded The Green Stewardess and you've also started the show on Yachting International Tadio about eco-tips. Tell me a little bit about The Green Stewardess, some of the things that are your values and what you're trying to accomplish. Absolutely. So The Green Stewardess was founded last year and it came from a very organic place. As I mentioned I've been in yachting for almost five years now and as soon as I stepped on board my first vessel, which was a fifty five meter Trinity superyacht for a lovely lovely family, I was just blown away at the uber luxuriousness of the interior and the exterior and so that was the first thing that wowed me and then immediately after I just noticed how much waste there was. Unfortunately in many different departments and it was even back then that we could see it sort of creeping into our our industry into the water around us and so slowly but surely I kind of went on a personal crusade to to find alternative products, find alternative solutions which I implemented successfully on the boats that I was employed on and they really worked and I had to trial a good few products some were very bad some were okay and some were eco-friendly and fantastic at getting the job done so it was a long process of elimination over many years but it definitely came from a very pure and honest place of just wanting to make a small difference where I could personally and then of course people started listening and seeing what I was doing and I was asked to implement systems on other boats and people were really eager to make these changes and so The Green Stewardess was born and it's doing very well as doing great things and I'm so honored that we've had such a great response to be honest. Well and I see too that you've probably inspired other 'stewardesses', if that's the term, because I do see more and more content being placed online for people in your industry and probably not not just stewardesses but probably just boat owners and people who are just starting to understand that they can make an impact. So you have inspired others as well in that story. Well I hope so I mean it's really great making a difference and it you know it makes me feel good about myself doing it in my own personal capacity so I'm really grateful that people are coming on board because it's making them feel good as well and it's doing good on a larger scale Yes. So many of us think that, especially in the boating industry, which has a reputation whether you own a small boat or a large super yacht that it is for the luxury, you know, the uber rich as you mentioned and they don't care but boaters do care they are the ones that actually see what's going on. Did you meet any resistance or challenges in trying to implement some of the things that you wanted to do to reduce the impact on the environment? Yeah well that's a very interesting question all the way through from you know resistance from boat owners to resistance from crew and you really know when you implement systems on board a boat it's it's really a slow process. I mean anything that's implemented very quickly and takes people by surprise often the first reaction is resistance. For crew the biggest bit of resistance that we get onboard boats that I consult on and the boats I join is the water bottles - so your your refillable water bottles and fitting in that filtration system in the crew mess at the pantry sink at the galley sink so that crew need to just refill their bottles instead of us buying plastic water bottles - store bought. But it's a very understandable resistance because a lot of the yachts and not all yachts most yachts do definitely do their due diligence when it comes to their maintenance systems with regards to their water filtration and their tanks but it's crew safety first that's always their number-one priority and it's very important whenever I implement these systems onboard a boat I make sure I get in touch with the engineers and I make sure that they're doing their regular water tank maintenance and checks and tests and that that tank is safe and the water is a good quality and then we put the filtration system right at the end of the line so it's gone through all the plumbing work and it's the last last frontier that it's filtered so you're ensuring the crew is getting that good water. But crew will be skeptical because you know not everybody does their job 100% and that's okay because crew safety comes first. So soon as that's taking care of crew a lot happier! And then when it comes to other aspects I mean when we're talking about guest resistance I think the main resistance comes with the price tag and a couple of years ago when I started this these eco alternatives they were pretty expensive to be to be very honest and they're actually they've become quite cost-effective they're becoming very competitive with the similar range of products that are not organic not eco-friendly not biodegradable so everything's coming in line now so there's a less resistant from a budget perspective but I always tell my clients, my chief stews, the captains onboard, you can never force the owners to do something they're not comfortable with if they want their Molten Brown crude oil I mean guess toiletry or their there Hermes or Bulgari that's okay because you can still get the crew to take the more eco-friendly route even if the guests want to stay with their choice that's their choice and to be honest all of the luxurious brands they're definitely going to come around to the eco-friendly alternative as well it's going that way which is very exciting and then they can keep their Hermes because it'll be organic and eco-riendly. I have found just in my brief time trying to implement these systems on my own boat is that I'm always surprised when you share with somebody what the issue is and what the solution is and what the impact could be - I'm surprised actually how positive people take it. They go 'oh I didn't know' and I think that's part of it - is just educating. That's probably part of your role is you meet resistance which is fair enough because people aren't you know naturally in their own habits but education must be a big part of you convincing them to change some of those behaviors which have little behaviors but a big impact. Yeah absolutely I mean when I work with management companies I completely offer my services and going onboard and talking it through with the crew kind of giving them a bit more information so that they have a bit more understanding they can relate to why it's necessary and it's a big part of it is also changing old habits and and getting into new habits which can can be very tough it can be super challenging to drop old habits and that I was very surprised every time I go onboard a boat I mean 90% of the crew are all for the implementation of eco-friendly products and always be a small percentage that resist it. But on a whole it's great to see the feedback great to see positive steps being taken. I was just in Barcelona Benetti Yacht's invited me to be a speaker at their annual yacht-master event and it was so well received lots of great feedback lots of very positive interaction some good questions and a couple of lightbulb moments and it kind of felt like we were all coming together and we understood why and where we need to go with sustainable yachting. You know that's so encouraging to hear because again our perception on the outside of these super yachts is these folks don't care that's really encouraging! If there was a boat owner of a large yacht, superyacht and they were looking to move in this direction where would you start?
