- Are There Environmentally Friendly Alternatives To Harsh Detergents For Cleaning My Boat?: Kind: captions Language: en Are there environmentally friendly alternatives to harsh detergents for cleaning my boat? If you are a boat owner, you might be wondering how to keep your vessel clean without harming the environment. The good news is there are plenty of eco-friendly alternatives to harsh detergents. These options not only help maintain your boat, but also play a role in oil spill prevention and sustainable boating practices. Harsh detergents can be harmful to marine life and contribute to water pollution. Therefore, choosing environmentally friendly cleaning products is essential. Eco-friendly boat cleaners have some key features that make them a better choice. First, they are non-toxic, meaning they do not contain harmful chemicals that can hurt aquatic life. Second, they are biodegradable, which means they break down naturally in the environment, reducing long-term pollution. Lastly, look for products that are certified by the Environmental Protection Agency as these meet specific Environmental Standards. Some recommended eco-friendly cleaners include Starbrite Sea Safe Biodegradable Boat Wash Soap. This concentrated soap is safe to use near water and effectively cleans various marine surfaces without leaving residues. Another great option is Better Boat Soap, which is also biodegradable and works well in both fresh and salt water. It is effective for cleaning holes, decks, and other surfaces on your boat. You might also consider 3003 multi-surface cleaner, known for its versatility across different surfaces. If you prefer homemade solutions, you can create effective cleaners right at home. A simple vinegar and water solution can help remove dirt and mold. For tougher stains, mix baking soda with vinegar to create a paste that works wonders. Incorporating these eco-friendly cleaning practices into your boating routine helps protect the environment and aligns with sustainable boating practices. By choosing these alternatives, you contribute to preventing pollution and maintaining healthy marine ecosystems. Plus, these cleaners are often gentle on boat surfaces, ensuring your vessel remains in good condition while minimizing environmental impact. By adopting these eco-friendly cleaning methods, you can enjoy your time on the water while supporting a cleaner, healthier marine environment. So, next time you clean your boat, think about the impact of your choices and opt for products that are kind to our oceans.

- The Shocking Truths of Yacht Ownership: Kind: captions Language: en-US Yacht ownership conjures images of luxury and freedom, but the reality is far more complicated than most people expect. Let’s uncover the surprising truths that come with owning a yacht. The purchase price is just the beginning. Routine maintenance, dock fees, insurance, and crew salaries can add hundreds of thousands of dollars every year. Even minor repairs, like fixing faulty wiring or updating navigation systems, can lead to unexpected and expensive service calls. Many owners find themselves spending more on upkeep than on actual enjoyment. Yachts lose value quickly, sometimes dropping by twenty percent in the first year alone. Over a decade, depreciation can be staggering, especially if the vessel is not meticulously maintained. Despite the investment, many yachts spend more time docked than cruising, with owners often managing only a handful of outings each year. This means the cost per hour of enjoyment can be shockingly high. Owning a yacht requires year-round attention. From hull cleaning to engine servicing, the work never stops. Legal complexities, like choosing a flag state and navigating taxes or regulations, add another layer of challenge and expense. While the prestige and unique experiences are real, so are the demands. Preparation and realistic expectations are essential before setting sail.

- Sailboat and Yacht Builders are Are Avoiding Teak Decks: Kind: captions Language: en Yachts and Teik decks have long gone together. Teik's rich golden wood is incredibly durable, saltresistant, and rotroof, which made it the gold standard for luxury yacht decking. But today, that romance is crumbling. High-end builders and owners are waking up to a hard truth. Most old growth teik, especially the finest Burmese teik, is now a legal and environmental headache. Because of the sanctions, once lucrative teak decks have become legal landmines, we've seen major fines and court cases. In late 2024, Dutch prosecutors slapped Ocean Co. builder of Bezos's yacht Koru with about €150,000, approximately $157,000 in fines for using teak with opaque origins. In the UK, Sunseeker was hit with £240,000 for importing Myanmar Teik without proper due diligence. And the US market isn't innocent. A recent Guardian investigation found US companies quietly importing conflict teak despite the Lacy Act ban. The heart of the problem is Myanmar's old growth teak. It's supremely durable, warm-looking, and easy on bare feet. Exactly why captains prized it. But over decades, Myanmar's military regimes have milked teak sales to fund themselves with almost no regard for the environment or human rights. After the 2021 coup, Western governments imposed sanctions on Myanmar's state timber firms. >> Uh almost immediately after the coup, the EU and the US pass sanctions against the import of Minamar Teik. Now Minamar or Burmatique as we know it is the finest teik used for furniture and used for ship decking. These sanctions banned import of all teik from Myamar. This story is a deeprooted investigation into how this teik find this way still to Europe, America and all over the world despite the sanctions. >> From that point on, all teik from Myanmar, often called Burmese teik became effectively illegal to import into the US, UK or EU. Any teik still floating around these markets must be old stock or it's being smuggled through loopholes, for instance, relabeled as plantation teak from other countries. Investigations have found traders using intermediaries in Singapore, Thailand, and elsewhere to sidestep the bans. The environmental toll is severe. Myanmar's teak trade has been called blood teik or conflict teik, a luxury good tied to ethnic cleansing and environmental destruction. The environmental investigation agency warns that at the current rate of logging, Myanmar will lose its forests by 2035. Boat design firms are now sounding the alarm. Even planting more teak trees isn't fast enough. A teak plantation takes 30 to 50 years to mature and the wood lacks the tight grain of ancient trees. In short, the ecology is fragile and the old growth supply is irreplaceable in our lifetimes. As the teak supply tightens, yacht builders are feeling the pinch. Reports say that Sunref Yachts Poland/ Dubai has now banned teak entirely, switching to thermally modified woods and other materials across its fleet. They say their secret sauce of heat treated woods performs just as well and needs less upkeep. High-profile owners are experimenting, too. For example, Google's co-founder Sergey Brin decked his new 142m super yacht entirely in tassumo, a German developed engineered wood instead of traditional teak. Yacht builders are now exploring a menagerie of deck materials. Each has pros and cons, but all aim to balance durability, grip, appearance, and sustainability. Key options include plantation or ethically sourced teak. Teik grown on managed plantations in India, Indonesia, Costa Rica, etc., and certified by bodies like FSC can still be used. These teak trees are younger, often approximately 30 to 50 years old and produce smaller, narrower boards. The wood is good, but not as buttery smooth or long-lasting as old growth teak. Plus, expanding plantations can sometimes harm rainforests if done irresponsibly. In practice, ethically sourced teak, is in very short supply. Thermally modified woods, engineered teak, fast growing timbers, often pine or African hardwoods, are heated and treated to mimic teak's durability and stability. Essentially, what it does is it dries out the sugars in the wood and it collapses the wood cells, and it doesn't allow the wood to contain and hold moisture anymore. So, it makes the wood last a lot longer, which is real important to um increase the life cycle of any any wood. >> Tumo is a prime example. It uses a quick growing African tree, then applies heat, resin infusion, and drying so the boards behave like teik on Sergey Brin's yacht. Tumo reportedly meets or even exceeds teik's performance while being traceable and free of Myanmar links. Another is a koa, a Dutch product that catalyzes soft wood with acetic anhydide, a kind of industrial vinegar. This changes the wood's molecules so it won't soak up water or rot. Aoya decks can last 25 to 50 years and are fully recyclable. Advanced synthetic composits. Fully artificial decks have come a long way. Etheet material is now widely used on high-end yachts. It's made from biioaterials, no PVC or real wood. is 8 mm thick and doesn't absorb water or get greasy. Essex stays cooler than teik in the sun and can even be recycled or retopped for a second life. Other manufacturers make PVC or polyurethane decking like Hercuin designed to look like teak. These can come very close in appearance and grip, though some owners worry about a plastic feel. Cork decking. A surprising underdog is cork. Yes, the same material used for wine stoppers. Cork decking brands include Marine Deck 2000. Sea Cork Aqua Cork is lightweight, naturally waterproof, and never gets hot underfoot. Cork trees, mainly in Portugal/ Spain, can be harvested every 8 to 9 years without cutting the tree down. On boats, cork is often ground into pellets and bonded into flexible sheets or tiles. It's grippy, easy to repair or replace, and feels softer under boots. Luxury tenders and refits are increasingly using cork, especially in areas like cockpits or fly bridges where heat is an issue. Green teak laminate. This is a crafty reuse of leftover timber. Wood that's too knotted or imperfect for conventional decking can be sliced into thin veneers, layered and glued together. The result is a laminated board that from above looks almost identical to vertical grain teak. Its fibers run perpendicular to the plank, so it's extremely stable. One yacht maker reports having entire 100 meters plus decks built with this green teak veneer. Exotic hardwoods. Certain tropical hardwoods resist rot and termites naturally. African iOart are sometimes cited. They can be nearly as hard and water friendly as teik. Ioko often called African teik though unrelated ages to a rich copper color and green heart is incredibly dense. The downside, they can be very difficult to work with, blunting tools quickly, and Greenheart especially can splinter. Greenheart is excellent for holes and docks. Shackleton's Endurance was sheathed in it, but it's heavy and not ideal for yacht decks. Oko is easier to use and is found on some classic tall ship decks, but it's not immune to the same supply and certification issues as teik. Each alternative comes with trade-offs. A composite deck can look nearly perfect, but it feels different. A sustainable wood like a koa is eco-friendly but may look paler. Cork is soft and ecooitive but has a very different texture. What all the above share is that they don't tie back to illegal logging in Myanmar and they free the industry from a depleting and dangerous resource. For boat owners and designers today, this means the teak debate isn't theoretical. It affects projects now. If you're building or refitting, you might not even be able to order Myanmar teik. Any supplier should be ready to prove a clear supply chain via FSC, DNA tests, and documentation for any teak sold as legal. Expect prices to stay high. Old growth teak in Europe was still around €30,000 per cubic meter in 2023, and plantation teak only slightly cheaper. The industry consensus is that true Burmese teik is effectively gone from the market. On the plus side, this challenge has driven rapid innovation. Some owners have even turned it into a badge of honor, like Sergey Brin showcasing Tumo on a mega yacht or boutique builders offering no teak packages. Early adopters of alternatives report that many guests can't even tell the difference, especially when the pattern is right. Designers now often present the alternative as a premium choice with its own advantages. Better grip, cooler in sun, or eco-friendly bragging rights. In short, boat owners should start thinking beyond teak now. Ask your yard or joinery about these options. See, for example, a Kebony treated pine deck used by some European yards or an aesthetic composite sample. Feel it underfoot. You may find you like the alternatives as much as the real thing, or at least understand why going all teak is increasingly untenable. Ultimately, the yacht industry is evolving. Teik's golden era is the only decking material is ending. It's being replaced not by one silver bullet, but a toolbox of solutions. Each boat will choose what fits its style. Some may mix a little ethical plantation teak with synthetic trim. Others may go fully synthetic or cork. What's clear is that standing pat isn't an option. As one industry insider puts it, "We do have to acknowledge that decks are working surfaces. Alternative materials can work and it's up to designers and owners to embrace a different look." The silver lining. This shift could actually yield better decks in some ways. Deck materials like esthetic can stay cleaner and require less sealing. Thermally treated woods can be milled in lengths that make use of smaller logs efficiently. Cork decks stay cooler and softer and we all benefit from not driving deforestation. As awareness grows in boardrooms, on forum threads, or at boat shows, the teak taboo is loosening. Expect more consensus and market availability of these substitutes in the next few years. By the end of this decade, a no teak super yacht may be commonplace rather than odd. For now, boat owners should educate themselves and discuss alternatives early. The era of risk-taking on teak decks is fading. Going green or at least greener is the new norm.

- 7 common items that are ILLEGAL to have on a boat in 2026: Kind: captions Language: en Have you ever wondered if a small detail forgotten on your boat could turn a peaceful trip into a legal nightmare? The scary part isn't the obvious dangers, but rather items so familiar you'd never suspect them. And the irony is that many people only realize their mistake when it's too late. So, what are the things that seem harmless but could cost you dearly? Let's get started right away with the top seven. Top seven, drones. Drones have become an essential part of the sea loving community, especially when everyone wants to capture spectacular moments from above. With just a short flight, you can turn your journey into a mesmerizing film. But behind that convenience is a complex system of regulations that not everyone fully understands. There are areas where drone flight is strictly restricted, particularly near ports, crowded shipping lanes, or protected zones. Notably, regulations can change over a very short distance, legal in one spot, but completely prohibited just a short way. In some cases, you don't even need to use the drone. Just carrying it into a restricted area is enough to cause trouble. And the question is, before bringing this device, are you truly certain you're in a place where it's allowed to be used? Top six invalid marine radios. A marine radio is not just a communication device, but also the lifeline connecting you to safety while offshore. But what many people don't realize is that owning a radio doesn't mean you're using it legally. A very common error is a device that isn't registered or lacks an MMSI identification code, which helps identify the boat within the communication system. Without this code or by using the wrong one, your device could be seen as operating under a false identity. More dangerously, some devices require separate licenses, even if you don't use them at all. During an inspection, simply detecting an invalid device can lead to trouble. Think of an emergency where you need to send a distress signal, but the device isn't recognized. Will you still have a chance for timely assistance then? Top five, expired flares. In the most critical moments, a flare might be the only thing that gets you spotted in the vast ocean. It's not just a device, it's hope. Unfortunately, many people accidentally turn it into a hazard by keeping expired flares. There's a common mindset that something is better than nothing. But the reality is quite the opposite. Expired flares are no longer considered valid safety equipment and in many cases are seen as a violation. Over time, they can become unstable, fail to burn correctly, or worse, explode uncontrollably. In the eyes of an inspector, it's not a backup plan. It's a potential risk, and that can cause you to fail an inspection or be fined. So, how long has it been since you checked these supposedly vital devices? Top four, high power laser pointers. A tiny laser pointer might look so harmless that you'd never think it could cause a problem. Many people carry them for signaling, observation, or simply for convenience. But behind that appearance is a completely different story. High power laser devices have caused many serious incidents, especially when misused. Because of this, they are very strictly controlled. The problem lies in the fact that many products on the market don't clearly state their power level or lack safety certification, leaving buyers unaware they own a device that exceeds legal limits. And during an inspection, finding such a laser can leave you with a lot of explaining to do, even if you've never used it. Are you sure that little item is really as harmless as you think? Top three undocumented medications. A first aid kit is essential on any boat, and most people prepare thoroughly for the worst scenarios. But few realize that those very pills can become a source of trouble. Not all medications are allowed everywhere, and some that are legal in one place are strictly controlled in another. When medication lacks clear labeling or doesn't come with a valid prescription, it can be viewed as a controlled substance. Many people keep old medication without noticing that over time, proving its origin becomes nearly impossible. During an inspection, just one ambiguous bottle of pills is enough to put you in an awkward situation. When asked what it is, how will you explain it to convince the inspector? Top two, direct overboard discharge systems. There are things on a boat you almost never notice until they cause a problem, and the sanitation system is one of them. Some boats are still equipped with systems that discharge directly into the water, which was accepted in the past, but is now strictly regulated. Many areas now completely ban direct discharge and notably even if you don't use this function, its existence can be considered a violation if not properly locked. A diverter valve that isn't secured is enough for the system to be deemed operational during an inspection. This can lead to unwanted consequences like fines or forced system modifications. Are you sure you fully understand how the system operates on your boat? Top one, non-compliant fuel containers. Carrying spare fuel is a common habit, especially for those who want to ensure an uninterrupted journey. But what seems like a precaution can become a risk if you use the wrong type of container. Many people still use standard plastic cans designed for land environments. However, the marine environment is completely different. With temperatures, humidity, and constant vibration that can cause these containers to degrade quickly, they can leak gas fumes, crack, or create fire hazards you don't even notice. Meanwhile, marine rated containers are designed to withstand these harsh conditions. During an inspection, finding a non-compliant container can be seen as a serious danger. And with just a small spark, the consequences could far exceed what you imagine. Is saving a little money really worth trading the safety of your entire trip? The sea brings a sense of freedom, but freedom always comes with responsibility. Sometimes just a small detail you overlook can ruin an entire trip. So take the time to recheck everything on your boat before it's too late. If you find this content helpful, don't forget to like and subscribe to the channel so you don't miss future videos where you can discover many more things you never thought of before.

4. High-power laser pointers
5. undocumented medications
6. direct overboard discharge systems
7. non-compliant fuel containers.

- Why Half the Boats Built Between 2020 and 2022 Are Already Back on the Market: Kind: captions Language: en Pull up Boat Trader right now, filter for model years 2020, 2021, and 2022. What you are going to see is something the marine industry is not talking about openly, listings by the thousands from owners who bought these boats brand new. Most with under 200 hours, many still carrying the dealer sticker in the glove box. Barely used boats from people who are completely done with them. Here is the number that changes everything. Between 2020 and 2021, over 800,000 first-time boat buyers entered the US market according to the National Marine Manufacturers Association. Nearly 1 million people who had never owned a boat before bought one anyway. On impulse. On low-rate financing. On a lifestyle they had never actually lived. Now those boats are back. And what is sitting in that used inventory right now is the most important thing you need to understand before you write a single check. It is four separate forces that hit simultaneously and created the most predictable exit pipeline the recreational marine market has ever produced. Stay with us because force number four is the one nobody sees coming until the bill is already in their hands. The first force is the one that was always going to catch up. When the pandemic hit in 2020, the Federal Reserve dropped rates to historic lows. Boat loan rates followed. For a brief window, buyers could finance a boat below 4%. Monthly payments felt manageable. Dealers were selling boats before they arrived on the lot. There were six-month waiting lists. >> [music] >> Buyers paid above sticker. The market was completely disconnected from economic reality and every first-time buyer who stepped into a showroom in 2020 or 2021 made their decision during that window. >> Then the window closed. By 2024, the average 60-month boat loan had climbed to 8.4% according to LendingTree. On a $150,000 boat financed at 4%, a new buyer now faces a monthly payment hundreds of dollars higher for the same asset. The pool of buyers who can absorb that payment shrank [music] dramatically. Sellers who bought at the peak on low-rate financing >> are now competing for buyers operating in a completely different cost reality. >> The exit math runs in both directions. Carry the boat and pay for it or sell it and take the [music] loss. Most are choosing to sell. That is what the inventory you are looking at right now actually is. Here's the second force and this is the one dealers are least interested in discussing before the transaction closes. The purchase price of the boat is not the cost of the boat. The annual operating cost is. And for the specific buyer profile that drove the pandemic surge, >> that number arrived as a complete shock in year one. The marine industry's documented benchmark is the 10% rule. >> Budget 10% of your boat's purchase price every single year for maintenance. center console, >> that is $15,000 per year. Before fuel, before slip fees, before insurance, before anything breaks. [music] Add a coastal marina slip at $3,000 to $8,000 annually, twin outboard service $1,000 to $1,500 every 100 hours, annual bottom paint, and insurance. The all-in annual number on a mid-size saltwater boat crosses $30,000 a year before a single unscheduled repair occurs. The NMMA documented that the average American boat is used just [music] 54 days per year. For the pandemic buyer who used their boat 20 days in year one and 12 in year two, that annual cost divided by actual use days became a number that made selling not just logical, but urgent. The boats flooding the used market right now are not the result of mechanical failure. They are the result of owners doing the cost per use math for the second time after the purchase and arriving at a number they cannot justify. This is the force most buyers have no idea happened. And it matters because it tells you something critical about the quality of the specific boats you are shopping right now. Between 2020 and 2022, mid-tier manufacturers raised production targets by 30 to 45% to meet pandemic demand. Plants hired workers with no boat building background, compressed training timelines, and shipped product on schedules driven by dealer order pressure rather than quality verification. What came out looked identical to boats built under normal conditions. Same gel coat, same hardware, same electronics. The differences were hidden inside the laminate, behind liner panels, at stringer connection points, and in bilge areas no buyer examines on a standard walk-around. The aftermath was not subtle. Brunswick Corporation, parent of Mercury Marine, saw revenue collapse by $1.16 billion in 2024 compared to 2023. Mercury permanently laid off nearly 300 workers in June of 2024, then furloughed another 1,700 through year end. Bass Pro's White River Marine Group cut over 170 workers across multiple plants in the same period. These companies had overbuilt for a market that evaporated. The inventory produced during that production surge is exactly what the used market is full of right now. >> A pre-purchase inspection protocol for any boat from that window is not optional. If you want to avoid costly mistakes most boaters make, be sure to check the description for our cheap guide that will save you thousands and subscribe. This is the one nobody in the marine industry wants to say out loud, >> and it is the real reason the exit pipeline is as large as it is. A significant portion of the 800,000 first-time buyers who entered the market in 2020 and 2021 bought boats that were not matched to the water they actually had access to or the lifestyle they actually lived. The buyer who purchased a 32-ft offshore center console discovered their real use pattern, family day trips and protected bay fishing, was better served by a platform a third the size at a fraction of the operating cost. The wake boat buyer discovered their local lake had a no wake ordinance most of the season. The cruiser buyer discovered the cabin added complexity and maintenance without adding days on the water. A Boat Trader survey found that a significant number of younger private sellers, [music] those who bought within the last 1 to 3 years, were actively listing their boats >> with millennials representing 49% of that group. >> These are not people who ran out of money. They are people who ran out of the lifestyle they believed they were purchasing. The mismatch between the boat purchased and the water the buyer actually uses is the emotional engine that drives the exit decision [music] before the financial forces even finish their work. And right now, both are running simultaneously [music] on nearly a million boats that entered the market in a two-year window. Here is what all four forces add up to if you are a buyer approaching this market with your eyes open. The opportunity is real. Boats with under 200 hours, documented service records, >> and clean surveys are sitting at prices that reflect genuine seller motivation, not mechanical distress. >> The Denison Yacht Sales 2026 Market Report confirmed that North American average sold prices rose in 2025, meaning quality boats with honest pricing are still moving. >> The ones sitting for 6 months at the same listing price are overpriced, undermaintained, or both. You want the first kind. This market has more of them than any point in the last decade. But this market requires a specific inspection protocol. Run a moisture meter across the full hull bottom and transom before anything else. Readings above 12% require a certified marine surveyor before any offer. Drain the lower unit oil. Milky or gray-white oil means water has entered through a failed shaft seal, and the conversation stops there. Pull the engine cowling and trace the wiring for green oxidation at connector points on boats from the 2020 to 2022 production window. This is a documented pattern, not a theoretical concern. Check every anode on the hull. A depleted or missing anode is not an isolated maintenance item. It tells you exactly how every other service interval on that boat was treated. The boats are there, the prices are moving, the sellers need out, the buyers who did the work before the check cleared are getting the best deals in years. The ones who skipped the inspection found out afterward exactly why the price looked the way it did. Saltwater does not care about the hour meter reading or how recently the gelcoat was buffed. It has been working on that hull from the moment it entered the water.

The reality of it is that you are clearing up rooms that have just had sex parties, orgies, prostitutes. Coke residue on the tables the next day. Veal or livestock so fresh that it actually gets flown in on a helicopter, slaughtered on board, and served to the guests as fresh as can be. Unfortunately, it can sometimes get a bit seedy, a bit creepy with drunk people who think they can do whatever they want. These owners, these millionaires, these billionaires, they’re incredibly powerful people, and a lot of the time, they get their own way. Drug smuggling can go on in the industry. If people wanted to be moving drugs and illicit substances, I reckon it’d be pretty easy to do. It’s an incredibly secretive world which no one really knows much about. The guests just use super yachts as their little playground. They live in this little bubble where they think anything goes, anything can happen. [Informer] [Super Yacht Deckhand] These yachts are always pretty mad, particularly the larger ones. They will pretty much always contain a pool or some sort of hot tub, equipped with a gym. Some of them have cinemas. Some of them have their own spas, helidecks, and will store a helicopter on board. The cost of these yachts can exceed anywhere about half a billion pounds. I think some of the most expensive chartered yachts on the market at the moment can actually cost four million a week. You do always secretly know that the people you’re working for haven’t always done the best things in their lives. I’ve received job offers for the owner of a boat who is one of the most notoriously corrupt people on the planet. And during the job interview, the chief officer actually asked me am I morally OK with who this person is and what he’s done. And you kind of just have to ask yourself, “Am I OK with this?” The expectations of the guests are pretty much whatever they ask for, we can deliver to them. As the day tends to go on and the more alcohol that gets consumed, their expectations go up and our ability to deliver goes down. Sexual harassment definitely does go on in the industry. You do hear quite a few stories which make the hairs on the back of your neck stand up. There is a sexual element to the job, 100 percent. It’s a very superficial industry that a lot of boats will only hire on the way you look and what waist size you are or what dress size you are, et cetera. I think they can forget that you’re here for work, you’re not here to be violated in any way. The victims of these cases are paid off. They’re told to keep their mouths shut in order for their bonuses or perks. It gives the people this right to think that they can get away with anything. There have been a few serious incidents in super yachting that have gone wrong and actually led to fatalities. One of the most famous ones in the industry was actually an anchor chain coming loose. The chain span around in what’s called the mooring bay, which is the forward section of the boat called the bow. and actually took the leg off one of the officers, and unfortunately, he bled to death and died. Basically, you’ve just got to stay really switched on at all times, which can be a big part of the reason why working in the industry can make you so tired and run down. I did a pretty long stint working on a charter boat. It was nearly 130 days of season, back-to-back charters, and within that time, we only had one day off. At the end of that, when I actually left that boat, I felt like I was ready to leave the industry because it was just killing me, essentially. You can burn out really quickly. And you can look at guys who are, you know, they’ve been in yachting for ten years or so, and they look pretty weathered. Their skin’s destroyed by the sun. The lack of sleep has just f***ed them over. And it can really have quite a physical effect on your body, and it's pretty visible. The reason that we’re so busy is because obviously we only have a certain number of crew cabins on board. You’re always in a cabin for two, if not four on some bigger boats, and if not six on some super large ones. Cabin fever is definitely, definitely a real thing. You can feel cooped up like a caged animal sometimes, and personal space becomes a serious issue. Working in yachting definitely teaches you a lot of mental fortitude. You know, you work very long hours with little rest, and that teaches you how to be resilient and just kind of push on, push on through. Obviously, there are some negative effects on your mental health. I don’t really understand the mindset of a billionaire, but when a lot of money is involved, it changes people, and I think it always brings out the worst in a lot of people’s personalities.

For one of Russia's richest men and a former senator, relatively little is known about him. Mr Guryev was among a group of businessmen who took advantage of the collapse of the Soviet Union to acquire former state assets and make a vast fortune. His was in the fertiliser industry, setting up a company called PhosAgro. Mining phosphate rock for fertiliser proved hugely lucrative, propelling the company onto the London Stock Exchange. Igor Sychev once headed the company's tax department, but he fell out with Mr Guryev over a stake at the firm. Tell me about Mr Guryev. What's he like? He's a controversial person. He truly believes in God publicly and as it turned out, he loves luxury. The US government describes Mr Guryev as a known close associate of Vladimir Putin, one that has been living the high life. In 2008, Mr Guryev bought Wittenhurst House in North London. It's behind those rather imposing looking gates. This is as close as we can get. He took what was a dilapidated Georgian mansion and turned it into one of London's most expensive properties with a huge number of rooms, a swimming pool, a vast underground basement and parking for 25 cars. Like much of Moscow's oligarch elites, he was enjoying a quiet life here in London and then the invasion of Ukraine started. A missile strike on an airport As the news reverberates around the world, Vladimir Putin assembles the oligarchs Thirty-seven members of Russia's business elite that control key sectors of the economy. One of those in attendance is Andrey Guryev's son. Like many of the men in this room, he'll be sanctioned by the West along with his father, but not quite yet. In the Caribbean, the Alfa Nero turns its tracker off. The ship goes dark. As other oligarch-linked yachts scramble to get out of the reach of sanctions, the first big bust happens in northern Italy where a number are seized. This is one of the yachts that has been taken by European authorities. A second yacht, linked to Mr Guryev, leaves port in Livorno. Recently built, Luminosity is one of the biggest hybrid superyachts in the world, catering for 27 guests and 37 crew on its six decks. It makes its way around the boot of Italy and into non-European Union Montenegro, where it's safe from EU sanctions for now. As pressure mounts on more countries to condemn Russia's invasion, Montenegro's government ignores significant pro-Russian sentiment and adopts EU sanctions. A month after Luminosity arrives in Montenegro, an inspector boards and finds that it's owned by a company registered in Guernsey. Here a pattern emerges. The companies that own the palatial Wittenhurst estate and Alfa Nero are linked to the same address as Luminosity. And that address is here on the British Crown Dependency of Guernsey. It's a place known for beautiful beaches, a relaxed pace of life, and of course, offshore finance. That is the office of Opus Private. Now on their website, they said they help some of the world's wealthiest individuals to manage and protect their assets. Now normally that's being done under a veil of offshore secrecy, something that's rather harder with Russia's invasion of Ukraine. This company's house filing from May is for one of the Guryev family's UK mansions that we've traced back to Opus. In all, we've found half a billion pounds worth of assets and a relationship going back at least a decade. It is legal to continue to work for a sanctioned client with government permission, and court documents we've obtained from Antigua show Opus was involved in a failed legal action to stop the Alfa Nero being auctioned off. The judgement details how the ownership of the superyacht is tied up in a trust, which a judge in Antigua suggests Mr Guryev still exercises a degree of control over. In the latest challenge to the yacht's seizure, Mr Guryev's daughter is trying to argue that she is the sole beneficiary of the trust which owns Alfa Nero. When you are a very wealthy individual, you want to protect your wealth. So obviously you would trust your own relatives to take care of their wealth should something happen to you. And is that process completely legal? It is legal as long as the individual is not sanctioned. If the individual is later sanctioned and then decides to restructure their own wealth, that's when it's not legal and that becomes sanction evasion. When it comes to these Guryev family assets, including Alfa Nero, there's no way of us independently finding out when Mr Guryev's daughter became the sole beneficiary. Both Opus and Mr Guryev's daughter told us it happened before her father was sanctioned. Opus also insisted that Mr Guryev is not their client, as he is no longer a beneficiary of the trust, despite being connected to half a billion pounds worth of the family's assets. Knowing who really owns assets is essential if sanctions are to be effective. Complex ownership arrangements like the ones we've found make that much harder. This kind of cat and mouse game of using multi-jurisdictional approaches to try and hide what you're doing is a real challenge. And actually that's where diplomacy comes in, where the foreign office should be saying we expect more from you. The Guryevs both resigned their positions at PhosAgro, but despite the sanctions, the family remain major shareholders. The company has made record sales since the invasion of Ukraine. Guriyev Jr even met President Putin to discuss the fertiliser industry. It's led to calls from Kiev for the UK to go further. Vlad Vlasiuk is President Zelensky's sanctions advisor. He's overseen not just the freezing, but the seizing of assets, something their allies have largely failed to do. We have this aggression unleashed at us and we just have to protect ourselves with all the means available and we've been doing quite intensively this confiscation of the assets belonging to the Russian individuals. In all, they've sanctioned around 9,000 people and companies, including Mr Guryev and his son, but also his wife and daughter. Mr Guryev being one of the biggest players in the field of fertilisers, Mr Putin himself has been weaponising the fertiliser and causing a lot of threat to the whole of the world in terms of global food security and we just don't want that to happen. The UK government says sanctioned assets, like the Guriyev families, will remain frozen until Russia pays reparations. It's clear Russia must pay for the destruction that they've inflicted. So we're working with allies to explore lawful routes to use Russian assets. What would you like to see happen to the UK-based assets of Mr Guryev? I think that those assets should be confiscated and then transferred to Ukraine. The money would be very much used to rebuild Ukraine and that would also send a strong message A spokesman for Mr Guryev said there was no proper or legitimate basis for the sanctions against him. Opus Private told us trusts are legal structures and their use is lawful. Opus provides trust services to and for the benefit of the discretionary beneficiaries of the trust structure. It provides those trust services in compliance with all applicable laws. And that Opus has made the Guernsey and UK authorities aware of its ownership and control of trust assets, which it treats as frozen, in line with the UK sanctions against Mr Guryev. Despite being auctioned off, the Alfa Nero remains in Falmouth Harbour. Its fate disputed in the courts. It is a powerful symbol of how a sanctioned Russian oligarch can claim, with lawful British help, that the yacht and his family's other assets in the West are not really his at all.

couldn't afford to build my own boat at the time yach didn't seem like a good idea so the day after I graduated college I went down to Fort Lauderdale Florida did all my training got on a brand new stateof the our 80m charter yacht called excellent 6 all within 1 month over the next few years I traveled through the Mediterranean and the Caribbean taking care of high-profile guests like Bill Gates Chris Jenner and a lot of Russian billionaires you've probably never heard of I was even invited to be part of the hit reality TV show below de saling yacht which profiles the hidden dramas and lifestyles of the crew who work on these luxury Yachts I now own my own yacht which is crazy to say and I'm currently traveling around the world aboard it and I still take breaks to work in the yacht charter industry so this is one topic that I actually feel very qualified to help you with after being on the show the question that I get asked all the time is how do I get into the audient industry so today once and for all I'm going to answer that question and any other questions you may have or don't know that you're going to have and I'm going to run through a lot but just stick with me because it's actually super easy and you can do this so how do you actually do it the first thing you're going to need is your stcw it stands for standards of training and certificate of watchkeeping the international Maritime organization requires that all working seafarers have this training so you're going to need it so as soon as you finish watching this video book that course because it's your first step it's actually a really fun course it teaches you the basics of first aid and life saving you're going to learn CPR firefighting and you get to jump into a life raft wearing these sweet Gumby like survival suits news flash if you're in the middle of the ocean and there's a medical emergency or God forbid there's a fire you're the first responder and it's part of your job to be able to handle it the stcw certificate lasts for 5 years and after that you're going to need to do a refresher course the next step is to get yourg one this is basically a physical to ensure that you're in fit working condition to be aboard the Y it's nothing to worry about super easy exam the doctor's going to listen to your heart and lungs they're going to take your blood pressure they're going to ask you questions about your medical history including drinking and smoking tell them you're healthy and they'll take a urine sample to test for kidney function don't worry it's not a drug test the one thing that actually slips a lot of people up is the color blind test so before you spend any money on these qualifications find out if you're at all color blind cuz you might be surprised further education it should be noted that you don't need a college degree to get into the yachting industry but if you do have one it's probably going to help you get on a better boat because it just gives taking care of people as far as further yacht training to get your first job you don't need anything else but I do have some recommendations to help you make your chances better for de can you're going to want to get your powerboat level two well technically you're going to get level one and two it's a basic theoretical and practical training for how to maneuver power boats it only cost a couple hundred the course takes two days typically and it's actually a lot of fun I got to do mine in Fort Lauderdale and we actually drove a pretty sweet boat through the canals practiced docking and we tested on the rules of the road so to speak which way do you turn when there's a boat on a collision course with you what do these markers mean etc etc good yacht programs are going to want to know that you can operate the tender safely I've driven tenders worth over a million dollar filled with guests worth over a billion dollars so it's kind of a big deal this certificate is one way for you to help yourself stand out from the crowd when trying to get that first job and trust me you don't want to learn how to drive the tender for the first time in front of your whole crew for engineers you a week and is relatively easy to get through I've actually completed this course as a deck hand because a specific yacht wanted me to for the Manning requirements of the vessel and I can say it's a really fun course super easy too for steart s's the equivalent is the Silver Service courses you don't need them to get a job but having the certificate will help you get a better job faster Silver Service 1 and2 is super affordable and it only takes a couple days to complete it'll teach you about how to deliver high-end service to your guest you'll cover topics like the terminology me and plus how to set up a Beach barbecue table decorations religious considerations when serving and a lot more even if you've worked in a high-end restaurant you're coming from the service industry these courses are still a really good idea and you're less likely to piss off your Chief stew once you get to that first job I can also recommend the course for basic housekeeping and laundry trust me it's rare and more difficult to land a job on when you're first getting started out most Charter Yachts operate two seasons one in the Mediterranean between May and October and one in the Caribbean between November and April that being said although there's lots of places to do your training the best three places are Fort Lauderdale Florida on tebs France and Palma Spain these three locations are going to house everything you need from training facilities to crew houses to crew agencies and lots of busy Charter Yachts who are always hiring where you go for your training is obviously going to depend on your current location your passport limitations price and which Charter season is approaching but I can tell you from my own experience that Fort Lauderdale Florida is an amazing place to land a HMC Maritime clinic to get your eg1 it's the only place visas if by chance you are American congratulations you have landed the golden ticket passport in yachting don't waste it otherwise please look to need to be a little bit honest with yourself you see there's two types of people who get into the yting industry those who want to travel and make a lot of money and work their butts off and those people who have seen below deck and just want to have a big party as a general rule of thumb motor Yachts have bigger Crews and probably stricter rules than sailing Yachts but every boat is different if you work on a 55- ft catamaran in the bbis you're probably going to have a crew of two to three you're going to Charter frequently work your butt off probably make pretty good money but you're also going to have more relaxed rules and lifestyle because there just not as much of a hierarchy there's not as much of a crew to enforce those rules if you work on an 80 M super yacht you're going to Charter a little less frequently still working all the time but you're going to have a crew of 25 to 40 people maybe you're going to make a lot of freaking money but there's going to be a lot of rules and it's very unlikely that you're going to be allowed to party it up in the hot tub when the guest off you see there's tradeoffs a crew of 30 means if one person is a complete casserole then it's not so hard to live with them because there's so many people around but if there's a crew of six and you don't get along with someone it's going to ruin the job for you in a smaller crew you're going to have more responsibility and freedom you're going to do everything whether that's a good thing or not is up to you and up to your personality on a big boat with a crew of 30 people or 100 people you're going to have a more compartmentalized job on some of the big Yachts a deck hand might only be responsible for cleaning one deck like main deck aft and there's definitely going to be a stewards who's only in charge of laundry sometimes two one piece of blanket advice I can give though is don't work on a private yacht private yachts work just as hard as Charter Yachts most of the time but on as much so there's more of a relaxed schedule and there's more of this like family kind of environment so that might be for you if you still want to have some kind of landbased schedule or maybe to 3500 a month but if you get on a good boat it can be a lot more it should be said that it depends on where the boat is if you are yachting in Australia you're not going to get paid as much as if you are yachting in the Mediterranean or the Caribbean it's just a fact my starting salary as a junior deckhand with no experience was 4500 a month with a promise that every 6 months we would get a $500 raise so after a year it was 5,500 a month additionally chter tips are usually about 20% of the charter price of the yacht split evenly throughout the crew that means the deck hand and the stewards get the exact same share as the captain the chief stew the first mate to give an example I once did a 12-day charter in St Barts and everyone on the crew was tipped1 13,300 and we were all given a gift Midway through the chter I was literally told to run ashore and spend $3,500 as quickly as I could as a Christmas gift from the chter guest this was tiered so the chief stew and the first officer got way bigger gifts than me but it was still pretty awesome I got a Caribbean exclusive tag watch by the way and the chief officer got got a lot of Louis Vuitton it was freaking awesome so - Inside The $200M Shipyard Kickback Scandal Bankrupting Today's Independent Yacht Designers: Kind: captions Language: en The yacht looked perfect. 62 meters of handlaid carbon fiber. A hull so flawless it reflected the Mediterranean sky like a mirror. Inside floors of fossilized wood, ceilings handstitched in Italian cashmere. A wine seller stocked with bottles that cost more than most people's cars. The owner paid $47 million for it. He never knew that 2 million of that went to a man he never met. A man who did nothing, who built nothing, who simply picked up the phone and made sure the right name got the design contract. That's not a fee. That's a tax, a secret tax paid by designers who couldn't afford to say no. And right now, that tax is bankrupting the most talented yacht designers in the world. We've traced the paper trail, $200 million, hidden inside buried in shell payments, disguised as consultancy fees, and market access premiums. And by the end of this video, you're going to understand exactly how this scheme works, who's running it, why almost nobody talks about it, and why one designer who did talk about it never worked again. The super yacht industry sells a fantasy. Freedom, exclusivity, craftsmanship, no limits. But behind the glossy renders and the Monaco boat shows, there is a system, a ruthless, invisible system. And it has one purpose, to control who builds what, for whom, and at what cost. This is the story of the kickback economy. The shadow market that runs parallel to every major yacht construction contract on Earth. And it's bigger than anyone wants to admit. >> How the scheme works. Here is something the industry does not want you to understand. When a client commissions a superyacht, they hire an independent designer, an architect, someone with a vision, someone who spent decades perfecting their craft. That designer then selects a shipyard. Or at least that's how it's supposed to work. In reality, the selection was already made before the first sketch, before the first meeting. The shipyard had already paid to be at the top of the list. The mechanism is elegant in its corruption. Major European shipyards, names you would recognize, names with century old legacies on their letterheads, demand what insiders call a placement premium, a commission, typically 10% of the total design contract value, paid in cash or funneled through a third-party consulting firm that exists for no other purpose. 10% sounds modest until you run the math. On a $40 million build, the designer's fee might be $2.5 million. 10% of that is $250,000 gone before a single hour of work, before a single drawing is produced. Now, multiply that across dozens of projects, dozens of shipyards, dozens of years. That is how you get to $200 million, and the designers pay it because the alternative is worse. Think about that for a second. You spend 20 years building a reputation. You win awards. You get written about in architectural journals. Your work gets displayed in museums. And then you discover that access to the most prestigious construction facilities in the world costs a secret fee you're not allowed to tell your client about. What would you do? Most designers do exactly what the shipyards expect. They pay. They pad the invoice. They add a line item labeled something vague. procurement coordination, technical leaison support and they absorb a loss or they pass it quietly to the client but some didn't and what happened to them is the part of this story that should make everyone uncomfortable. The blacklist her name is not important. What matters is her reputation. 15 years designing vessels that won international awards. Work displayed in design retrospectives from Milan to Dubai. a client list that read like a Forbes register. In 2023, she was approached by a senior representative from one of the largest shipyard groups in Northern Europe. The conversation was brief. The expectation was clear. 10% paid to a consulting company registered in Cyprus. Standard practice. Everyone does it. She said no. 6 months later, she couldn't get a call returned. Her studio of 11 people is now down to three. Two major clients cited production concerns and moved their projects to a different designer. A designer who her former colleagues say has a very good relationship with that same Northern European shipyard group. She didn't talk publicly for almost a year. When she finally did, she told a maritime trade journalist one sentence that got buried in paragraph 14 of a 4,000word article. She said, "They don't need to fire you. They just need to make sure no one hires you. This is how a blacklist operates in a world without an official blacklist. Here's a statistic that the industry's own internal surveys do not publish. Between 2018 and 2024, the number of independent yacht design studios with fewer than 15 employees dropped by 31% in Western Europe. The large vertically integrated firms, the ones with ownership ties to shipyard holding companies, grew by 19% in the same period. This is not a coincidence. This is the market being restructured one kickback at a time. The 2026 landscape. We are recording this at a specific moment in history. The superyacht order book for 2026 is the largest it has ever been. Demand is not the problem. There are more ultra high netw worth clients commissioning bespoke vessels than at any point in the industry's recorded history. And yet, the design community is shrinking. Why? Because the economics of independent practice are becoming impossible. When a boutique studio absorbs a 10% kickback, they are not just losing money on that project. They are losing the ability to reinvest, to hire the next generation of designers, to develop new materials, new engineering approaches, new construction techniques. The shipyards, meanwhile, are consolidating. Three major European groups now control access to 62% of all construction slipways capable of handling vessels over 50 m. If you are an independent designer and those three groups decide they don't like you or more precisely decide that a designer who does pay their premium is a better use of their dry dock, you are finished. Not officially, never officially, just quietly, systemically completely finished. This is what the industry calls market pressure. What it actually is is extortion with excellent PR. Now, here is the part nobody expected. The kickback system didn't start with corruption. It started with survival. In the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis, the superyacht industry collapsed. Orders evaporated overnight. Shipyards that had expanded aggressively were suddenly carrying massive debt loads with empty order books. Some of them did not survive. But the ones that did learn something about power. They learned that designers needed them more than they needed designers. that the client relationship was asymmetric, that the physical infrastructure, the cranes, the slipways, the skilled labor pools was scarce and irreplaceable. And someone somewhere made a calculation. If access is scarce, access is a price. What began as an informal relationship fee between old industry contacts became over 15 years a structured, expected, quietly enforced financial obligation. The corruption didn't corrupt the industry. The desperation did. And by the time the money returned, the mechanism was already too profitable to dismantle. Here is the question that no one in this industry will answer on the record. Does a client know? In some cases, almost certainly yes. Ultra high netw worth individuals who commission multiple vessels over a lifetime eventually learn how the system works. Some accept it. Some even participate in structuring payments to preferred suppliers. But for firsttime buyers, for new wealth entering this market from outside traditional circles, they are paying for something they cannot see, cannot audit, and were never told existed. A $200 million shadow economy hidden inside the invoices of the most luxurious objects on Earth. The designers who are fighting back are doing so carefully. Some have begun organizing informally, deliberately off the record, using encrypted channels, meeting at secondary events away from the mainboat shows, building documentation of what's happening. They are not going to press. Not yet. Because a press that covers this industry is largely funded by the advertising budgets of the same shipyards. They would need to investigate. They are not going to regulators because maritime construction sits in a jurisdictional gray zone that makes crossber financial enforcement nearly impossible particularly when payments route through Cyprus, Malta and the aisle of man. What they are doing is building a case and what happens when that case is ready. When the documentation is dense enough, the source is numerous enough, the evidence undeniable enough is the moment this industry faces a reckoning it has been avoiding for 15 years. Because here's the truth that the shipyard groups do not want spoken out loud. The designers are not powerless. The clients are not powerless. The system only works because the people inside it believe it cannot be changed. The world's most extraordinary yachts are built by designers who imagine the impossible. Who spend years engineering solutions to problems that have never been solved before. Who treat a vessel not as a product but as a statement about what human craft can achieve. Those designers are now being squeezed out an industry they created. Not through competition, not through market forces, through a shadow system that rewards compliance and punishes integrity. The $200 million isn't just money. It's the cost of an industry soul paid in the silence of designers who couldn't afford to speak and the bankruptcy of the ones who did. The question now is not whether this gets exposed. It will. The question is whether anything gets built, truly built without compromise before it does. If you made it this far, you already understand why this story matters beyond yachts. This is about what happens to any creative industry when infrastructure becomes a weapon. Comment the word maritime if you want to go deeper, specifically into the legal mechanisms that make this kind of scheme so difficult to prosecute. And the three designers who are currently building the documentation to try. And if you want more investigations into the hidden economics of industries that sell luxury while running on coercion, subscribe.

It had beaten all the Indian brands and was in the leagues of Apple, Xiaomi, and Samsung. Then, between FY20 and FY23, Boat's revenue increased from 700 crore rupees to 3,400 crore. And that's a 5x growth in just 3 years. Boat's smartwatches and their earphones were everywhere, and Aman Gupta was one of the most celebrated entrepreneurs in India. And his company was getting ready for a blockbuster IPO. But then, things started to change. Boat's revenue started to fall. It started losing to other players in the segment. In fact, things got so bad that both its co-founders, Aman and Sameer, quit their positions and moved to non-executive roles. So, what exactly went wrong with Boat? And what does the future look like for the company? Welcome to our weekly Indian startup news show. I'm Pankaj, your host, and you're watching Backstage with Millionaires. So, let me take you back to where the story really starts. Which is not 2014, when Boat was founded, but in 2020, when Boat really took off. See, when the pandemic hit, Boat was already an established and growing brand. But COVID helped them accelerate at an unprecedented pace. The screen time for people grew manifold. Also, almost everyone was working from home. And because of that, people needed headphones. In fact, demand for audio products grew 20% in the early months of pandemic alone. And Boat, which had spent years building the most recognizable and affordable audio brand in India, benefited [music] from it the most. But that wasn't the only wave Boat caught in 2020. As people started tracking their health obsessively during the pandemic, smartwatches suddenly became a mass-market product. And Boat saw this as an opportunity and launched their own smartwatch line in October 2020. And this wearable segment of theirs, which did not exist until 2020, went on to bring 900 crore rupees in revenue within the next 2 years, which was nearly a quarter of everything the company made. And thanks to these two waves and Aman Gupta being Tank, Boat had crossed 3,376 crore in revenue in FY23. But then, things started to change. The two waves that Boat was riding suddenly started to disappear. And let's first talk about their smartwatch business. See, once the hype of smartwatches started to fade after the pandemic, people started evaluating their smartwatches more critically. And what they found was that these entry-level smartwatches, the kind that Boat made, which were typically priced below 2,500 rupees, did not really have the most accurate [music] health tracking. Basically, the consumer expectations from these products had gone up, but these products failed to keep up with the consumer. And as a result of this, the smartwatch market started shrinking in India. In FY24 alone, it collapsed by 34% and Boat was the most impacted company. Boat's smartwatch segment that brought in 901 crore rupees in revenue in FY24 had gone down to just 330 crore rupees in FY25, a 63% decline in just 1 year. But the thing to understand here is that consumers were not necessarily ditching smartwatches entirely. They were just ditching the low-quality ones. And I'm saying that because the market for premium smartwatches had essentially doubled during this time. The kind of smartwatches which are priced over 20,000 rupees, a segment where players like Apple and Samsung exist. So, smartwatches was the first bad news for Boat. Next came its audio business. The segment that had essentially made Boat also started declining. And I think this is a more deeper and existential problem for Boat. See, when Boat had built their reputation, they had a genuine edge. They were selling affordable products with cool designs. But that edge is now gone. Basically, this category is being commoditized right now. And when a category commoditizes, the brand that's perceived as affordable is usually the one that gets hurt most. And the culmination of all this happened when the two people who built Boat, Sameer Mehta and Aman Gupta, decided to quit and transition to a non-executive role. And now the company has brought in a new CEO. In fact, Boat has changed three CEOs in the last 3 years. And that's not good for a brand that's already losing its market. So, is this the end for Boat, or do they have a plan? Well, sort of. According to their DRHP, Boat now wants to focus on the premium segment, products above 5,000 rupee range. And the company now wants to focus on building better technology instead of just playing on the price. And that is the right direction. The premium audio market is where the growth is. But the problem with the segment is that it is owned by Apple, Sony, Samsung, and JBL. And competing with these brands won't be easy. All of them have spent decades [music] building credibility for premium audio. And Boat has never competed at that level. And now, repositioning a brand that built its reputation on affordable and trendy to suddenly play in premium is one of the hardest things to do in consumer electronics. And at that time, they were planning on raising 2,000 crore rupees. But the company decided to postpone it because of bad market condition. Then, they again filed for an IPO in 2025. This time with the plans of raising 1,500 crore rupees. But again, these plans were postponed. And we don't know when the company will finally go public. Also, there are some concerns with their DRHP as well. Boat's own auditors have flagged that Boat's quarterly returns filed with the banks did not match the company's internal books. And that too for three consecutive years. And that raises a lot of concerns. See, trying to go public with 3 years of bank versus book mismatches is not the best story to tell to the public market investors. And that at a time when your revenue is also falling. - The DISGUSTING Truth Behind Catalina Yachts' Collapse — Customer Deposits GONE: Kind: captions Language: en The deposit you wire for your next boat is not as safe as your dealer is telling you. One Catalina Yachts customer paid six figures for a sailboat that is now locked inside a factory in Florida. And the man who took that money was evicted from the building 5 months after he bought the company. Your dealer's parent company is one bad acquisition away from that same call. And the paperwork you signed does not protect you the way you think it does. What I am about to tell you about how Catalina Yachts was destroyed could cost me referrals from every yacht broker on the east coast. But the customers who lost their deposits deserve to be heard. To understand what happened here, you need to know what Catalina Yachts actually was. Frank Butler founded the company in 1970 in California. He spent 56 years building one of the most recognized American sailboat names on the water. The Catalina 22, the 27, the 350, the 425. Boats people saved for, planned retirements around, and passed down to their kids. Butler died in November 2020 at the age of 92. The brand outlived its founder by 5 years. In May 2025, Butler's Estate sold Catalina Yachts and its sister company, True North Yachts to a man named Michael Reirdon. Rearon was the founder of a small operation called Dedalus Yachts. The press release that went out that week announced what it called America's largest recreational boat building network. The trade press ran with it. Rearen was called the savior of American boat building. And then one month after he took over, he stopped paying the rent. The factory was in Largo, Florida. After taking ownership in May 2025, Reirdon made a single rent payment on that building. Then the payment stopped. Workers started getting paid late. Then, not at all. The deferred portion of the purchase price, roughly $1 million, that reared an owed to California Catalina, the seller, went unpaid. Inside the factory at that point, nine boats were in active production. Customers had already wired six-f figureure deposits on those builds. Their boats were real, partially assembled, sitting on the floor inside a building that was now 3 months behind on rent. But the part nobody printed in the press release was already in the court filings. On September 18th, 2025, California Catalina filed suit against Reirdan in Panellis County, Florida. The complaint did not bury the problem. The complaint said, "By falling behind on rent, Reirden has defaulted on the asset purchase agreement." That default triggered a clause that made the entire $1 million deferred purchase price due immediately. That is the line that gets lost in every news summary I read about this story. The man who bought Catalina Yachts, the self-proclaimed savior of American boat building, never had the money to buy it. He did not buy a boat company. He borrowed it. And then the eviction notice came. In late October 2025, a Florida court evicted Michael Rearen and his Catalina Holdings LCC from the Largo factory. The locks changed. Inside the building when those locks changed, nine partially built customer boats, the tooling Frank Butler had spent 50 years assembling, the molds that gave every Catalina hull its shape, and the tools workers needed to finish their jobs, jobs they had been showing up to without paychecks for weeks. The Penllis County Office of Human Rights later ruled on a wage theft case brought by two longtime employees, David and Lisa Pace. The magistrate ordered Catalina Holdings to pay double the unpaid wages owed to them. What was inside that factory the day the locks changed is the part that should worry about What was inside that factory the day the locks changed is the part that should worry every boat buyer in America. Now imagine you wired the deposit 18 months ago, $250,000. You have been getting build photos every 6 weeks. last month. The photos stopped. You drive out to Largo, Florida on a Tuesday morning. The parking lot is empty. The gate is chained. You can see the bow of your boat through the warehouse window. The phone number on your contract goes to a voicemail that has not been checked in 2 weeks. There is nobody to call. That was the last time you saw the boat you paid for. Catalina Yachts officially declared bankruptcy in January 2026. 56 years after Frank Butler built the first one, the nine unfinished boats still sit inside a building Rearon does not control, the customers who paid six figure deposits are now fighting a three-way battle against the bankrupt shell that took their money, against California Catalina that hosts the Lean on the factory assets, and against the landlord that owns the building in which those assets are sitting. Nobody has told the customers when they will get their money back. Nobody has told them when the building opens. The bankruptcy filing does not make that clear. And the deposit agreements they signed almost certainly do not make it clear either. If this is the kind of thing you want to know before you write the next check, hit subscribe and the bell. I do not water this down. I do not protect the dealers. This is what the industry looks like from the inside. Now, if you are thinking that you do not buy sailboats or you run a power boat or you buy outboards, this Catalina story is a sailboat story and it does not apply to me. Here is why you were wrong about that. BRP, the company that makes Evanrude and Sidu, just sold Alumac after laying off 175 workers in 8 months. That is the same playbook. Acquisition, production disruption, workers caught in the middle. Brunswick raised model year prices by 4% on top of a 200% tariff on Chinese aluminum. That pressure does not stay contained to one corner of the market, the leveraged buyer, the deferred purchase price, the deposits caught in the middle when the deal falls apart. That is not a sailboat problem. That is a boat industry problem and it is running right now in your segment with brands you recognize. The Catalina story is the version that got caught on paper. Most of them do not. Michael Rearen is not a mystery. He is a pattern. He announced he was saving American boat building. He made one rent payment. He did not pay his purchase obligations. He did not pay his workers. He left nine boats locked inside a building he no longer controls. And he left the customers who paid for those boats to figure out the legal system on their own. The press release that called him a savior is still indexed on Google. The court filings that tell you what actually happened are in Panellis County, Florida in the public dockage if you know where to look. Your dealer is not Michael Rearen. Your dealer's parent company might be talking to someone exactly like him right now. The deposit you wire for your next boat moves the moment you wire it. Where it goes after that, whether it sits in a segregated escrow account, whether it is protected by a state licensing bond, whether the paperwork you sign gives you any real priority if the company stops operating. That is the information your dealer will not volunteer. You have to ask.
