- 7 Tips for Living on a Boat with a Baby | ⛵ Sailing Britaly ⛵: Kind: captions Language: en Hello! In this video I'm gonna share seven tips with you to make life on board a boat with a baby easier.Emma and Rossella and are currently in the V birth and Emma is gonna get some sleep so it's just me but don't worry you'll see Rossella and Emma throughout this video. We have spent the last couple of months living on board with Emma. First of all on the hard during a refit - that was hard work - and then now on the water continuing the refit so we've learned a few things over that period of time and we thought we'd share these tips with you to help people out. Tip number one is a baby seat. When we first arrived here Emma couldn't sit down unsupported on her own but she could sit in her seat and because of that, we were able to put her in a seat and leave her for a little bit of time unattended, which freed us up to do other things around the boat. Not long after we'd been on the boat Emma learned to sit up on her own unsupported, but in order to do that we needed to have somewhere safe for her to be, so to meet that need I made her a play area. You can also lie on your front and you might start crawling in here. Yipee, crawling! Number three is an inflatable bath. This is really useful of course it can be deflated and stowed away in a very small space but when it's blown up it's a really comfortable bath it's very comfortable for Emma to be in she doesn't need supporting with our hands or anything in there she can't bang herself against anything because it's soft obviously the edges connected to that is a pull-out shower. First of all we had to fill the inflatable bath using containers but then afterwards I fitted a pull-out shower and that means that we can fill it straight from the tap. Number four is a washing machine we bought a small washing machine marketed at caravans and it's been absolutely fantastic. We've used it tons on board anybody with a baby will know how much washing they create and that has been a real godsend.Iit was cheap too and it's quite lightweight so it can be moved around and stowed away quite easily. Number five: A DVD player. We didn't fit this it was already on the boat when we bought it but it's been amazing if you need just 10 or 15 minutes of respite to do something together ie Rossella and I if we needed to do something together it's great to be able to put a DVD on also with Emma because I'm from the UK and Rossella, my wife, is from Italy so we're teaching Emma to be bilingual. I always speak English and Rossella always speaks Italian and the DVD can be switched between the two languages so basically we would do that we'd put it on once in English and once in Italian so Emma can continue to learn the two languages even without us teaching her. ..but a DVD player just gives you that 10 or 15 minutes if you've got something going on perhaps you need to anchor or do something you can put baby in the play area with the DVD and that frees you up to do something else. Number six is a potty. We bought a very cheap potty it was the smallest one we could find it was actually from poundshop so it cost one pound and we've been giving E|mma the opportunity to go to the toilet as much as possible which has been brilliant. In all honesty I started to take Emma to the toilet when she was six days old so this isn't a new thing her going to the toilet, but having a potty on the boat just makes it really really easy Emma loves the independence to go into the toilet you can tell after she's been she's very happy with herself she's very content she's proud of herself and it makes things easier for us because in the last six weeks Emma has only been to the toilet - I'm not talking about liquids here - but other than that she's only been to the toilet once in her nappy all the rest of the time she's gone in her potty and that makes it a lot easier for us you're not cleaning up a potty is very easy much easier than cleaning a dirty baby so it just makes sense and as I say you can see that Emma is very very content about that. Number seven is breastfeeding. Now, this is very personal thing but we're just sharing our experience. We have used bottles after Emma was released from hospital there was a period where we were feeding her with bottles in addition to breastfeeding so we have experience of how much more convenient breastfeeding is compared to bottles and sterilizing and all the other things that you have to do to stay on top of that. We haven't had to think about any of that when we've been on the boat because Emma has been breastfed from Rossella. She's now eating solid foods - but she's continuing to be breastfed and it just makes life super super easy also for traveling getting on a flight or something it's just a lot less to think about and it really is super super convenient of course it's good for baby too - having said that we know how difficult it is Rossella especially knows how difficult it is the the early periods of starting to breastfeed it really is not easy at all and it's very very easy to see how many people would stop because it's so difficult but if you persevere... I'm sorry Rossella isn't here for this section of video but I'm sure she would tell you stick with it it gets very easy after a while and your life is so much easier afterwards so that would be our tip number seven. There we are then we need to leave the boat now we're going back on land to meet our other commitments we need to make some money for example so we hope you enjoyed this video we hope you found it useful if you did give it a thumbs up. We are making videos like this to try and help people like you so if you found this useful then please consider going to our patreon page you can give one dollar per month and that will help us to make more useful videos for you. We'll put links to some of the products that were mentioned in the description of the video if you've got any tips of your own for making life on board with a baby any easier and please leave a comment below and help other people out and if you haven't done already subscribe to our channel for more videos okay that's it for now thank you for watching and we'll see you soon ciao

  4. @topshelf321 [1 likes]: I would start by checking and cleaning all the grounds.  If you have some corrosion it will cause a DC voltage drop with will cause the ECU to get bad readings from the sensors.  This is pretty common, especially in the salt water environment. Good luck, that's got to be aggravating with the limited time and access to tools.  If you have a good multi meter you can troubleshoot the wiring harness to see if you have a voltage drop.
  5. @ZipaDeeDooDahDays [1 likes]: You keep that engine room spotless, clean and well maintained, so my guess it's going to be something simple and probably just not obvious. With LOW VOLTAGE errors its usually a simple connection issue somewhere in the circuit. One simple place to look is the FUSE box connections. Many times those shake loose or a screw gets loose and then the full voltage doesnt get through and pops an error. The knee jerk reaction is to replace the ECU, but 9 out of 10 times that is never the issue, especially on a well maintained and under 20 year old boat as ECU's are self contained and built to practically last forever.

I am sure you know by now that Starlink as a NEW RV package, a different design to handle weather better and better electronics and software to handle not having a specific location to use while traveling. Also your location in Granada is a LOW CAPACITY location so you will receive low speeds during peek hours, to check for coverage see https://www.starlink.com/map?source=rv for more information - Users can expect high speed, low latency internet in areas marked "High Capacity", and notably slower speeds and higher latency during hours of peak usage in areas marked as "Low Capacity" or during events with many collocated users.

Three decades of premiums built on misrepresented exposure, all linked to his wife's interference. Her empire is collapsing. She responds by flailing, calling developers about emergency sales, asking if they'd be interested in controlling Marina Access if she can somehow regain authority. She still thinks this is a game she can win if she just pushes hard enough. Dr. Martinez calls me with a small piece of poetic symmetry. Garrett, the EPA wants to feature your marina as a model for sustainable waterfront development. She says Karen's violations gave them a perfect what not to do. Even her crimes are feeding a better future. I stand on my rebuilt dock. Yes, already rebuilt using legitimate marina funds. Watching the sunset bleed into the harbor. Tomorrow is harbor festival. She thinks she's planning a lastditch PR offensive. She has no idea what's coming. The new dock planks creek under my boots. Solid and permanent. Game almost over. One move left. Harbor Festival morning. Karen shows her last card. At dawn, she arrives with a moving truck, three hired laborers, and a frantic energy that's almost painful to watch. From my dock, coffee in hand, Officer Bradley, and Dr. Martinez at my side. I watch her direct the crew to tear apart her illegal dock modifications. She's trying to erase evidence before inspections. Martinez says, raising a telephoto lens. Too late. We've documented everything. Chainsaws buzz. Supports fall. Debris splashes into protected water. Ma'am. Bradley calls across the water. You need permits for demolition in a protected environmental zone. She ignores him. Sawdust and panic sweat glisten on her face. She's racing the clock, convinced if she can destroy the evidence before the festival crowd arrives. She can spin the story. What she doesn't know is that federal environmental officers are already here, watching from unmarked vehicles. Cameras running. By 8:00 a.m., vendors and volunteers are setting up for the festival. Lobster rolls, kids activities, boat displays, conservation booths, three news crews, too. Karen's workers slow down suddenly uneasy. Lady, this doesn't feel legal," one says, eyeing the water. "We really supposed to be cutting dock supports in a nature preserve? Keep working," she snaps. "I'll handle the paperwork, right?" The queen of demanding permits has none for her own emergency demolition. At 10:00 a.m., the official marina inspection starts. Engineer Roberts arrives with his team, clipboards, cameras, and the gravitas of a man who loves code numbers. He moves methodically along the properties. When he reaches Karen's dock, he turns to the gathered crowd. "Mrs. Peton's boat lift exceeded weight capacity by 40%." He announces her support modifications compromised the entire shared dock structure. Karen hurries over covered in sawdust. "Those modifications were HOA approved," she snaps. Ma'am, HOA approval is meaningless here, he replies. Only the Marina Corporation can authorize structural changes affecting shared waterway infrastructure. Environmental officer Palmer arrives mid inspection flanked by federal backup. Mrs. Peton, she says, you're being cited for unpermitted demolition in protected waters. The debris your crew created requires immediate hazardous cleanup. Karen's voice climbs towards hysteria. This is persecution. I'm being targeted for protecting property values. Festival goers gather, recognizing the woman who made their lives miserable. Pete grins as channel 5 sticks a microphone in his face. She spent 3 years telling us our gear was illegal, he says. Turns out she was the one breaking the law. Then Karen spots me talking to reporters and snaps. She storms across the lawn, finger aimed like a weapon. This is all his fault. She screams for the cameras. Garrett Blackwood is a mentally unstable veteran who forged documents to steal our waterfront. He destroyed my life. He's using fake authority to terrorize homeowners. Someone needs to stop this madman before Mrs. Peton. FBI agent Collins steps into frame. Badge out. You're under arrest for attempted bribery of a public official and conspiracy to defraud federal environmental agencies. The crowd falls silent except for camera shutters. As the cuffs go on, her face cycles from disbelief to rage to something like blind terror. You haven't heard the last of this. She shrieks as they lead her away. My husband will sue every one of you. This marina will be shut. Frank standing in the crowd just shakes his head. His career is already circling the drain. He knows Dr. Martinez squeezes my shoulder. What you did here protecting working waterfront from fraudulent authority. That's going to be a case study. The festival rolls on around us. Kids get their faces painted. Musicians pick up where they left off. People line up for lobster. For the first time in 3 years, the harbor feels free. And we're not done yet. By late afternoon, Harbor Festival is at full roar. 2,000 locals, three TV crews, federal officers, fishermen, yacht owners, tourists. Karen's arrest is already looping on the evening news. Harbor Commissioner Walsh steps up to the main stage at 4:00 for the official marina authority recognition ceremony. The crowd gathers. Fishermen in worn jackets, yach club types and polos, families with dripping ice cream cones. Ladies and gentlemen, Walsh announces, "We're here to formally recognize the reactivation of Lighthouse Cove Marina Corporation and clarify waterfront authority that's been misunderstood for several years." Karen arrives midspech, recently bailed out, looking like the storm she's been living in finally hit her. hair wild, clothes wrinkled, arms full of legal folders like shields. This is a fraud, she yells from the crowd. He manipulated all of you with fake documents and veteran pity. People turn, music stops, she has the room's full attention, and not in the way she wants. Walsh raises a hand. Mrs. Peton, would you like to present evidence contradicting the Marina Corporation standing? She all but storms the microphone. I've spent three years protecting this community from substandard boats and irresponsible owners. She declares the HOA voted to maintain aesthetic standards that do you have any legal documentation granting the HOA authority over waterways. Walsh interrupts gently. Yes or no? She falters. We have community standards. The board has always legal documentation, ma'am. he repeats. Not feelings, not habits, law. Silence. Seagulls fill the gap. The HOA serves community interests. She finally stammers. That's not jurisdiction. Walsh replies. Mr. Blackwood. I step up again. Uncle Robert's folder in hand. These incorporation papers filed in 1962 and verified by the state. established Lighthouse Cove Marina Corporation with authority over all waterfront activities in this harbor. I say holding the documents high for the cameras. For 3 years, Mrs. Peton has enforced rules she had no right to create over boat owners she had no authority to regulate on waters she never owned. This is a conspiracy, she shrieks. You're all being manipulated by a mentally unstable. The universe interrupts her. A sound rips across the harbor. Metal screaming under stress. The oversized boat lift on her property. Weakened by her frantic demolition work and stressed by the afternoon wind. Finally gives up. Chains snap. Her $60,000 yacht drops like a stone. The first three rows of onlookers are showered in cold harbor water as her boat plunges stern. A heartbeat later, fuel starts to bloom across the surface in an iridescent slick. Karen staggers toward the shore, lips moving. My boat, my beautiful boat. Harbor patrol and environmental crews move instantly. Containment booms, spill kits, a flurry of trained efficiency. Dr. Martinez takes the microphone with the authority of science behind her. This is exactly why marina corporations enforce weight limits and engineering standards. She says oversized, unpermitted installations can fail catastrophically and damage the entire ecosystem. Karen wades into the oily shallows, skirts swirling in contaminated water, staring at the sinking symbol of everything she thought she controlled. Officer Palmer returns to the mic. Mrs. Peton, she announces, "You are now facing additional federal charges for causing an environmental hazard during a public event. This spill threatens protected shellfish beds. The irony is surgical." Karen tried to play environmental cop without authority. Real environmental cops are arresting her for actual environmental crimes. Crews work around the mess while the band starts up again. Kids go back to their games. People pick up their drinks and keep celebrating. I look out over the harbor. Booms encircling the spill. Her yacht slipping out of sight. The crowd cheering as Walsh hands me a framed certificate. Ladies and gentlemen, I tell them, Lighthouse Cove Marina Corporation is officially operational. We're committed to protecting this harbor's heritage and its health. For everyone who works and plays here now, and for whoever comes next. Applause rolls across the water as her boat disappears beneath it. Her rain sinks with it. Justice finally has teeth. 6 months later, I'm standing on the same dock where this started. This time, the water is clear. The air smells like salt and varnish, not diesel and fear. Karen is 6 months into an 8-month federal sentence for bribery, environmental crimes, and conspiracy. Frank lost his insurance license and paid $200,000 in fines. They sold their waterfront place at a loss to the Torinos. The family she tried hardest to shove out now owns her old mansion. Uncle Robert would have loved this. Pete says, watching his grandkids tack a small dinghy in the afternoon breeze. Working families staying on a working harbor. The Uncle Robert Maritime Heritage Fund has grown beyond anything I imagined. between back marina fees, penalties, and donations. We've established three annual scholarships for local kids pursuing maritime careers. Funded a harbor restoration project that brought university research teams here. Built a community boat workshop where anyone can learn to repair and maintain vessels. The workshop smells like fresh varnish and sawdust. Kids and retirees work side by side learning knots and splices. This is what my uncle wanted. A waterfront that pulls people together instead of pushing them out. Every Tuesday, Dr. Martinez walks the docks with me, updating me on water quality. Ironically, Karen's fuel spill unlocked federal restoration funds. The harbor is now cleaner than it's been in decades. Three universities want to use this place as a long-term research site, Martinez says, pointing at charts. Your Marina is a model project. Our legal case, Blackwood v. Peton, shows up in maritime law textbooks. An example of how Marina corporate rights override HOA fantasies in waterfront disputes. Law students read about Karen as a warning. Never assume authority you can't prove in court. The insurance settlement from my destroyed yacht didn't just buy a replacement. It helped launch third Chance. A 42 FFT sailing school boat donated by a grateful owner. Karen once harassed out of the harbor. Every Saturday I take local kids sailing. Most of them could never dream of yacht club dues. They learn points of sail right of way, how to read wind off the water. Their laughter carries back to shore, high and bright. Every time one of them nails a tricky maneuver or grins into the wind, something inside me heals a little. Other coastal towns have started calling. Turns out our story isn't unique. rich newcomers moving in, trying to turn working harbors into ornamental backdrops. I've become an unofficial consultant on marina law and corporate water rights. The boating magazine runs an annual piece now, Lighthouse Cove, as a case study in community backbone. Harbor Festival has doubled in size. People come specifically to see the place where an HOA bully lost to a marina corporation and a stack of old papers. And yes, I'm dating. Martinez and I figured out there's more between us than environmental briefings. She's been helping me understand just how far ahead of his time Uncle Robert really was. The dock planks creek under my feet as I walk the boundary lines each morning. Coffee in hand. Eight waterfront families now have secure sane leases. The fishing cooperative has permanent space. The conservation partnership protects water quality. The heritage fund protects the story. The rigging sings in the breeze. Kids practice tax in the outer harbor. Old fishermen tell young captains where the best lobster ledges are. Uncle Robert's vision is alive here. Honest boats, honest work, no harassment from people who think a bank balance equals divine right. Karen wanted to turn this into a gated playground for people like her. Instead, her campaign birthed legal protections strong enough that the next version of her won't stand a chance. Sometimes revenge isn't about what you do to the person who wronged you. It's about what you build that they can never touch. I stand on my dock as sunrise paints the harbor gold and red. In a few hours, kids will show up for sailing lessons. Pete will bring coffee and stories. New families will step onto these docks without bracing for a fight. The fight was worth every minute. The harbors learned the difference between real authority and makebelieve power. And it remembers.

  Top Comments:
  1. @brokenmechanic [2 likes]: Hi Brandon. I would like to see you test the Potentiometer for the Port engine in the power commander. Each engine has its own Pot to Control RPM. Just because the Starboard one is fine doesn't mean the Port side is good. In me career I have changed many Accelerator pedals for just such an issue. Generally the potentiometer is made with carbon strip. Vibration and use over time causes the the contact between the strip and the arm that runs on the strip to fail. When you turn on the key the ECU sends a signal to all systems associated with engine start and and run asking for system check and report. This happens in milliseconds and you can see it when you turn on the key the gauges all go up then down. If the voltage reading sent back to the ECU is not at the correct value it will be sent in to fault mode, then it will list a fault and sand a warning to the dash for you to then sit there and pull your hair out until you find the issue. Your power commander will have a test procedure to establish if the voltages are correct. Lugger would have a scan tool connector to plug in to so you can see all the values in real time. This is the easiest way to test power commander. Find your OBD 2 port and plug in a real time value scanner. With a good quality scanner you can also do a wiggle test on the wiring harness. If a plug or wire is bad this will show a voltage change in a circuit on the screen when you shake a plug or connector. I hope this helps
  2. @thomasfastiggi6306 [2 likes]: This is more than likely higher resistance in a wire between the engine and your panel. Or a loose connection in the plug where the wires in the plug attach. You will need an ohm meter to check out the resistance level one wire at a time and as well as about 50 feet of 16-24 gauge sized   wire to run from the panel down to the plug end on the engine. Bypass each wire feed  one at a time from the engine to the panel using your jumper wire and then see if it will reset. When it does then you know you’ve got your bad wire. Follow the color chart for your wires orange for orange red for red etc. 

Check your grounds, to make sure you’ve got a good ground between the panel and the engine.  Run a temporary wire from a good connection on the engine block up to your panel ground at the helm.  

 Check your battery cable ends to make sure you don’t have condensation between the cable clamp and the battery post. That will add resistance to the circuits.
also check any sensors that are exposed to saltwater either as a direct screwed in sensor In the block or cooling system. 

Voltage issues are just like sewer lines, you gotta find a back up. And then when you clear it everything goes back to normal.

There are so many electronics, sensors, computers, boards, etc that failure is more common than ever.  They have engineered these motors into certain failure and brain racking solutions that can only be fixed by a specialized technician in many cases.  Nordhavn has the right idea with their new N41.  Going back to basics with dual motors equipped with a few gauges and removing all the computers.  Well done!  Anyone can diagnose and fix most issues.  Easy to outfit with spares for long distance reliable travel.

- Why Catamarans Are Expensive Nightmares to Maintain: Kind: captions Language: en What's the problem with catamarans? Why do they dominate charter fleets, fill marina dream boards, and keep showing up as the ultimate liveaboard fantasy, while still getting side-eyed by serious sailors? Because the catamaran problem is not that they're bad boats. It's that they solve the easy problems first. They solve space. They solve comfort. They solve stability at rest. They solve the fear a lot of people have of healing. But in solving those things, they create a whole new set of trade-offs that don't show up in the brochure. And those trade-offs are exactly why catamarans still aren't universally respected, even though the market keeps growing. On the surface, the case for catamarans looks almost unbeatable. They're wide. They're stable. They have huge cabins. They feel more like waterfront apartments than traditional boats. They can be fast. They don't lean dramatically. They're shallow draft. They work brilliantly for charter. And as a market, they've become enormous, valued at around $4.7 billion in 2024, with projected growth continuing through the next decade. So if they're growing, if people clearly want them, if they're everywhere in warm water cruising grounds, then why does this weird resistance still exist? Why do so many experienced sailors still treat catamarans like the boat nobody wants to talk about honestly? Because once you move past the dream, once you stop imagining a perfect anchorage with flat water and sunset lighting, and start thinking about ownership, offshore conditions, maintenance, repair access, upwind performance, structural loads, and storm behavior, the whole picture changes. And that's the paradox. The catamaran is one of the most desirable boats to live on, and one of the most debated boats to actually sail seriously. That contradiction is the whole story. The problem starts with something most non-sailors have never even heard of, bridge deck slamming. This is the technical complaint that comes up again and again because it's not subtle. It's violent. You hear it. You feel it. And once you've experienced it in bad conditions, you don't forget it. A monohull has one hull entering the sea. It cuts through waves with one central body. A catamaran has two hulls connected by a platform, and that platform creates a tunnel between them. In calm water, that setup looks brilliant. In confused seas, especially short, steep chop, or head seas, it becomes a problem. Because when the two hulls hit waves, they displace water into that gap between them. That water gets forced upward. It piles up under the bridge deck. And if the clearance isn't high enough, the underside of the platform gets hit hard. Not brushed, not splashed, hit. The result is a heavy explosive slam that can sound like the boat is being smashed from underneath. And even when the structure is technically fine, the effect on the crew is real. That's the part people underestimate. Sailors don't experience boats as engineering diagrams. They experience them through fatigue, sound, stress, sleep deprivation, motion, and confidence. If every few minutes the underside of the platform is getting hammered in rough water, the crew stops feeling like they're on a tough, capable boat, and starts feeling like they're on something being punished by the sea. That matters, because offshore morale is not a luxury. It's part of safety. Designers try to manage this with bridge deck clearance. A useful rule is that the bridge deck clearance ratio should be at least around 6% of the boat's length overall. The more clearance, generally, the less violent the slamming. That's why older designs with low clearance developed bad reputations, while more modern offshore-oriented cats push the clearance higher. A performance-oriented 50-footer might carry roughly 37.4 inches of clearance. A larger offshore model, like a 57-footer, can push above 42 inches. Those numbers aren't random. They're the difference between tolerable and punishing. But then, the next compromise appears. Buyers want interior volume. They want bigger beds, lower cabin soles, more room, more apartment. So some builders effectively steal space from the tunnel to create more interior comfort. They add intrusions into the bridge deck zone. They lower sections of the structure. They optimize the layout for the marina, not the sea. And that makes slamming worse. So right away, you can see the catamaran problem in its purest form. The boat sells because it offers more volume, but more volume can reduce clearance. Reduced clearance increases slamming. Slamming increases fatigue. Fatigue reduces confidence. And confidence offshore is everything. That alone would be enough to explain a lot of the skepticism, but it gets worse. Because the next major issue is performance, and specifically the kind of performance that matters most to sailors who care about sailing, rather than just boating. Upwind ability. This is where a lot of the catamarans are faster marketing starts to fall apart. Because yes, catamarans can absolutely be faster than monohulls. In the right conditions, on the right angles, they can be dramatically faster, especially on reaches. But speed is not one thing. It depends on the point of sail, sea state, design, displacement, and how the boat generates lateral resistance. And most production cruising catamarans are not optimized for upwind performance. Why? Because they're designed for broad appeal, for shallow draft, for charter use, for comfort, for easy systems, for coastal cruising, not for clawing efficiently to windward in ugly conditions. A lot of them don't have deep keels. Many rely on fixed mini keels instead of deep foils or retractable daggerboards. That means when they try to point high into the wind, they often lack grip in the water. And when a boat lacks grip, it doesn't just go slower. It makes leeway. It slides sideways. So while a good monohull can point higher and convert more of its effort into forward progress, a lot of cruising catamarans have to sail lower angles and accept more sideways loss. That changes everything. Because sailors don't judge weather performance by brochure speed claims. They judge it by whether the boat can actually get where it needs to go when the wind shifts forward and the sea gets uncomfortable. And for a lot of cruising cats, that answer is less impressive than people expect. Yes, a performance cat with daggerboards can do much better. Yes, some high-end offshore cats can match monohulls surprisingly well upwind. But that is not most of the market. Most of the market is charter grade or comfort first. And those boats are exactly the ones critics are talking about when they say catamarans aren't real sailboats in the traditional sense. Because to a purist, if the boat struggles upwind and ends up leaning on twin diesel engines to make progress against bad angles, then it starts to feel less like a true sailing machine, and more like a motor platform with sails attached. That perception is harsh, but it exists for a reason. And even when the catamaran is fast, there's another problem. The rig itself creates limitations. Most catamarans don't use a conventional backstay because it would interfere with the large mainsail shape and cockpit layout. So instead, the mast is supported by heavily swept spreaders and diamond stays. That works structurally, but it creates a geometry problem. On a monohull, you can often ease the boom very far out on a deep run. The sail projects cleanly. The boat can head almost straight downwind. On a catamaran, if you ease the boom too far, the mainsail can press into those swept spreaders. That means chafe, pressure, and potential sail damage. So many catamarans can't simply run dead downwind the way people imagine. Instead, they often need to sail broader angles and tack downwind for better velocity made good. Again, not always a disaster. Sometimes the boat's raw speed makes up for the extra distance. But it's another example of the same pattern. The catamaran's strengths are real, but they're conditional. And the conditions where they shine are not the same conditions that define hardcore sailing credibility. Then comes the economic reality. And honestly, this is where a lot of the dream dies. Because the price of the boat is only the beginning. A 40-to-44-foot catamaran can easily cost between about $400,000 and $650,000. Move into the 45-to-50-foot range, and passing $1 million becomes normal. That alone excludes a huge part of the sailing world. But even if you can buy one, owning it is a completely different equation. Because catamarans come with what owners often experience as a double-everything tax. Two engines, two sail drives, two rudders, two hulls to antifoul, more plumbing runs, more systems, more structure, more deck area, more surface area, more maintenance points. Even when jobs aren't literally doubled, enough of them are doubled that the ownership pattern becomes obvious. You are maintaining a much larger, more complex platform, and the annual cost reflects that. A reasonable annual upkeep estimate can run around 5% to 10% of the boat's value. For a $700,000 catamaran, that means maybe $35,000 a year just to keep the boat in shape. And that's before financing and depreciation. A typical annual operating budget for a 45-ft cruising cat can easily break down like this. Maintenance and systems, $20,000 to $40,000. $12,000 to $25,000. Insurance, $6,000 to $18,000. Fuel and consumables, $5,000 to $10,000. So, all in, you're often looking at something like $43,000 to $93,000 a year. That's not a niche issue. That's the difference between a dream and a financial structure. And the marina part is especially brutal because it's not just about price, it's about beam. This is one of the most practical reasons catamarans are still less popular than people assume. Marinas were built for mono hulls. A catamaran's width is one of its biggest selling points. It creates the huge deck area, the stability, the giant cabins, the comfortable cockpit. But that width turns into a tax the moment you try to dock it. Because a catamaran often occupies the space of two mono hulls, marinas frequently charge 1 and 1/2 to two times the normal rate for dockage. And sometimes that's the best-case scenario. In places with premium waterfront pricing, especially where slips are scarce, the cost can become absurd. In some parts of the Mediterranean, long-term birthing for catamarans can be so expensive that it's effectively reserved for wealthier owners. And the problem isn't only cost, a lot of older marinas simply can't fit modern cruising catamarans. The slips are too narrow. The infrastructure was designed decades ago for a completely different beam profile. So, even if you can pay, you still may not have access. That forces owners into T-head spaces, premium berths, or anchoring out more often than they planned. And that sounds romantic until it becomes your daily logistics, provisioning, dinghy runs, weather exposure, shore access. All of it becomes harder. But there's an even less glamorous infrastructure problem that tells you a lot about why experienced owners think differently about catamarans than first-time dreamers do. Haul outs. Most buyers obsess over layouts and rarely think hard enough about who can actually lift the boat out of the water when something breaks. That's a mistake because a lot of travel lifts are built around mono hull beam limits. Under 22 ft is common, but a standard 40-ft catamaran can already be around 23 ft wide. A 50-footer can reach roughly 26.5 ft. That means a huge number of yards simply cannot haul them. Not because they don't want to, because they physically can't. So, now your service options shrink dramatically. Instead of asking, "Where's the nearest yard?" you start asking, "Which yard within hundreds of miles has a lift wide enough, strong enough, and configured for multi-hull blocking?" That changes ownership. Specialized facilities do exist. Some can handle beams in the mid-20s to low 30s with lift ratings from around 70 to 150 tons. But scarcity creates leverage. Those yards can charge more, and they often do. Multi-hull blocking alone can add 25% to the cost of a standard haul out. And if damage happens in a remote area, the problem gets more serious. A repair that would be manageable on a mono hull can become a logistical nightmare on a cat simply because the right haul out facility doesn't exist nearby. That's the kind of issue you never notice in a sales brochure, but owners never forget. Then we get to the safety debate, which is probably the most emotionally loaded part of the whole catamaran discussion. Because catamarans feel extremely safe until you define exactly what kind of safety you mean. In normal conditions, their stability is one of their biggest advantages. They don't heel much. They provide a flat platform that reduces crew fatigue. It reduces the chance of someone falling. It makes cooking, sleeping, and moving around much easier. For families, that's a huge benefit. But this is primary stability, not ultimate stability. And the difference matters. A mono hull gets much of its safety from ballast low in the keel. As it heels, that ballast creates a righting moment. The boat can be rolled and still want to come back upright. A catamaran works differently. Its stability peaks much earlier, usually only around 10 to 12° of heel. After that, once you push it too far, the righting moment drops away. That means the catamaran resists small heel extremely well. But if it is pushed beyond its limit, there is no heavy keel waiting to pull it back upright. And that is the psychological fault line in the whole debate. Because a mono hull is designed to self-right. A catamaran, once inverted, is stable upside down. Now, that does not mean modern cruising catamarans are constantly flipping. They're not. Capsize in a modern cruising cat is rare and usually associated with extreme conditions, serious inattention, or severe misuse. We're talking about weather that can exceed 70 to 100 knots, or seamanship failures that would put almost any boat in danger. But offshore sailors don't care only about what happens most of the time. They care about what happens when things go wrong in the worst possible way. And here, the catamaran asks them to accept something many never fully get comfortable with. If it goes over, it may stay over. Catamaran defenders respond with a very real point, positive buoyancy. A mono hull with a heavy keel can sink if the hull is breached badly enough. A catamaran, because of its construction, watertight compartments, and lighter materials, often remains afloat even when badly damaged or inverted. So, instead of sinking, it can become a giant life raft. That's not a trivial advantage. In many rescue scenarios, remaining with a floating platform is statistically safer than ending up in the water. So, the argument is not simple. It's not that one side is stupid and the other is right. It's that catamarans and mono hulls offer different safety psychologies. A catamaran gives you more stability in everyday life. A mono hull gives you self-righting in an extreme event. And a lot of traditional sailors will always prefer the second kind of reassurance. Then there's motion. This one matters more than people think. Catamarans are often praised for not rolling like mono hulls. At anchor, that can be incredible. Better sleep, less rolling, more comfort, more livability. But motion underway is a different story. A mono hull tends to roll in a slower, more rhythmic way. Some people hate that. Others find it predictable. A catamaran often moves differently. Because it's straddling wave systems with two hulls, it can react to multiple wave patterns at once. That can produce a jerky, high-frequency hobby horse motion in chop that some sailors find more nauseating than a mono hull's slower roll. So, the simplistic idea that catamarans are always more comfortable is not really true. They are often more comfortable in one specific way, but they can be more uncomfortable in another. Even at anchor, they create a different problem. Because catamarans have high windage and shallow draft, they don't always swing like deep-keeled mono hulls. In shifting breeze, they can move differently, swing wider, and behave unpredictably compared to nearby boats. That creates real anchorage friction, sometimes literally. Then we get to one of the least discussed but most important technical issues, weight sensitivity. This is where the catamaran's giant living space becomes a trap. A catamaran's performance depends heavily on staying light. Its speed comes from light displacement and narrow hull efficiency. But because the boat has so much room, owners are constantly tempted to load it up. More tools, more spare parts, more fuel, more water, more gear, more toys, more comfort. And unlike a mono hull, which can often absorb weight increases with less dramatic effect, a catamaran gets punished quickly. Add around 10% to a catamaran's design weight, and you can easily lose 10% or more of its speed. That's huge. And it doesn't stop there. More weight means the boat sits lower. Sitting lower means reduced bridge deck clearance. Reduced bridge deck clearance means more slamming. More slamming means more discomfort and more fatigue. So, overloading a catamaran can create a nasty negative loop. Slower, harsher, less efficient, more engine use, less sailing pleasure. That's why long-term cruising cats are such a paradox. They offer more storage than a mono hull of similar length, but using that storage fully can destroy the exact performance advantage that justified buying the boat in the first place. And then there's something even harder to measure, sailing feel. This is where the cultural divide gets really deep. A mono hull talks to the sailor. It heels, the helm loads up, the whole boat gives feedback. You can feel rising pressure. You can sense when it wants a reef. That tactile conversation is part of what many people think sailing is. A catamaran often doesn't do that. Because it sails flatter, and because many use steering systems that provide less physical feedback, the boat can feel detached. It goes where it's pointed. It feels stable, composed, almost clinical. For some owners, that's wonderful. For traditional sailors, it can feel unsettling. Because the boat can remain calm and flat while rig loads are quietly building. So, instead of talking to you through heel and helm pressure, it may simply keep going until you're much closer to the limit than you realize. To a lot of purists, that feels like sailing with one of the senses removed. And that is a big reason catamarans still carry a stigma in some parts of the sailing world. They're seen as floating caravans, as houseboats, as condos with masts. Not because they can't sail, but because they remove too much of the physical drama that many sailors consider central to the sport. Modern production cats don't help themselves here. High freeboard, vertical windows, huge saloons, flybridges. They often look more like luxury real estate than sea-going tools, and culture matters. There are sailors who will never buy a catamaran, not because the technical case is weak, but because the symbolism feels wrong. To them, sailing is supposed to involve compromise, sensitivity, seamanship, and physical dialogue with the elements. A catamaran can look like an attempt to keep the scenery while deleting too much of the experience. That judgment may be unfair, but it's real, and it affects adoption, which brings us to the final point. The market growth doesn't actually disprove the criticism, it explains it. Because catamarans are succeeding in exactly the parts of boating where their strengths matter most: charter fleets, warm water cruising, coastal hopping, liveaboard life, luxury social sailing. They dominate where people value space, comfort, stability, entertaining, and easy access to shallow anchorages. And they keep struggling for universal respect in the parts of sailing culture that care most about upwind ability, storm behavior, service access, weight discipline, helm feedback, and ultimate recovery. So, both sides are seeing something real. Catamaran buyers are not crazy. The boats really are fantastic for a lot of use cases. And catamaran critics are not just jealous or old-fashioned. The compromises really are serious. That is the multihull paradox. The industry often sells catamarans as if they are the obvious evolution of sailing, the upgraded version, the better answer. But that's not what they are. They're a different answer. An answer optimized for comfort, stability, social space, and a certain style of cruising. And when that answer is taken outside its sweet spot, the cracks become obvious: bridge deck slamming, upwind compromises, rig limitations, double maintenance, beam taxes, haulout scarcity, capsize psychology, jerky motion, weight sensitivity, reduced feedback, and cultural resistance. That's why catamarans still aren't universally popular, not because people don't understand them, because many sailors understand them perfectly. They understand that the same beam that makes them comfortable makes them expensive to dock. They understand that the same volume that makes them livable can reduce clearance and worsen slamming. They understand that the same flat sailing that feels luxurious can reduce warning feedback. They understand that the same stability that feels safe in normal life comes with a very different story if the boat ever goes over. And once you understand all of that, the catamaran stops looking like the final form of sailing. It starts looking like what it really is, a compromise machine. A brilliant one in some contexts, a frustrating one in others. So, no, catamarans aren't unpopular because they fail. They're unpopular in certain circles because they solve the wrong problems first. They solve the problems people notice at the dock, not always the ones that matter most offshore. They solve how to live on the water beautifully, not always how to suffer on it efficiently. And sailing, real sailing, especially offshore, has always been partly about how a boat behaves when life stops being comfortable. That's why the debate never goes away. The catamaran is commercially successful, technically impressive, deeply desirable, and still controversial. Because the closer you get to ownership reality, the more the conversation stops being about luxury and starts being about friction. Friction with waves, friction with windward performance, friction with marina pricing, friction with haulout infrastructure, friction with maintenance budgets, friction with storm psychology, friction with sailing culture itself. That's the catamaran problem, not that people don't want them, that too many people want them for the fantasy without understanding the cost of the geometry. And until designers fully solve the slamming issue without sacrificing interior volume, until marina and yard infrastructure catch up to wide beam cruising platforms, and until buyers become more honest about the gap between charter comfort and offshore reality, the catamaran will keep living this double life. A soaring success in the luxury and cruising market, and a controversial compromise among the people who take sailing most seriously.

  2. @uncaboat2399 [1 likes]: Southern California, slip fees will run you $500 to $600 and up, depending on the length of the boat and the length of the slip.  You might luck out an find something under $500, but don't count on it.  Most of the slips also have waiting lists.  Many of the used boats claim they can transfer their slip to you, thus skipping on the waiting list, but I haven't confirmed whether the slip companies actually allow that.

One advantage to SoCal tho, you don't have to take it out in winter to avoid freezing.  Just hire a diver to clean off the barnacles monthly ($50/month or more).

  16. @Voltairus30: He's right in principle, but as he sort started to make clear is that you get what you pay for. Just like people who want to " van life " and live out of their van and travel. 

It " can " be relatively inexpensive compared to normal residential living depending on what you're willing to cut corners on and how much of the work you're willing and capable of doing on your own. 

You can buy a 20-20 year work van with several hundred thousand miles on it for 5k or less, throw some plastic storage bins from walmart in there with some old mattresses and you're off. But, that also means you either need to have saved up money to live on or you need a remote job capable of sustaining your fuel, food, etc while you're living your life on the road. 

But, just getting started in the van life is very easy and relatively inexpensive. Just like its very easy to fall into debt trying to get the very best at each point in order to have all the comforts, bells, and whistles while living on the road. You can easily drop half a million dollars buying a brand new large scale van from a company that will out fit with the best suspension package, electrical system, and plumbing to make you fully off grid in a van for weeks at a time while having the comfort of a luxury apartment.

- Why Boat Ownership Feels Cheap — Until Year Two: Kind: captions Language: en A cruiser worth $120,000. Year 1 feels under control. [music] Survey, taxes, initial upgrades. Then year two arrives. The boat now has a burn rate, and the burn rate doesn't pause. Ownership rarely collapses in year 1. It becomes heavy in year [music] two. The first year with a $120,000 cruiser often feels like a series of deliberate, manageable steps. >> [music] >> The process starts with a professional marine survey and a hall out, typically running between $1,500 and $3,500. These are not just formalities. They are required [music] for financing and insurance, and they offer a sense of reassurance as the hull is inspected, [music] cleaned, and certified. Registration and documentation fees follow, usually [music] landing somewhere between $150 and $400 depending on the state. It is a small price compared to the purchase, and once paid, it rarely crosses the owner's mind again until resale. Attention quickly turns to safety and electronics. Many owners decide to invest in modern navigation gear, an AIS transponder, [music] or an updated EPIRB. These upgrades often total between $2,000 and $8,000, but they are seen as personal choices, investments in peace of mind and convenience. The same logic applies to cosmetic improvements. A fresh set of cockpit cushions, new canvas, or a minor gel coat repair. Each expense is weighed, approved, and checked off the list. A surveyor with years in the field puts it plainly. The first year is about catching up to your own standards. You are buying into a baseline. That baseline feels like progress. Bills arrive as one-time items, each tied to a specific project or improvement. Even the slip fee, often paid in a lump sum for the first year, blends into the background as part of the upfront cost. With every check written, the owner feels in control, proactive, [music] rational, and optimistic about the seasons ahead. In this phase, costs are interpreted as investments, not recurring obligations. The numbers add up, but they add up to something tangible. A boat made ready, safe, and uniquely theirs. Industry data sets a clear expectation for what comes after the first year. The cost of keeping a boat runs between 8% and 12% of its market value every year. [music] for a $120,000 cruiser. That translates to $9,600 to $14,400 annually or about $800 to $1,200 each month before factoring in any loan payments or major upgrades. This percentage is not a warning. [music] It is a baseline confirmed by surveys from the National Marine Manufacturers Association and the American Boat Owners Association. >> It is built from the ground up. Slip fees, insurance, routine maintenance, utilities, [music] and the small but steady stream of regulatory renewals. The pattern holds across regions and vessel types. Coastal marinas in the Gulf or the Pacific Northwest tend to push costs toward the upper end, especially as slip rates climb and insurance premiums adjust after storm seasons. Inland lakes and rivers come in lower, but the core ratio of 8% to 12% remains a reliable planning number. Even owners who cut corners or do their own maintenance rarely drop below this range once all recurring expenses are tallied. This annual share, quietly deducted month after month, is what transforms the experience. [music] The upfront outlays of year 1 give way to a steady burn rate. A rhythm of invoices that no longer feel like investments, but instead become the cost of simply keeping the boat afloat. What once looked like a series of choices now settles into a financial routine, revealing the true character of ownership as a persistent, predictable drain. Monthly life with a 40ft cruiser is defined by a handful of invoices that arrive with clockwork regularity. The slip fee stands out first. [music] In the Midwest or on inland lakes, the range runs from $12 to $20 per foot each month, about $500 to $800 for a boat this size. On [music] the coasts, especially in the Pacific Northwest or Gulf States, the same slip can cost as much as $30 per [music] foot, pushing the bill to $1,200 or more. Insurance, recalculated after the first year's discount disappears, lands between $2500 and $6,000 annually. That is another $200 to $500 each month with rates climbing for older hulls or boats birthed in hurricane prone regions. Utilities and marina fees, including power, water, [music] pumpouts, and Wi-Fi, add another $100 to $250, depending on [music] usage and the facility's amenities. A marina manager in Florida puts it simply, "It doesn't matter if you sail or not. The slip, the [music] power, and the insurance bill show up every month, rain or shine. These are the fixed costs of [music] simply keeping the boat in the water. expenses that persist even if the engine never turns over and the sales stay furled. Major maintenance cycles rarely announce themselves in the first year. [music] Instead, they wait quietly, building up in the background until the calendar or a surveyor's checklist brings them [music] forward. Every significant component on a 40ft cruiser, standing rigging, [music] sails, engine, and bottom paint has a service lifespan measured in years, not months. A professional surveyor will point out [music] that standing rigging typically needs replacement every 10 to 12 years. [music] For a full replacement on a boat this size, the bill usually lands between $7,000 and $10,000. [music] spread over a decade, that is $167 to $200 per month, whether or not the owner sets money aside. Sales follow a similar rhythm. A new main and head sales set [music] runs $9,000 to $15,000 with most owners facing replacement every 7 to9 years. That is another $100 to $140 per month. Quietly acrewing even when the sales look fine on the furled boom. Diesel engines are built to last, but a major overhaul common around the 15-year mark costs $12,000 to $18,000 or [music] $7 to $100 per month when advertised. Even bottom paint, which keeps marine growth at bay, needs to be reapplied every 1 to2 [music] years at $1,200 to $2,000 per haul out. When these cycles are averaged [music] together, the hidden cost approaches $20,000 over 10 years, or about $167 each month. What feels like an occasional manageable repair is in reality a persistent [music] monthly burden. These are not emergencies or upgrades. [music] They are the predictable cost of keeping the boat seaorthy, and they quietly raise the ongoing burn rate far above the visible invoices. [music] Optional upgrades and financing stack another layer onto the monthly burden, often in ways that catch owners offguard. Electronics packages, chart plotters, [music] radar, AIS, and autopilot can range from $8,000 to $20,000 for a 40ft [music] cruiser. Energy system upgrades like solar panels with lithium batteries [music] frequently exceed $10,000. Canvas and enclosure work, whether for sun protection [music] or cockpit comfort, typically runs between $3,000 and $7,000. Each of these choices [music] feels like an improvement, but none reduce the baseline cost of ownership. Instead, they add to the capital at risk and [music] in some cases drive up insurance premiums or property taxes. Financing magnifies the exposure further. Most buyers put 10 to 20% down and finance the rest over 5 to seven years. For a $120,000 boat, a typical loan payment falls between $900 and $1,100 per month, depending on term and rate. That payment lands on top of the recurring costs already in play. One marine finance sums it up. The payment is just the price of entry. The real cost is everything that follows. At this point, the monthly outflow can easily cross $2,500 to $3,000. With upgrades and financing layered in, the numbers begin to challenge even optimistic budgets, setting the stage for the next reality check. How the boat's value holds up against [music] this steady stream of spending. Depreciation shapes the financial reality of boat ownership from the moment the paperwork is signed. Unlike a home, a cruiser is a consumption asset. [music] Its value erodess steadily regardless of how carefully it is maintained. The NADA [music] used boat price index tracks this decline. New 35 to 45 ft production cruisers lose an average of 12 to 15% [music] of their value each year in the first 5 years on a $120,000 boat. That is a drop of $14,000 to $18,000 annually. That is over $1,000 in lost equity every month before factoring in a single repair or slip fee. Older vessels, [music] typically a decade off the showroom floor, see depreciation slow to 6 to 8% per year. Still, that equals $6,000 to $8,000, [music] vanishing annually from a $100,000 boat. The contrast is stark. New buyers watch their asset shed value [music] at double the rate of those who buy used. In both cases, the numbers reinforce a simple truth. >> Boats consume capital quietly and consistently in ways that cannot be reversed by upgrades or maintenance. By the second year, the numbers [music] stop feeling like progress and start to feel like gravity. Owners who once saw each expense as an upgrade now track monthly outflows as a steady loss. The excitement of preparation gives way to calculation. How much is left in the checking account after the slip fee, the insurance premium, the maintenance invoice, and the loan payment clear? For many, this is the moment the boat stops being a project and becomes a permanent line on the family budget. Industry data shows a noticeable uptick in used boat listings at the end of year 2 and into year three. Owners who thought they were ahead of the curve realize that the burn rate isn't going away. Some choose to sell, accepting a loss as the price of experience. Others scale back, moving to a smaller slip, dropping insurance coverage, [music] or deferring maintenance. The pattern repeats across marinas and brokerage sites. The second [music] year is when the illusion of control gives way to the reality of ongoing commitment. For every owner who adapts, [music] there's another who decides the cost is too high to carry any further. Today, the real question isn't whether a boat fits your budget, but whether its ongoing cycles fit your life. [music] With annual costs quietly reaching 8 to 12% of the vessel's value, the math [music] stays constant even as excitement fades. In the end, the burn rate is the owner's [music] true horizon.

Is this yacht meant solely for you and your loved ones? Or do you really want it to operate in charter? Not as an alibi, not for two invoices a year. Seriously, as a business, this is the point where everything [music] begins. Because if the yacht is to be your private toy, your private escape from the [music] world, then the logic and structure of the entire setup will be completely different than when you plan a fleet, charter management, clients for charter and a revenue table. [music] The second question is who should be the owner, you as an individual or your company? And the third, where will the yacht actually sail? Mainly in Europe or rather everywhere. Some Mediterranean Sea, some Caribbean, some other waters. If you reverse this order and start with the field flag Malta or flag Cayman Islands, there is a good chance you'll end up with a construction that looks nice in the presentation, but begins to fall apart in real life during the first major inspection. Imagine three people. The first is an owner from Europe. [music] He has a company, is financially well off, and wants to fulfill his dream. A yacht to sail with his family around Italy, Croatia, and south of France. No charter plans, no guests on invoices, just a private yacht. The second person is also a union resident, but thinks differently. I want the yacht to work for me. Charters in the summer, a few weeks for myself, the rest of the season for earning. It should be a business element, not just a hobby. Third person, lives outside [music] the union. Let's say in London after Brexit or in Dubai. The yacht is something global for her. Caribbean winter, Mediterranean summer, the whole world is open. Each of these three cases requires a different response. And right now, we will go through them one by one without dry points, just like talking to clients at the table. [music] Let's start with the first type, a union resident, a private yacht, usually valued up to about €2 million. [music] In the real world, in this configuration, the best and most sensible scenario looks like this. First, [music] we accept that VAT simply has to be paid. You buy a yacht with VAT from a dealer or you import it from abroad and pay [music] V8 upon import into the union. You receive a document that clearly states VAT paid. From that moment on, the yacht is clean throughout the union. During inspection, you just show the paper and that's it. No philosophy involved. Secondly, you choose a flag not for tax optimization but for user comfort and administrative costs. And here Poland very often wins. Why? Because the Polish flag is today in Europe a bit of a bureaucratic paradise for private yachts. Registration is relatively cheap, accessible to owners from different countries. You can handle everything simply and remotely up to 24 m. [music] And the registration itself is indefinite. You don't pay annually to renew the paperwork. Poland does not offer any magical [music] VIT exemption. But if you are still paying VIT, it doesn't make sense to overpay just for the registration [music] because someone advised you to go for a more exotic option. For a private owner from the EU who wants to have a yacht sail peacefully and not invent structures, the combination of paid VAT plus Polish flag is simply sensible. [music] Second scenario, owner from the union, but with a different approach. The yacht is to operate. It is to generate [music] income. Season in Croatia or the Mediterranean Sea, charter calendar, guests, [music] invoices, crew permanently. We no longer talk about a gadget here. We are talking about a business tool. And here [music] comes the question, is it even worth messing around with tax structures, commercial registrations, companies, and a lot of documents? My answer is simple. It makes sense especially [music] when we are talking about yachts around €2 million and above. For smaller vessels, half a million or a million, the cost of the entire entourage, lawyers, advisers, audits, certificates, additional technical requirements can very easily eat up what you save on VAT. Above 235 million, every [music] percent of VAT starts to become really significant. These are amounts where a well ststructured commercial setup can make a huge [music] difference. And now we come to the topic of Malta. If you are a resident of the EU, want the yacht to operate commercially across Europe, and we're talking serious money, Malta is very often the first place to consider for a discussion. Why Malta? Because it is a full member of the union and at the same time a country that has specialized for years in registering commercial yachts. This is not an exotic case. [music] It is a system that works with all its formalities, but it works. In Malta, there is an established model where the yacht is a fixed asset in business operations. [music] It is actively used in chartering. VAT is settled in accordance with EU regulations, [music] but with the appropriate structure and actual navigation outside EU waters, the effective tax burden can be lower than the typical 20some%. [music] Previously, these rates were often spectacularly low. Today, after the intervention of the European Commission, the rules are stricter. It is necessary to prove the route of the cruises. But still, with a large yacht and a wellestablished [music] commercial structure, Malta remains one of the most sensible solutions. Of course, with one condition, all of this must be real, not just on paper. There must be clients, charters, invoices, crew, audits. If you're trying to pretend to be commercial, but in reality, only you and your friends are sailing. It ends exactly like the stories I told in the fifth episode of our series. The third scenario is completely different. The owner lives outside the [music] union. The yacht is global for him. Europe is just one of the stops for a season, for two, for a moment. In such cases, it usually makes no sense to enter EU registers and pay European VAT on the value of the yacht. [music] The natural choice then is the so-called exotic flags, Cayman Islands, Marshall Islands, and similar registries, plus a mechanism called temporary [music] import, which allows for temporary entry into the EU without VAT and customs [music] duties. How does it work in practice? The yacht arrives in Europe as a vessel from outside the European Union. The owner is a resident outside the union. Customs authorities grant a specific period, most often up to 18 months, [music] during which the yacht can stay in EU waters without having to pay V8 on its full value. After this period, the yacht must leave EU waters or go through the full import procedure with tax. For many owners outside Europe, this is an ideal solution. They do not pay VAT on the entire vessel, arrive for the season, sail in the Mediterranean Sea, [music] and then move on to the Caribbean, other waters. But again, one key condition is crucial, the tax residence of the owner. If you are a resident of the union and try to pretend to be a guest from outside through a company in the Cayman Islands or Miami, sooner or later, some authority will determine that it is just a facade and then [music] the real problem begins. To all of this, there is yet another huge piece of the puzzle, financing. Because it's one thing when you buy a yacht with cash and quite another when a loan or lease is involved. In practice, [music] it is easiest to obtain financing in the country where you run your business. The bank knows you there. It knows your turnover, your risk profile. There you have a history and a real relationship with the financial institution. But it has its consequences. When you use financing, very often the formal owner of the yacht for an extended [music] period is the bank or leasing company. They are the ones listed in the documents. They hold the security and they often dictate where the yacht will be registered. From a VAT perspective, leasing has one major advantage. You don't pay the entire tax upfront. You pay it [music] in installments together with the leasing payments. Each leasing invoice includes a portion of VAT. This spreads out the pain over time. If additionally the yacht is used commercially and honestly meaning it is actually operated for charter [music] then the company can deduct this VAT from the installments just like with other fixed assets [music] and then the entire puzzle of financing plus the tax structure begins to fit together very nicely. However, if you put a yacht into a company that manufactures something completely different use it only privately and treat [music] vited deductible expense you risk not only the yacht you risk an audit of the entire business. If I had to summarize all of this for you in a few images, I see three main paths. [music] First, you are a union resident. You want a yacht solely for yourself. We are talking about a vessel below approximately €2 million. The most sensible approach is pay VAT. Have a VAT paid document. Register [music] the yacht under the Polish flag and focus on enjoyment rather than structures. [music] Second, you're a union resident. We're talking about a large yacht. You have a realistic charter plan. [music] You want the yacht to operate for itself. Then we enter the commercial structure with a company full accounting and very often with Malta as the operational flag. This is already the world of business, not a luxury toy. [music] Third, you are a resident outside the union. Europe is just one stage of your seasons. The yacht is large and will sail globally. At that time, exotic flag and temporary import are often the most natural and honest solutions without entering European VAT on the entire value of the yacht. And this is exactly what I wanted to convey to you in that last episode. [music] There is no single magical answer. Instead, there are specific paths and each has its place. Registering a yacht as commercial solely to avoid VAT without actual charter activity. Forcing the yacht into a company that has nothing to do with sailing, just to write off expenses [music] and deduct VAT and creating fictitious offshore structures when you are an EU [music] resident and the yacht is permanently mored in a European marina. It's exactly these kinds of ideas that lead to stories we discussed in episode 5. individual pursuits, long-term processes, fines, and sometimes a ruined reputation in completely different areas of business. [music] When we started this series, I wanted you to be able to say after watching it, I understand why registering the yacht, choosing the flag, and VAT are not just formalities, but crucial decisions for my safety and comfort.
