- Tax Implications of Buying a Yacht | Michelle Otero Valdés: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] there are numerous potential tax implications on buying a yacht here in the state of florida for example we have a tax cap of eighteen thousand dollars for anyone wanting to buy a yacht of any size the max you could ever be required to pay in sales tax is eighteen thousand dollars as that as an example if you are someone say from another state if you're planning to use florida a lot as a staging point to use the vessel say in the caribbean it might be prudent for you to pay the eighteen thousand dollar sales tax for the yacht or alternatively if you're someone from somewhere else you may choose to not pay the tax at all and there's certain ways that the vessel has to be utilized and structured for that purpose

- True Cost of Yacht Ownership: What You Must Know First: Kind: captions Language: en The hidden costs of owning a yacht revealed. You've seen them gliding across turquoise waters, featured in music videos, and parked at million-doll marinas. Yachts. They're the ultimate status symbol. A floating mansion that screams, "I've made it." But what if I told you that owning one could quietly sink your finances? Sure, buying a yacht sounds like a dream. But today, we're revealing the real price of yacht ownership. Spoiler alert, it's not just the sticker price that'll drain your bank account. It's everything else that comes after. Stick around because near the end, we'll cover the one hidden cost that shocks even seasoned yacht owners, and most of them never see it coming. Number one, the purchase price is just the tip of the iceberg. Let's start with the most obvious cost, the yacht itself. Prices vary depending on size, brand, and features. A basic used 30foot yacht may go for $100,000. Mid-range yachts range from $300,000 to $1 million. Ultra luxurious models, think $5 to 50 million and beyond. But here's the trap. People think if I can afford the purchase price, I'm good. Wrong. Buying the yacht is like buying the plane ticket. What you're really paying for is the vacation, the hotel, the food, the excursions, and everything in between. Except in this case, your vacation never ends, and the bills keep coming. Number two, insurance. You can't sail without it. Yacht insurance isn't like car insurance. It's specialized, complex, and expensive. Premiums can range from 1.5% to 3% of the yacht's total value annually. For a $1 million yacht, that's $15,000 to $30,000 every single year. And that's assuming smooth sailing. If you operate in high-risisk areas like hurricane zones or international waters, your rates can skyrocket. Plus, liability insurance is a must if you're inviting friends or renting it out. Imagine paying a monthly insurance premium higher than a luxury apartment rent just for peace of mind. Number three, docking and storage. Paying rent for water wear. You park your yacht matters a lot. Marina slips in popular coastal cities are outrageously expensive. In Miami, Los Angeles, or the French Riviera, annual docking fees can exceed $25,000, and that's for averagesized yachts. Wintertime, you'll likely need to haul your boat out of the water to prevent damage, which means paying for dry storage, shrink wrapping, and additional maintenance. That adds another $3,000 to $10,000 depending on the size. Oh, and prime marina slips often have a waiting list. Some owners even buy waterfront property just to have somewhere to dock their boats. Talk about commitment. Number four, fuel. Your yacht drinks like a rock star. Let's talk fuel because yachts are thirsty beasts. A 60 ft yacht can burn 80 to 100 gall per hour. That's right, per hour. At $5 per gallon, a single 4hour cruise could cost $2,000. Taking a week-l long coastal trip, expect to burn $1,000 to 2,000 gallons of fuel, translating to $5,000 to $10,000 in fuel costs alone. You're not just feeding an engine, you're feeding a monster. This is why many yacht owners calculate their trips by fuel cost first, fun second. Number five, maintenance. Because salt water eats everything, salt water is like sandpaper on steroids. It corrods, it rusts, and it destroys electronics. Annual maintenance, including engine servicing, hull cleaning, painting, propeller inspections, and generator checks, can easily cost 10% of the yacht's value. Let's say you own a $700,000 yacht. That's about $70,000 annually in upkeep. Miss a year, you risk catastrophic damage that could cost you double. Even docked yachts need monthly checks because corrosion doesn't take a vacation. Pro tip: yachts must be cleaned weekly. Algae, barnacles, and salt residue build up fast. Some owners even pay full-time cleaning crews just to keep their boat usable. Number six, crew. Luxury doesn't drive itself. Depending on the size of your yacht, you may need a licensed captain, an engineer, and at least one deck hand. If your yacht has a kitchen or cabins, you may also need a chef and steward. A full crew can cost anywhere between $150,000 to $300,000 a year in salaries. Yes, yearly. And that doesn't include benefits, travel costs, or bonuses for long voyages. Even smaller yachts often need a part-time captain, especially if you don't have the credentials to operate it yourself. Yacht management companies offer crew services, but of course, at a markup. Number seven, upgrades and customization. The endless wish list. Once you step into the yacht world, you'll find yourself constantly tempted to upgrade. A better radar system, new sails, custom teak decking, luxury seating, underwater lighting, Wi-Fi systems, the list never ends. Upgrading navigation software that could cost $10,000. Installing satellite communication around $50,000. Want a jacuzzi on deck? That's easily six figures. And if you host guests, there's pressure to keep up with other yacht owners, especially in social circles where luxury is competitive. Number eight, depreciation. Yachts are not investments. Unlike real estate or certain collectibles, yachts depreciate like cars, only faster. A brand new yacht may lose 20% to 30% of its value in the first year alone. After 5 years, it might be worth half. You might think you can sell your yacht later to recover some costs, maybe, but don't count on it. The used yacht market is flooded with desperate sellers looking to offload financial burdens. If you're looking for investment value, you're better off buying beachfront property or rental real estate. Number nine, taxes and registration. Luxury comes with paperwork. In many countries and US states, owning a yacht comes with annual registration fees, inspections, and sometimes even luxury or use taxes. California and Florida, for example, impose taxes on boats purchased instate international travel. Some nations require special permits, customs declarations, or impose VAT, value added tax on foreign yachts that enter their waters. That vati alone can add 15% to 25% of your yacht's purchase price if not handled properly. Imagine buying a yacht for $500 000 and owing an extra $100,000 in foreign tax just for sailing it across the border. Number 10, chartering it out, not the easy fix. You think you may have heard this common myth? I'll just rent it out when I'm not using it. While possible, turning your yacht into a charter business is anything but simple. You'll need commercial insurance, business registration, inspections, safety certifications, and possibly a licensed charter crew. Also, clients expect luxury, meaning your yacht must always be in top condition. Even with steady bookings, the profits barely cover the increased wear and tear. And if a guest damages something, you're still on the hook. Number 11, safety and legal compliance. Rules of the sea yacht owners must comply with a long list of maritime regulations from life jackets and radios to flare guns and fire extinguishers. Your yacht needs to be legally equipped at all times. If you're hosting guests or chartering, you'll need to meet additional safety codes, get inspected, and fail. You could face steep fines or be grounded until fixed. Plus, any accident on board could lead to liability issues. Many owners hire maritime attorneys to handle the legal side, which is another recurring expense. Number 12, your time. A cost you can't buy back. Even if you hire help, owning a yacht takes time. You'll need to schedule repairs, manage crew, coordinate storage, plan trips, deal with insurance renewals, and handle legal paperwork. Unless you hire a yacht manager, which costs even more, expect your yacht to become your second job. And for many, that time commitment starts to outweigh the joy of ownership. This is why so many owners quietly sell their yachts after just a few seasons. Number 13, the emotional cost. Stress, pressure, and regret. The final cost is the one no. One talks about the emotional burden. Some yacht owners feel buyer remorse after realizing they barely use the boat. Others feel pressure to entertain guests or get their money's worth, turning leisure into obligation. And when something goes wrong, a storm, an engine failure, or an unexpected bill, the stress can eclipse the joy. A yacht should be freedom on the water, but for many, it becomes a floating reminder of a financial misstep. So, is yacht ownership a rich person's dream or a rich person's trap? If you love the ocean, value privacy, and have the money to burn, go for it. But if you think a yacht is just an expensive toy, think again. It's a floating house, a business, and a full-time responsibility. One that comes with more hidden costs than most people ever expect. Now, it's your turn. If money wasn't an issue, would you buy a yacht or just rent one when needed? Let me know in the comments. I'd love to hear your take.

rotten wood and very open grained wood better than conventional epoxy this is because and this is important they're made with low molecular weight resins to reduce the viscosity measured in centio a lower C CPS means less viscosity conventional epoxy is about 850 CPS or fairly thick penetrating epoxy is about 100 CPS so much thinner varnish is about 80 CPS and water is 1 CPS so water will get in a lot of places that epoxy can't even the thinnest epoxy that's what I took from that in fact nothing will penetrate wet wood at all because the pores are already full except for underwater epoxies exposure to water will make epoxy weaker epoxy cannot kill rot no matter how deeply the epoxy penetrates there will always be an area of infected wood between the epoxy and the good wood this is where the rot will begin again what this means for me is if I have a soft spot in the deck I'm just going to cut the top layer off use an oscillating tool with a sharp blade and try to be surgical in your approach cut a square or a rectangle a little bit bigger than the damaged core and gut any wood that's roted make sure the core showing is totally and completely dry you basically cut back to Good Wood replace the missing core and replace the top layer of glass sometimes you can get away with using the old top skin sometimes you have to make a new one and yes this means that you'll have seams in the glass work but at least you did it right and now you know the core under it is totally rot free and fix the leak that let the water in there in the first place boat maintenance tip number three Try it out and if you’re really nuts once you’re done furnishing on top of it or do what you need to but from 10 feet away. It looks like you have mahogany everywhere.
  11. @NogrimStoneson: think of epoxy like the mortar in a brick wall. with out the fiberglass (bricks), the mortar isnt "strong".   so just filling the void with epoxy, is kind of like replacing a broken brick with a giant glob of mortar.

plastic is like wood, the type is really going to be important when it comes to marine applications. i would suggest making space for a small 3d printer and exploring the filament/resins available. theres a lot of those little parts being able to print even temporary replacements can be a life saver.
  12. @richardsuggs8108: Thanks I just subscribed.
  13. @alexshyshkov8585: Azek for Shims? I just 3D-print these. I can make a precise shape and professional look 👍
  14. @charlesbernier9319: I used Gorilla glue to harden up soft spots.  I drilled a bunch of holes, squirt some in and plug the holes with pencil pieces.  Forcing it to expand throughout.
      The beauty is, Gorilla glue works well with wet wood.  In fact, they suggest wetting wood before you use it as a glue.  And though it expands like spray foam, it hardens up nicely and is waterproof.  
       Not a perfect fix, but it’s a great quick and dirty fix.  I fill the holes with white Marine Tex.  I didn’t even paint the decks and it still looked great when I sold the boat.  I told the buyer what I did and he was quite impressed as well.
  15. @derykmacleod4979: Practical Sailor should offer a veteran's price to join up...hint hint
  16. @TheHammer77: ❤❤❤
  17. @oldmansailor: Ok so oil based deck sealer with teak or cedar color/uv, add tong oil, teak oil, and diesel tank biocide... wipe on monthly and the more coats you add, pollishing with a buffer will make it look like varnish. Take that Cetol, no weathering in the sun like yours does, no sanding ever again if you maintain it.

- The Impact of the Shipping Industry on the Environment: Kind: captions Language: en welcome to kode express where we explore the world of Maritime industry and its broader impact on our planet in today's video we're diving into a crucial topic the impact of the shipping industry on the environment shipping is the backbone of global trade responsible for moving 90% of the world's Goods but this massive industry also comes with significant environmental challenges let's explore how shipping affects our oceans air and overall ecosystem the maritime industry a colossal Force driving global trade is a double-edged sword while essential for economic growth its operations have far-reaching environmental consequences from the vast oceans to the delicate Coastal ecosystems the impact is profound to understand the environmental impact we first need to appreciate the scale of the shipping industry over 50,000 Merchant ships are currently active crisscrossing the world's oceans to transport everything from food to electronics this massive Fleet Burns large quantities of fuel primarily heavy fuel oil a byproduct of crude oil refining while shipping is an efficient means of transportation its environmental footprint is substantial the industry's vast reach means its impact on the environment is global and multifaceted one of the most significant environmental impacts of shipping is air pollution ships are a major source of greenhouse gases ghgs particularly carbon dioxide CO2 contributing to global warming the international Maritime organization IMO estimates that shipping accounts for nearly 3% of global CO2 emissions but CO2 isn't the only problem ships also emit sulfur oxides s Sox and nitrogen oxides iox which contribute to acid rain respiratory problems in humans and the formation of fine particulate matter which is harmful to both human health and the environment in recent years there have been efforts to reduce these emissions through regulations like the Imo's sulfur cap which Limits The sulfur content in ship fuel however these regulations are still in the early stages and compliance and enforcement remain significant challenges Beyond air ution the shipping industry also has a direct impact on Marine environments accidental oil spills though less frequent today due to stricter regulations still pose a significant threat to marine life when an oil spill occurs it can devastate local ecosystems killing fish seabirds and other marine animals and causing long-term damage to coral reefs and shorelines but oil spills aren't the only form of marine pollution from ships there's also the issue of B B water Ships Take in ballast water to stabilize themselves and this water is often released at their destination unfortunately ballast water can carry invasive species that disrupt local ecosystems the introduction of non-native species can lead to the decline or Extinction of native species fundamentally altering the balance of marine environments another often overlooked impact of shipping is noise pollution the constant drone of ship engines and the use of sonar navigation create underwater noise that can be harmful to marine life particularly marine mammals like whales and dolphins these animals rely on sound for communication navigation and finding food excessive noise can disrupt these activities leading to disorientation stress and in some cases even death the cumulative effect of noise pollution is an increasingly hostile environment for marine species which can have cascading effects on the entire Marine ecosystem the hum of ship engines a constant Rhythm across the world Seas comes at a significant cost these vessels are major contributors to greenhouse gas emissions releasing carbon dioxide methane and nitrogen oxides into the atmosphere these emissions exacerbate climate change leading to rising sea levels more extreme weather events and disruptions to marine ecosystems the environmental impact of the shipping industry doesn't end when a ship's operational life is is over shipbreaking the process of dismantling old ships often takes place in countries with lack environmental regulations these shipyards can be hazardous both for workers and the environment toxic materials like asbestos heavy metals and oil residues can leech into the surrounding environment polluting water and soil and posing serious health risks to the workers Waste Management on ships is another critical issue despite regulations like marpole which aims to prevent pollution from ships illegal dumping of waste still occurs Plastics chemicals and other waste products can end up in the ocean contributing to marine pollution and harming Wildlife our oceans once pristine and teeming with life are increasingly burdened by plastic pollution microscopic plastic particles often originating from the shipping industry infiltrate the marine food chain with detrimental effects on countless species from seabirds to marine mammals Wildlife is entangled and suffocated by plastic debris leading to population declines the Spectre of oil spills looms large over the shipping industry these catastrophic events often caused by accidents human error or equipment failure wreak havoc on marine life and Coastal communities the cleanup process is arduous and expensive and the ecological damage can be longlasting Beyond Beyond oil spills the discharge of harmful substances and pollutants from ships poses a significant threat to Marine biodiversity Port activities while essential for trade also contribute to environmental challenges air pollution from Port Machinery vessel traffic and cargo handling operations impacts air quality in nearby communities leading to respiratory problems and other health issues additionally Port runoff containing harmful pollutants can contamin Coastal Waters harming marine life and impacting recreational activities recognizing the urgency of the situation the shipping industry governments and international organizations are working together to implement sustainable practices advancements in ship design the development of cleaner fuels and stricter environmental regulations are steps in the right direction despite these challenges there are ongoing efforts to mitigate the shipping industry's environmental impact the IMO has introduced regulations to reduce sulfur emissions and is working on strategies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by at least 50% by 2050 additionally there is increasing interest in alternative fuels like liquefied natural gas LNG hydrogen and even wind and solar power to reduce the industry's Reliance on heavy fuel oil initiatives such as the international Maritime organizations IMO regulations on air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions are crucial in driving industrywide change the development of green ports equipped with Advanced waste management and Pollution Control Systems is another positive step furthermore technological innovations including the use of alternative fuels and energy efficient vessels offer promising Solutions technological innovations such as more efficient ship designs slow steaming and the use of exhaust gas cleaning systems or scrubbers also hold promise for reducing emissions furthermore stricter regulations on ballast Water Management are being implemented to prevent the spread of invasive species the shipping industry is indispensable to our global economy but it comes with significant environmental costs as the world becomes more aware of the importance of sustainability the industry must continue to evolve and adopt Greener practices this includes not only reducing emissions and preventing pollution but also rethinking how ships are built operated and ultimately dismantled the future of shipping depends on our ability to balance economic growth with environmental stewardship as consumers we also have a role to play by supporting companies and policies that prioritize sustainability transitioning to a sustainable shipping industry requires Collective effort and a long-term commitment by prioritizing Environmental Protection investing in clean Technologies and fost in international cooperation we can Safeguard our oceans for future Generations let's work together to create a cleaner healthier Planet by supporting sustainable shipping practices thank you for watching if you found this video informative please give it a thumbs up and don't forget to subscribe for more content on Maritime issues stay safe and see you in the next video

- How cruise ships became a catastrophe for the planet | It's Complicated: Kind: captions Language: en Ecotourism has hit the cruise industry. There's a rollercoaster on board and it's got&nbsp;a smaller environmental footprint, you were saying. And true to form, they're going big on their green innovations. Which might explain why 50% of US citizens believe cruising is environmentally&nbsp; friendly and business is booming. What was once an elitist pastime, is now on a par with&nbsp;many mainstream package holidays. But although many cruise companies claim they are becoming more climate-friendly, the reality can be quite different. So, how did cruise ships&nbsp;become such a catastrophe for the planet? This is the world's largest cruise ship, Royal Caribbean's Icon of the Seas. Its design is typical of modern cruise ships, big, boxy and wide. Which is what we've come to expect. Something that functions less as a ship and more like a floating resort, but they haven't always looked like this. The Prinzessin Victoria&nbsp;Luise, widely regarded as the first purpose built cruise ship, looks completely different. Like&nbsp;many passenger vessels at this time, it has a sleek, streamlined profile. Designed for speed to&nbsp;ferry passengers across the Atlantic as quickly as possible. Over the years these ships became more lavish, prioritising comfort and luxury to attract more wealthy passengers. But the advent&nbsp;of affordable transatlantic flights, meant that there was no longer a demand for travel by sea. As a result cruise ships focused on trips to Caribbean ports and warmer climates. And the&nbsp;design of cruise ships changed to maximise what goes inside and on top of the vessels, rather than speed. Which these days is a lot. The world's largest cruise ship, the Icon of the Seas, carrying&nbsp;nearly 10,000 people. Across its 20 decks, the Icon of the Seas can accommodate the world's largest onboard water park. with six water slides, a 17-metre indoor waterfall, entertainment shows and&nbsp;more than 20 different dining options. But if you're happy to board a slightly smaller vessel, the costs can fall well below the average holiday spend and as prices have fallen,&nbsp;cruise tourism has grown more popular than ever. All this increase in passengers and facilities means&nbsp;that more energy is needed and more waste is produced. To get an idea of the scale of the pollution produced by an average marine fuel-powered cruise ship, it works out as roughly four&nbsp;times more CO2 per passenger than flying. And it can also emit as much particulate matter as 1 million cars every day. The 218 cruise ships operating in Europe in 2022 emitted more than four&nbsp;times more sulphur oxides than all the continent's cars combined. And on top of emissions, there's oil, sewage, plastics and other harmful chemicals that are dumped into the sea. 25% of all ocean waste&nbsp; comes from cruise ships, even though they only account for 1% of the world's merchant fleet. And then there's the noise from cruise ships, which displaces fish and predators. And light&nbsp;emissions from the ships at night can create problems with migration of zooplankton, cephalopods and fish. There are things that cruise ships can do to limit their impact, but most companies are failing to adopt them. Emissions could be as much as 24% lower if ships reduce their speed. And using electrical connections in ports, a cleaner alternative&nbsp;to running highly polluted generators when docked, has resulted in less air pollution&nbsp; and greenhouse gas emissions in the cities of Hamburg, Rostock and Kiel. And ships could also&nbsp;be using fuel cells and renewable hydrogen or methanol, which emit fewer greenhouse gases, but&nbsp;many have opted for liquid natural gas instead. Which does cut carbon dioxide emissions, but emits&nbsp;more greenhouse gas overall, because LNG engines leak methane, which traps about 80 times more heat than CO2 during the 20 years after it's released into the atmosphere. Royal Caribbean has made much&nbsp;of the Icon of the Sea's green innovations, but while its advanced water treatment technology&nbsp; has been welcomed by environmental groups, its waste-to-energy system has limited benefits. Its fuel cell&nbsp;batteries have not been installed due to a problem with the suppliers. And its shore power capability&nbsp;can can only currently be accommodated by 2% of the world's ports. But port cities in Europe have found a solution. An increasing number are restricting cruise ships and some are banning them altogether. Barcelona and Amsterdam are the latest to follow suit. And bans can have a huge impact. Venice&nbsp;was Europe's third most polluted cruise port in 2019, but thanks to a 2021 ban on large cruise ships, the city has reduced sulphur emissions by 80%.

  2. @stevepin8042 [1 likes]: Many cruise ship run on Bunker oil. Bunker fuel has so much sulfur, nitrogen, and other contaminants that, according to a 2009 study, a single large container ship using it produces as much asthma- and cancer-causing pollutants as 50 million cars. Large container ships and large cruise ships have comparable daily fuel consumption, typically ranging between 150 and 250+ metric tons. . While container ships are generally larger and designed for heavier cargo, cruise ships often burn more fuel due to higher speeds and massive energy needs for onboard facilities. The impact on the Ocean is enormous.

- The New 'Billionaire Flex': Why The Ultra-Rich are DITCHING Yachts for Digital Privacy: Kind: captions Language: en You know, for the longest time, we all knew what wealth looked like. You could see it, right? It was the penthouse in Manhattan, the Gwagon, the private jet sitting on the tarmac, that massive super yacht just slicing through the water. These were the ultimate trophies of success. But what if I told you that entire way of thinking is over? That the truly powerful are no longer interested in buying things that we can see. And this quote, man, it just nails it. It gets right to the heart of this huge kind of silent shift that's happening at the very top. The ultimate status symbol isn't about being seen anymore. It's about having the power to just completely vanish from the digital world. So, what gives, right? Why would anyone in their right mind trade a tangible glittering asset for something well for something totally invisible? Okay, let's get into it. Because it turns out that in our hyperconnected world, being seen is no longer a perk. It's become a serious liability. We are literally witnessing the end of an era. The age of display wealth, you know, where your success was measured by how much extravagant stuff you had on show. That's giving way to something way more strategic. The glittering trophies are being quietly taken off the board. And this right here is the core trade-off. It's a very deliberate pivot. The kind of capital that used to buy those yachts and pen houses, it's now being funneled into building these complex, layered, and completely invisible systems of defense. Okay, so why the sudden urge to go dark? Because in today's world, being visible isn't just annoying. It's an active and constant financial risk. What used to be the ultimate reward for success is now basically an open invitation for an attack. We've hit this tipping point where the whole equation has been flipped on its head. The social cred you might get for a flashy car or a public stock portfolio, it's just nothing compared to the financial danger that that same exposure creates. The math just doesn't add up anymore. And these modern threats, they're coming at you from all sides. It's like a three-front war. On one side, you've got criminals and hackers trying to get your data. On another, you have regulators tracking every single move with KYC and anti-moneyaundering rules. And then there's the public itself, which can weaponize your private info through doxing. We all know a yacht is crazy expensive to maintain, but honestly, that's not the real cost. The real cost is the data it spews out everywhere. The public registration, the crew payroll, the docking fees. Every single transaction is a digital breadcrumb that leads right back to the owner. Basically screaming, "Hey, over here, I'm a target." But beyond all those immediate threats, there's a much bigger, more systemic anxiety that's pushing this whole shift into overdrive. It's a deep fear about the very nature of money itself in the very near future. And this is the big one. Central bank digital currencies. The fear isn't just about the government tracking you. It's about something called programmatic control. Just imagine a currency that could be programmed to expire if you don't spend it fast enough or one that literally prevents you from buying certain things. For the wealthy, the threat of losing that financial freedom makes having untraceable private assets an absolute must. So, if they aren't buying yachts anymore, what exactly are they spending these millions on? Well, they're buying something called digital defense infrastructure. This is the new luxury good. Seriously, it's not a single product you can buy off a shelf, but an entire ecosystem of legal structures, specialized software, and intense security protocols. And the goal is simple. Make it as hard as humanly possible to connect the owner to their assets while still letting them manage their wealth without a hitch. And the upgrade is just stark. Instead of publicly traded stocks, think anonymous Dows and these incredibly complex trusts. Instead of using one email for 20 years, think layered LLC's that shield your real identity. And forget a simple home alarm system. The new security is hiring elite teams of hackers called red teams to constantly try and hack your own systems just to find the weaknesses. It is all about defense, not display. And make no mistake, this level of privacy is astronomically expensive. The teams of lawyers, cyber security experts, and accountants it takes to maintain this invisible fortress. Yeah, that can easily cost more per year than just running a super yacht. That's the price of peace of mind in this new world. Now, I know what you might be thinking. This is all fascinating, but I don't have a super yacht or a financial red team. But listen, the principles we're talking about here, they're scalable and they absolutely apply to anyone who wants to protect their financial life. This is such a critical point. Financial privacy isn't some luxury item just for the rich. It's a fundamental necessity for every single one of us. In fact, if you're not super wealthy, you're often the one most at risk because your data is just constantly being taken from you for free. So, what can you actually do? Here are three simple but really powerful steps. First, get skeptical about free financial apps. You are always paying and you're paying with your data. Second, create what I like to call a digital clean room. A phone and an email used only for your finances, never for social media or anything else. And third, start thinking about how to make your emergency fund more resilient and less traceable than just sitting in a single bank account. When you boil it all down, the lesson from the ultra rich is crystal clear. In a world that tracks everything we do, the ability to control what is seen, to operate from the shadows, financially speaking, that has become the ultimate form of power and security. And really, that's the question this whole thing leaves us with. It's not about how much you have anymore. It's about how well you've protected it from prying eyes. Is your financial life an open book for anyone to read, or is it a well-guarded secret? The choice, now more than ever, is yours to make.

- Mastering Saillogger's Geofencing Enhance Your Boat's Security and Efficiency: Kind: captions Language: en in this short tutorial we will explain how to effectively use sale loggers geofencing feature geofencing isn't just about keeping your boat secure it offers a range of versatile applications whether you're managing a personal vessel or running a charter Fleet Geo fencing helps you Monitor and control boat movements in real time for example you can define a safe Zone where your boats should remain or set up specific zones to meet insurance requirements let's dive in and explore how this powerful feature can enhance both safety and operation efficiency when you log into s logger you'll see your boat here our boat is at anchor and S logger shows its details close the popup then zoom in on the map to reveal more details let's now Define a Geo fence a geofence is a virtual boundary drawn around your boat that triggers notifications if your boat leaves that area click the anchor icon in the top right to get started please note that this is different from an anchor alarm we highly recommend that you use an anchor alarm installed on your boat s logger's Geo fencing function further enhances your security by enabling remote monitoring and allowing you to create custom-shaped zones now you can draw a Zone on the map by clicking on at least three points Begin by clicking on a point on the map continue clicking on the map to create your desired Zone feel free to add as many points as you need in this example we are approximating a circle once you're finished click the first point to close the zone you have now set a Geo fence if your boat leaves this Zone you'll immediately receive notifications via email and push notifications on your mobile device provided that you have enabled these features you will also notice that you can now see the track of your boat as well as those of neighboring boats on AIS this provides insight into whether these boats are holding their anchor or possibly dragging you may also notice that nearby boats are displayed in purple or green green indicates the boats you've designated as friends we'll cover this functionality in more detail in future tutorials now let's explore another application of the Geo fencing feature we'll leverage an advanced sa logger function that allows you to switch to a different boat if you have multiple boats and would like to use this feature please please contact us here you can see a boat docked at a marina zoom in to take a closer look now let's proceed by setting a Geo fence Define a zone that you deem safe if the boat leaves this Zone you will be notified here we are going to draw a rectangle you can define an entire Bay or even an entire state or country as a Zone if you'd like once you're finished click on the the first point to close the Zone this feature is useful if you're leaving your boat and want to ensure that no one takes it for a ride while you're away geofencing can be applied in various scenarios for example if you operate a charter Fleet you can establish a safe Zone where your boats are expected to remain or set up Geo fencing zones to meet What's a typical day look like? >> I don't actually think there's a typical day in yacht management. I think there's typical times of the year, you know, from October, we start budgeting for the following year. In January, trying to get the budgets over the line. Either side of the summer, we might be doing a lot of audits and writing up the audits and yacht visits when the yachts are back on the coast in between guest trips and things like that. But it can be anything really. It can be anything from writing up the reports, from checking the accounts, from doing the budget, from catching up with captains to listening to them, you know, being a bit of an agony aunt or uncle for issues that they might have on board because at the end of the day, it's quite a lonely job, I think, for captains, >> even if they have an opera who might be on leave or, you know, they like to bounce things off to make decisions and things like that. That I mean, the whole planet struggled with that, I know, but it was a very different experience for in yaching because everything still kept going, but in a very different way. And I think although I'm not an accountant myself, knowing how to get the information out that is being asked of me is the key thing. I might not be in charge of processing the invoices or even doing the accounts, but when an owning team wants data analysis, for example, I'm best placed to work out why they've spent this much on this in that year when it wasn't in the budget, for example, because I'm the one involved in the daily operations. But equally, you know, I think I started out in compliance and I think that's a very good basis for going forward as a yacht manager because you, you know, you understand the importance of the legislation and what the requirements are. I think the least favorite part of the job, you know, when things are really tough and you're dealing with stressed crew, stressed captain, or worse maybe a stressed owning team is trying to navigate that. It's trying to really listen to what they want, what they might be saying in between the lines and delivering on that. I think the thing with yacht management is it's very boutique. like it's not an offtheshelf product that one size fits all because we're dealing with a personality and a person behind that and it's adapting to that person. So what we offer to one client might not be the same as what we offer to another because of their needs. So yeah, I think that can be tough. It's trying to figure out what they really want. It's learning. It's part of the challenge. - Yacht Captains Estate Managers More in C: Kind: captions Language: en-US Yacht captains and estate managers have more in common than you might think. Both roles demand exceptional organization, adaptability, and a commitment to delivering seamless experiences for owners and guests, whether at sea or on land. Managing a luxury yacht or a sprawling estate requires meticulous attention to detail, financial acumen, and the ability to juggle shifting priorities. Yacht captains plan every voyage with precision, while estate managers use similar methods to maintain daily routines and long-term projects. Both must anticipate needs and adapt to unexpected challenges, ensuring nothing is overlooked. Yacht captains excel at uniting diverse crews, fostering teamwork, and resolving conflicts quickly. Estate managers benefit from this approach, creating a culture where staff feel valued and communication flows freely. Coordinating between hospitality, maintenance, and security teams, they ensure every detail is handled efficiently, delivering a seamless experience for owners and guests. Both professions oversee complex systems, from navigation equipment to security technology. Their logistical skills, such as planning maintenance schedules and coordinating with vendors, keep operations running smoothly and assets protected. Proactive risk management and safety protocols are always top priorities. Adaptability, foresight, and attention to detail make yacht captains exceptional estate managers. Their unique skill set ensures luxury properties are managed to the highest standards, creating memorable experiences for all.

I say it goes beyond that it's about also for a charter,&nbsp;&nbsp; you work together with the broker&nbsp; Etc...because you need to have the perfect&nbsp; experience for the client, but also you need to convince the owner that&nbsp; client is actually the right person to charter the vessel. I mean&nbsp; we're not talking about AMARYLLIS, but in general. Do offers often get declined? Yes. Yes. For various reasons. Yeah for various reasons as you mentioned, it depends on the client. So we do some checks on the client and if the client is not legitimate or if&nbsp; he for example is on a sanction list or what we we don't accept the the offer.&nbsp; That's first. Secondly, when the offer is too discounted the owner might say no&nbsp; I'm not interested there is a charter rate and I okay there is a bit of&nbsp; flexibility but not 30% not 40%, so that's another possibility.&nbsp; Another way sometimes offers are declined because it's too short.&nbsp; For example, if you have an offer for 3 days the owner might say no, I want minimum&nbsp; one week. I'm not interesting in 3 days. Or for example during the Caribbean&nbsp; season, generally they ask for 10 days for Christmas and New Year. So, you might&nbsp; receive a good offer for 7 days and we would say no sorry the owner is looking&nbsp; for 10 days. Okay, so that's because it's someone's private vessel in the end. So, they&nbsp; probably also will like a certain type of clientele. We all know that&nbsp; there's certain charter vessels that are more suited for families or for group of&nbsp; friends or for very private things and I I guess that's also one of the decisions&nbsp; that owner will make. Do these people fit my vessel? Exactly that's also an important criteria&nbsp; as long as we receive this information&nbsp;&nbsp; from the broker because sometimes they&nbsp; disclose this information when we sign the&nbsp; contract. When the owner has agreed on the booking he can always not sign&nbsp; the contract in the end, but generally we try to get as much information as we can.&nbsp; Generally the information comes after, when the client has paid and is&nbsp; organizing his own charter. Even at the beginning of the the inquiry, generally&nbsp; we don't know how many people will come, we don't know the nationality of the&nbsp; client, so we we try to ask those question but sometimes it's really&nbsp; difficult to get that information for for the owner. What's the reason for that? Is that like people don't like&nbsp; to disclose that personal information? They want to&nbsp;&nbsp; protect their clients? Two reasons to that. Some&nbsp; brokers they don't really know&nbsp; their clients. They have the inquiry and other than that it's not to disclose&nbsp; some very important information about their clients. This is what you need to think about before you decide to put an&nbsp; on a offer in. My advice is always look at the crew that is on board instead of&nbsp; looking at the boat that is appealing because of its design or because of its&nbsp; features. I think the reality of the enjoyment comes from the crew and&nbsp; the experience that they deliver when you are on board. So,&nbsp; generally that's why we also participate with the boat to all these&nbsp; charter shows for the brokers to come on board to meet with the crew, see what level&nbsp; of service there is on board, see what food they serve on board, see what&nbsp; water toys they have and all the enjoyment that they can provide to their&nbsp; clients. So look first look for this information not for the technical&nbsp; details or the price of the boat it's mainly the quality of the crew on boat. - Queen Elizabeth’s favourite home | The Royal Yacht Britannia documentary @HistoryCalling: Kind: captions Language: en-GB Step aboard the Royal Yacht Britannia and&nbsp; uncover the secrets of Queen Elizabeth II’s&nbsp;&nbsp; floating home and the only royal residence&nbsp; she and Prince Philip helped to design. In&nbsp;&nbsp; this behind-the-scenes tour from History Calling,&nbsp; I show you footage and photographs taken during&nbsp;&nbsp; my recent visit which showcase the interior&nbsp; world of Britain’s most iconic royal ship,&nbsp;&nbsp; where history, luxury and diplomacy sailed side&nbsp; by side. From the beautiful State Apartments,&nbsp;&nbsp; to the private royal bedrooms and studies and the&nbsp; slightly less comfortable crew quarters, don’t&nbsp;&nbsp; miss the chance to discover the story behind this&nbsp; iconic ship and what life was truly like aboard&nbsp;&nbsp; the yacht that carried the Queen and her family&nbsp; across the globe for over forty years. You’ll even&nbsp;&nbsp; hear which honeymooning couple had the ship’s&nbsp; only double bed brought aboard for their use! Discussions for a new royal yacht to replace&nbsp; the aging and increasingly fragile ship,&nbsp;&nbsp; the Victoria and Albert, dated back to the&nbsp; short reign of Edward VIII in 1936, but it&nbsp;&nbsp; was only at the start of the reign of Elizabeth&nbsp; II that the plans were finally put into motion.&nbsp;&nbsp; The Queen and her husband, the Duke of&nbsp; Edinburgh (later created Prince Philip),&nbsp;&nbsp; were able to have a strong say in the design,&nbsp; making Britannia the only royal home they really&nbsp;&nbsp; had an input into during her reign, as all the&nbsp; other royal residences were already built and no&nbsp;&nbsp; others would be constructed for the monarch. The&nbsp; keel of what was then known as ship no. 691 was&nbsp;&nbsp; laid in Clydebank, Scotland on 11 June 1952 and&nbsp; the interiors were designed by Patrick McBride&nbsp;&nbsp; and Sir Hugh Casson, after McBride’s initial&nbsp; plans were deemed too ostentatious. Contrary&nbsp;&nbsp; to the 17th century French inspiration he had&nbsp; used as a template, Elizabeth and Philip didn’t&nbsp;&nbsp; want a floating palace, they wanted something&nbsp; simpler and more informal than what their other&nbsp;&nbsp; homes offered. The result would be a ship with a&nbsp; comfortable country house feel to it, at least in&nbsp;&nbsp; the apartments used by the royals. As for the&nbsp; exterior, the paint scheme was to be navy and&nbsp;&nbsp; red with gold trim. On 16 April 1953 she was ready&nbsp; to go into the water and the Queen and the Duke of&nbsp;&nbsp; Edinburgh attended as the ship was both launched&nbsp; and formally named Britannia, by Her Majesty.&nbsp;&nbsp; This name had been chosen by the royal couple and&nbsp; demonstrated the ship’s links to its homeland,&nbsp;&nbsp; as that was the name given to the land by the&nbsp; Romans and to the female personification of&nbsp;&nbsp; Great Britain which you’ll often see in images and&nbsp; statues, or hear referenced in poetry (it’s a bit&nbsp;&nbsp; like Uncle Sam in America). It had also been used&nbsp; before for ships including those of the Royal Navy&nbsp;&nbsp; and for a cutter built for the future Edward VII&nbsp; and also used by his son, George V and of course&nbsp;&nbsp; I’m sure you’ve all heard of the very famous song,&nbsp; Rule Britannia. The new royal yacht was (and still&nbsp;&nbsp; is) 412 feet and 3 inches long which is not&nbsp; huge, but nevertheless a very respectable size. With the shell built, now the work of outfitting&nbsp; the ship, including its interiors, could begin in&nbsp;&nbsp; earnest. The hollow masts were put in place, each&nbsp; with a few coins beneath them as was tradition and&nbsp;&nbsp; the funnel was added. Britannia would have&nbsp; some other interesting design and running&nbsp;&nbsp; details too. The windows of the royal apartments&nbsp; are set slightly higher than on a normal ship&nbsp;&nbsp; to afford the family more privacy and the ship&nbsp; was to be run with as little noise as possible,&nbsp;&nbsp; with orders on deck communicated via signals&nbsp; made with flags, paddles and notice boards,&nbsp;&nbsp; rather than by voice. Again, this was to make&nbsp; life onboard more pleasant for the royals. In&nbsp;&nbsp; the main drawing room the fire had to electric&nbsp; too so that a sailor wouldn’t need to stand next&nbsp;&nbsp; to an open flame with a bucket all day long (as&nbsp; per navy rules) in case it went out of control. The total cost of her construction was £2,098,000&nbsp; and she was ready for sea trials by November 1953,&nbsp;&nbsp; which Philip attended. She was then formally&nbsp; commissioned on 7 January 1954. In the meantime,&nbsp;&nbsp; her crew had been selected and so were ready&nbsp; to carry out additional trials to learn how&nbsp;&nbsp; the ship handled and practice the manoeuvres they&nbsp; expected to have to make on her. Her first stint&nbsp;&nbsp; of duty carrying the royal family finally came&nbsp; on 14 April in Portsmouth. The Queen and the&nbsp;&nbsp; Duke of Edinburgh were finishing up a tour of the&nbsp; Commonwealth at the time, while their children,&nbsp;&nbsp; Prince Charles and Princess Anne, stayed at home&nbsp; with the Queen Mother and Princess Margaret. It&nbsp;&nbsp; was the little Prince and Princess who took the&nbsp; first royal voyage on the new yacht, having been&nbsp;&nbsp; dropped off there with their governess and other&nbsp; members of the royal household and left in their&nbsp;&nbsp; care and that of the ship’s crew. Britannia then&nbsp; sailed to Malta, arriving eight days later and&nbsp;&nbsp; picking up the Earl and Countess Mountbatten&nbsp; whilst there, then headed on to Libya where&nbsp;&nbsp; it collected the Queen and the Duke on 30 April.&nbsp; Then it was back to Malta where some additional&nbsp;&nbsp; furniture and royal luggage was collected,&nbsp; before heading home to London via Gibraltar.&nbsp;&nbsp; She arrived and sailed up the Thames and beneath&nbsp; Tower Bridge on 15 May to a rapturous welcome.&nbsp;&nbsp; It had been a very good beginning to what would be&nbsp; a spectacular 44 year run for the new royal yacht. Before we get any further into Britannia’s many&nbsp; voyages however, let’s stop and talk about what&nbsp;&nbsp; life was like onboard her for both royals&nbsp; and crew. Britannia was surely the smallest&nbsp;&nbsp; of the monarch’s official residences (if&nbsp; we can call a boat an official residence),&nbsp;&nbsp; but and I say this as someone who’s visited quite&nbsp; a few of them, it’s probably the one that gives&nbsp;&nbsp; you the best idea of what Queen Elizabeth was like&nbsp; behind closed doors and what she liked in a home,&nbsp;&nbsp; no doubt due to her aforementioned input into&nbsp; its design and décor. It’s quite fancy to us mere&nbsp;&nbsp; mortals, but very simple by royal standards and&nbsp; I’m sure lots of millionaires and billionaires out&nbsp;&nbsp; there have far more elaborate private yachts. The&nbsp; Queen and Prince Philip had their own bedrooms,&nbsp;&nbsp; each with a single bed and decorated according&nbsp; to their tastes. The Queen’s was outfitted in&nbsp;&nbsp; light colours, with a bed, a desk and mirror&nbsp; and bedside tables and lamps. Prince Philip’s&nbsp;&nbsp; had a red colour scheme for the linens and&nbsp; darker wood for the furniture to give it a&nbsp;&nbsp; more masculine feel. Across the hall was&nbsp; another little suite for other members of&nbsp;&nbsp; the family and here we find the only double bed&nbsp; on the ship, brought onboard at the request of&nbsp;&nbsp; the then Prince Charles for his honeymoon with&nbsp; Princess Diana in 1981. There were also phones&nbsp;&nbsp; in the rooms so that an attendant could be called&nbsp; if a member of the family needed anything. Other,&nbsp;&nbsp; smaller bedrooms could be used as&nbsp; royal nurseries for the children. The State Dining room was where much of the&nbsp; formal entertaining took place and took an&nbsp;&nbsp; incredible three hours to set up properly&nbsp; for a state dinner as everything had to be&nbsp;&nbsp; perfectly laid out. The menus could be taken&nbsp; home as mementoes by those lucky enough to get&nbsp;&nbsp; an invite. Around the sides of the room are&nbsp; various souvenirs acquired by the royals on&nbsp;&nbsp; their many journeys, including a head from&nbsp; Easter Island gifted to the family in 1971.&nbsp;&nbsp; Britannia had its own band which would play for&nbsp; the family during dinners, whilst during state&nbsp;&nbsp; visits they would also play the national anthem&nbsp; of the host country on the outer deck. The dining&nbsp;&nbsp; room could also be converted into an onboard&nbsp; cinema for the family, with the officers and some&nbsp;&nbsp; of the yachtsmen allowed to come in and watch the&nbsp; movies with them and there is a dance floor under&nbsp;&nbsp; the carpet which was used during Princess Anne’s&nbsp; 21st birthday party. Just beyond this room are the&nbsp;&nbsp; separate studies of Her Majesty and Prince Philip,&nbsp; one on either side of the hallway and included so&nbsp;&nbsp; that the couple could continue to attend to their&nbsp; duties whilst onboard as unfortunately the Queen&nbsp;&nbsp; never really got a day off (bar Christmas),&nbsp; no matter where she was in the world. Again,&nbsp;&nbsp; the décor reflects their different tastes.&nbsp; Philip had a replica of one of the boats he&nbsp;&nbsp; had served in in the Royal Navy over his desk for&nbsp; instance, while Elizabeth had more flowery fabric&nbsp;&nbsp; on the chairs and pictures of her parents. There&nbsp; is in fact a really lovely selection of informal&nbsp;&nbsp; photographs of the royal family on display in&nbsp; these rooms, as well as elsewhere on the ship.&nbsp;&nbsp; Moving down the hallway you come to an ante&nbsp; room with chairs which the family and their&nbsp;&nbsp; guests could spill out into if necessary from the&nbsp; adjoining drawing room which looks very cosy with&nbsp;&nbsp; its squishy armchairs in flowery fabric, tables&nbsp; set up for games like backgammon, a piano that&nbsp;&nbsp; was played by the likes of Princesses Margaret and&nbsp; Diana and of course the aforementioned electric&nbsp;&nbsp; fire. The only thing I don’t recall seeing&nbsp; were the royal loos and baths or showers,&nbsp;&nbsp; but perhaps I missed them or they were omitted&nbsp; from the tour for the sake of royal modesty. Outside, the verandah deck could be used for&nbsp; games, lounging around, painting (Prince Philip&nbsp;&nbsp; liked to do that sometimes) or even water sports,&nbsp; as a little collapsible pool could be put out for&nbsp;&nbsp; the family’s use. Just inside is a sunroom with&nbsp; wicker deck chairs purchased by Philip in Hong&nbsp;&nbsp; Kong in 1957 and a bar for those who didn’t fancy&nbsp; getting splashed. Britannia carried some unusual&nbsp;&nbsp; cargo on deck as well. There was a spot onboard&nbsp; for a Rolls Royce and a land rover for instance,&nbsp;&nbsp; which had to be hoisted on and off the ship so&nbsp; that they could be used during royal visits and&nbsp;&nbsp; holidays or even taken on and off on special&nbsp; rafts. Nowadays these are sitting on shore,&nbsp;&nbsp; just next to the yacht though I’m afraid&nbsp; they aren’t the originals and alongside&nbsp;&nbsp; them is a smaller boat known as the Royal&nbsp; Barge which could ferry people to shore and&nbsp;&nbsp; back when it wasn’t possible for Britannia&nbsp; to dock. You might also recognise it from&nbsp;&nbsp; the 2012 celebrations for Elizabeth’s diamond&nbsp; jubilee when it carried her and Prince Philip&nbsp;&nbsp; down the Thames as part of the jubilee&nbsp; pageant (in the pouring rain by the way). So much for the formal and informal royal&nbsp; quarters. Let’s now turn to where the crew&nbsp;&nbsp; lived and by the way, if you’re wondering where&nbsp; I’m getting all my information about the ship,&nbsp;&nbsp; part of it comes from the fact that I’m creating&nbsp; this video just three days after I was actually&nbsp;&nbsp; on Britannia myself and the rest is from an&nbsp; official history of the ship written by Richard&nbsp;&nbsp; Johnstone-Bryden which I’ll leave linked below&nbsp; for you. It’s full of all the information you&nbsp;&nbsp; could want about the ship’s adventures and lots&nbsp; of great anecdotes which I can’t fit in here. As you might imagine, the crew’s bedrooms were&nbsp; considerably less comfortable than the royals and&nbsp;&nbsp; only the most senior amongst them would get his&nbsp; own room and even a bath. For the lower ranked men&nbsp;&nbsp; there were bunkbeds in shared rooms which could&nbsp; take up to twelve people at once. The slightly&nbsp;&nbsp; nicer ones have fewer bunks, more space to move&nbsp; around in and even the odd TV and little privacy&nbsp;&nbsp; curtains run alongside the beds. What I didn’t see&nbsp; very often in crew quarters though, were windows&nbsp;&nbsp; and even the beds were, in some cases, only&nbsp; put in during a 1972-73 refit. Prior to that,&nbsp;&nbsp; many of the men were sleeping in hammocks. There&nbsp; were several recreational spaces too, kitted out&nbsp;&nbsp; with settees, TVs, books, little bar areas and&nbsp; space to relax and as the ship hadn’t been built&nbsp;&nbsp; with enough accommodation for the royal band, some&nbsp; of its members had to sleep on camp beds in the&nbsp;&nbsp; unwinding room after the yachtsmen had gone to bed&nbsp; themselves and it was apparently a bit smoky and&nbsp;&nbsp; bumpy. The officers also had a very nice dining&nbsp; room with signed photos from the royals and other&nbsp;&nbsp; memorabilia around the edges of the room. Both&nbsp; this and the little den next to it are decorated&nbsp;&nbsp; with furnishings sporting Tudor roses on them&nbsp; too, which I thought was a nice touch. In all of&nbsp;&nbsp; these recreational rooms there are photos of the&nbsp; royal family and souvenirs either acquired during&nbsp;&nbsp; the ship’s travels or brought over from other&nbsp; royal vessels of the past. Britannia was in fact&nbsp;&nbsp; something of a floating royal museum even before&nbsp; it was decommissioned and opened to the public. I may not have spotted the royal loos, but I&nbsp; did see the shared bathrooms and shower areas&nbsp;&nbsp; frequented by most of those onboard, plus&nbsp; a laundry room to keep everyone’s clothes&nbsp;&nbsp; (including the royals) in pristine condition and&nbsp; a tiny mail room to receive word from home. The&nbsp;&nbsp; shop in the bowels of the ship was open to&nbsp; all too and was sometimes frequented by the&nbsp;&nbsp; royal children when they were allowed&nbsp; to buy sweets and let me tell you,&nbsp;&nbsp; if I’d had any room in my luggage, I would&nbsp; have bought some of the fudge they were selling&nbsp;&nbsp; because I was allowed a little sliver of it as&nbsp; a sample and it was very nice. Unfortunately,&nbsp;&nbsp; when you’re only allowed a rucksack on the plane&nbsp; though, space is at a premium. The kitchens and&nbsp;&nbsp; storerooms to keep things like plates,&nbsp; glasses and cutlery are all downstairs&nbsp;&nbsp; and of course once you’re out of the state&nbsp; and family apartments the décor you see in&nbsp;&nbsp; the ship’s corridors isn’t quite as good, though&nbsp; everything is very neat, clean and functional. For those unlucky enough to fall ill or be&nbsp; injured, the ship has a medical bay which could&nbsp;&nbsp; take up to three patients at once and this could&nbsp; also be used on the rare occasion when a distress&nbsp;&nbsp; call was received from another ship requesting&nbsp; medical assistance for one of their crew.&nbsp;&nbsp; Britannia responded to such a call in January&nbsp; 1963 for instance, whilst en route to Tahiti,&nbsp;&nbsp; changing course to take onboard a man suffering&nbsp; serious burns from the SS Glenmoor. Thankfully&nbsp;&nbsp; he made a good recovery. Then there were the&nbsp; engine rooms, always kept absolutely gleaming&nbsp;&nbsp; and there of course to keep the entire&nbsp; show on the road, or rather the waves.&nbsp;&nbsp; This room was kept so immaculate that people were&nbsp; asked to wipe their feet on a mat before going&nbsp;&nbsp; into it, rather than when coming out of it,&nbsp; an extremely unusual state of affairs onboard&nbsp;&nbsp; a ship. Up on deck was the bridge from where&nbsp; everything was managed and courses plotted and&nbsp;&nbsp; Britannia’s original wheelhouse came from the 1893&nbsp; sailing yacht of the same name that I’ve already&nbsp;&nbsp; mentioned and which was raced by George V during&nbsp; Cowes Week (a sailing regatta held on the Isle of&nbsp;&nbsp; Wight) after World War One. Let’s not forget the&nbsp; decks and walkways around the entire ship either&nbsp;&nbsp; which will have afforded some stunning views&nbsp; over the years as Britannia sailed the world. The ship was used for numerous royal tours and&nbsp; other activities over the years starting from the&nbsp;&nbsp; year she was completed, for Prince Philip took her&nbsp; to Canada in 1954. Then, in 1955 Princess Margaret&nbsp;&nbsp; used it for a tour of the Caribbean and in that&nbsp; same year it took the Queen and Philip to Norway.&nbsp;&nbsp; It would be tedious to go through all the voyages&nbsp; the Royal Yacht went on, which encompassed almost&nbsp;&nbsp; the entire world that’s accessible by water,&nbsp; so let me just give you some statistics for&nbsp;&nbsp; what she accomplished between 1954 and December&nbsp; 1997. There were 85 State Visits, 162 visits to&nbsp;&nbsp; non-Commonwealth countries by the Queen and 90&nbsp; to Commonwealth countries by Her Majesty, 133&nbsp;&nbsp; visits to Commonwealth countries by other members&nbsp; of the Royal Family and 226 to non-Commonwealth&nbsp;&nbsp; countries by them. 162 visits by the Queen within&nbsp; UK waters and 110 such visits by other members of&nbsp;&nbsp; the family. In short, Britannia was very well&nbsp; used and though I can’t run through all of her&nbsp;&nbsp; activities here, allow me to tell you about&nbsp; some of her most notable trips and adventures.&nbsp;&nbsp; There was Prince Philip’s world tour of&nbsp; 1956 into 1957 which included opening the&nbsp;&nbsp; 1956 Summer Olympics in Melbourne and a trek&nbsp; to the Antarctic (as seen in the TV show,&nbsp;&nbsp; The Crown) from where he made a live Christmas&nbsp; Day speech which directly followed the Queen’s&nbsp;&nbsp; and which was read out from Her Majesty’s sitting&nbsp; room at 6.56am local time (I know this isn’t very&nbsp;&nbsp; seasonably appropriate if you’re not watching&nbsp; this in December, but as we’re on the topic,&nbsp;&nbsp; you might also want to check out my video&nbsp; all about the history of the royal Christmas&nbsp;&nbsp; broadcasts. I’ll leave it linked for you). In 1959&nbsp; he and Queen Elizabeth took Britannia to Canada,&nbsp;&nbsp; hosting President Eisenhower onboard at one&nbsp; point during the trip and in 1960 it took&nbsp;&nbsp; Princess Margaret and her husband, the Earl of&nbsp; Snowden to the Caribbean for their honeymoon.&nbsp;&nbsp; Four years later the Queen Mother used it for a&nbsp; convalescent cruise to recover from having her&nbsp;&nbsp; appendix removed and in 1965 it was with the Queen&nbsp; and Prince Philip for an historic trip to Germany,&nbsp;&nbsp; the first time a British monarch had set foot&nbsp; there since 1913. There were also annual family&nbsp;&nbsp; cruises around the western isles of Scotland&nbsp; where the royals could disembark for quiet&nbsp;&nbsp; picnics on deserted beaches, well away from&nbsp; the prying eyes of both press and public and&nbsp;&nbsp; the honeymoon of Princess Anne and Captain&nbsp; Mark Philips in 1973. It was involved in the&nbsp;&nbsp; celebrations for the Queen’s Silver Jubilee in&nbsp; 1977 and in 1983 President Reagan and his wife,&nbsp;&nbsp; Nancy, even spent part of their 31st wedding&nbsp; anniversary onboard, with the Queen throwing&nbsp;&nbsp; them a party and having them to stay overnight.&nbsp; Britannia wasn’t just for parties though. She&nbsp;&nbsp; could be and was a lifesaving vessel on occasion,&nbsp; as the next story I have to share with you, shows. One of the most famous jobs the royal yacht ever&nbsp; fulfilled was when it helped to evacuate refugees&nbsp;&nbsp; from Aden in South Yemen in January 1986 after&nbsp; civil war broke out there. Imagine for a minute&nbsp;&nbsp; what an incredible sight that must have been for&nbsp; those stuck on the beaches, exhausted and starving&nbsp;&nbsp; according to the reports from Britannia’s crew and&nbsp; in fear of their lives, to see nothing less than&nbsp;&nbsp; the Queen’s own yacht coming over the horizon&nbsp; with Union Jacks flying so that the refugees&nbsp;&nbsp; would recognise her. It must have been one of the&nbsp; best moments of their lives and it was certainly&nbsp;&nbsp; great PR for the ship. Between 17 and 23 January&nbsp; Britannia rescued 1082 people of 55 nationalities,&nbsp;&nbsp; plus one French dog, despite the fact that&nbsp; fighting broke out on the beaches at one point,&nbsp;&nbsp; with shells landing in the water between Britannia&nbsp; and the shore and rebels who had to be bought off&nbsp;&nbsp; with supplies including cigarettes, chocolate&nbsp; and alcohol from the ship in order to get them&nbsp;&nbsp; to allow the refugees to leave. The feat was&nbsp; accomplished by using little boats called jolly&nbsp;&nbsp; boats to ferry them out to the ship where they&nbsp; were given a blanket, registered at a temporary&nbsp;&nbsp; registration point set up in the Verandah, then&nbsp; given hot soup and shown to their accommodation&nbsp;&nbsp; in the royal apartments. The dining room and&nbsp; drawing room had had their furniture removed&nbsp;&nbsp; to make space for the unexpected passengers&nbsp; and if you’re wondering why there were so many&nbsp;&nbsp; nationalities, it’s because although Britannia&nbsp; was technically sent to collect British subjects,&nbsp;&nbsp; out of humanity the crew took onboard any refugees&nbsp; that they could. No one was rescued at the expense&nbsp;&nbsp; of anyone else though and at one point the ship&nbsp; turned back to pick up one London bus driver who&nbsp;&nbsp; had arrived on the beach half an hour after&nbsp; Britannia had left. He later told the press&nbsp;&nbsp; that it brought tears to his eyes to see Britannia&nbsp; return for him, saying ‘The Queen’s yacht turned&nbsp;&nbsp; back for me, just for me. I can’t believe&nbsp; it’. Guys, I have to tell you, even I welled&nbsp;&nbsp; up just reading that. How has there not been&nbsp; a Hollywood movie made about this episode yet? After all this drama it was back to&nbsp; normal operations very quickly and&nbsp;&nbsp; a few months later the yacht hosted the new&nbsp; Duke and Duchess of York on their honeymoon&nbsp;&nbsp; (this was the last honeymoon cruise it would&nbsp; provide by the way). Then there was an historic&nbsp;&nbsp; state visit to China that September which marked&nbsp; the first time a reigning British monarch had gone&nbsp;&nbsp; there and was a sign of improving relations&nbsp; between the two countries. notable events,&nbsp;&nbsp; duties and voyages included the time Britannia&nbsp; got frozen in its berth on the Hudson River in&nbsp;&nbsp; New York in February 1994 (there was even&nbsp; a snowman built on the verandah deck),&nbsp;&nbsp; her involvement in the commemorations for D&nbsp; Day later that year, followed by an historic&nbsp;&nbsp; state visit to post-Soviet Russia (the first time&nbsp; a British monarch had been there since before the&nbsp;&nbsp; murder of the Russian royals to whom the Queen and&nbsp; Prince Philip were related, in 1918). There was&nbsp;&nbsp; another to South Africa in 1995 and both were&nbsp; of enormous political importance at the time.&nbsp;&nbsp; The trip to South Africa was known even then to be&nbsp; the last state visit Britannia would make however,&nbsp;&nbsp; as she was now reaching the end of&nbsp; her life as a commissioned yacht. The Queen and Prince Philip had actually&nbsp; offered to give up the ship in 1968,&nbsp;&nbsp; when the country was facing economic hardship,&nbsp; as a show of unity with the British people,&nbsp;&nbsp; however this offer was not taken up, though other&nbsp; jobs were found for Britannia over the years so&nbsp;&nbsp; that she could be useful in between royal trips&nbsp; and therefore be even better value for money. She&nbsp;&nbsp; was used in Royal Navy exercises for instance.&nbsp; Despite her range of uses however, by the mid&nbsp;&nbsp; 1990s, there was serious talk of decommissioning&nbsp; the now more than 40-year-old ship, given her age&nbsp;&nbsp; and the cost of maintaining her. There were&nbsp; plans though to replace her with a new boat,&nbsp;&nbsp; for the royal yacht had proven over its lifetime&nbsp; to be very popular with the people of both the&nbsp;&nbsp; UK and the many countries it went to visit. Huge&nbsp; numbers coming out to see it on its travels and it&nbsp;&nbsp; was considered an honour to be invited onboard&nbsp; to stay with, or even just dine with the Queen&nbsp;&nbsp; and Prince Philip. It allowed them to flatter&nbsp; the dignitaries they visited in other countries&nbsp;&nbsp; and bring a little of the glamour and prestige&nbsp; of a royal residence to them, for after all,&nbsp;&nbsp; you can’t cart Buckingham Palace or Windsor&nbsp; Castle around the world with you. As such, it was&nbsp;&nbsp; invaluable in promoting the so-called soft power&nbsp; of the monarchy. It also provided accommodation&nbsp;&nbsp; and an office base for their support staff to work&nbsp; from during royal trips, instead of having to find&nbsp;&nbsp; them hotel accommodation everywhere they went.&nbsp; This was an added convenience and when you go&nbsp;&nbsp; to Britannia today these offices are preserved&nbsp; as they were at the time of decommissioning,&nbsp;&nbsp; complete with mid 1990s’ computers which&nbsp; look like dinosaurs of the digital age now.&nbsp;&nbsp; As well as the aforementioned naval exercises&nbsp; the government even used it to host industry&nbsp;&nbsp; and trade leaders to help drum up investment&nbsp; in the United Kingdom with several British&nbsp;&nbsp; politicians commenting on the fact that they&nbsp; could get people to come to such events (known&nbsp;&nbsp; as Sea Days) who would never accept an invitation&nbsp; if it was being held in a hotel or conference&nbsp;&nbsp; centre. Britannia was iconic in the way that&nbsp; something like Air Force One was and is today.&nbsp;&nbsp; Sadly though, it had sailed its course.&nbsp; In January 1997 it was announced that she&nbsp;&nbsp; would be decommissioned by the end of the&nbsp; year and although several plans for a new,&nbsp;&nbsp; supposedly cheaper-to-run yacht, had been drawn&nbsp; up, ultimately the idea was dropped in October&nbsp;&nbsp; by the new Labour government which had come&nbsp; into power in May. Britannia’s last official&nbsp;&nbsp; overseas duty was to take Prince Charles to Hong&nbsp; Kong to be present at the handing over of this&nbsp;&nbsp; former British colony to the Chinese on 1 July. It&nbsp; then brought the Prince and the last governor of&nbsp;&nbsp; Hong Kong and his family to Manila from where&nbsp; they could fly back to the UK. There was one&nbsp;&nbsp; last family cruise around the Western Isles in&nbsp; August, then a farewell tour around the United&nbsp;&nbsp; Kingdom beginning in October and lasting until&nbsp; December when it was finally time to say goodbye. On the morning of 11th Queen Elizabeth II boarded&nbsp; her ship for the last time (or at least the last&nbsp;&nbsp; time while it was still hers) to say farewell to&nbsp; it and to its crew. She was pipped off again at&nbsp;&nbsp; 3.01pm to watch the decommissioning ceremony&nbsp; and if you look closely, you’ll notice that&nbsp;&nbsp; the clocks on Britannia have remained frozen at&nbsp; that time almost ever since. It was the end of an&nbsp;&nbsp; era and Elizabeth was seen to shed a tear at the&nbsp; decommissioning ceremony for the ship which held&nbsp;&nbsp; so many memories for her and which she had said&nbsp; was the only place she could really be herself.&nbsp;&nbsp; Britannia had sailed 1,087,623 miles since 1954&nbsp; and although there had been plans to scrap her&nbsp;&nbsp; rather than let her languish and deteriorate&nbsp; somewhere, it was decided to turn her into&nbsp;&nbsp; a floating museum and she was sent to Leith in&nbsp; Edinburgh for that purpose where you can visit her&nbsp;&nbsp; today, though she’s only accessible via a shopping&nbsp; centre which has always seemed quite odd to me.&nbsp;&nbsp; She is one of Scotland’s most popular attractions&nbsp; and rightly so. If you get the chance to visit&nbsp;&nbsp; her, it’s well worth your time in my opinion. She&nbsp; was renovated and new entrances installed in the&nbsp;&nbsp; hull so that visitors could come aboard her more&nbsp; easily and glass panels were put into the walls&nbsp;&nbsp; of rooms like the royal bedrooms and studies, so&nbsp; that visitors can look in without walking through&nbsp;&nbsp; the rooms. The royal family also allowed enough&nbsp; of their belongings to remain onboard so that&nbsp;&nbsp; tourists can get a real sense of what the ship&nbsp; was like when they used it. She was opened to&nbsp;&nbsp; the public in October 1998 and also made available&nbsp; for hire for private events. This latter function&nbsp;&nbsp; has ensured that the royals have been back&nbsp; onboard since her decommissioning. For example,&nbsp;&nbsp; Queen Elizabeth’s granddaughter, Zara Philips and&nbsp; her soon-to-be husband Mike Tindall, hosted a pre&nbsp;&nbsp; wedding party onboard her the day before their&nbsp; marriage (which took place in Edinburgh) in 2011. I hope you enjoyed that history of and&nbsp; behind the scenes look at Britannia.&nbsp;&nbsp; Please consider giving the video a thumbs up&nbsp; as that really helps YouTube (and myself) to&nbsp;&nbsp; know that you liked it and if you haven’t&nbsp; already done so, please do subscribe and&nbsp;&nbsp; switch on the notification bell to get more&nbsp; from me. I’d love to have you stick around.&nbsp;&nbsp; For more from History Calling you can also&nbsp; become a YouTube member, or join me on Patreon&nbsp;&nbsp; where I share bonus material such as early&nbsp; access to ad-free videos and bonus podcasts&nbsp;&nbsp; about history and also life as a YouTuber. The&nbsp; link is below for you, as are links to my book,&nbsp;&nbsp; Find Your Irish Ancestors Online, available from&nbsp; Amazon and my website, historycallingofficial.com&nbsp;&nbsp; where you can get two complimentary downloads&nbsp; if you sign up to my free mailing list. Let me know below if you think the King&nbsp; should get another royal yacht to replace&nbsp;&nbsp; Britannia and if you enjoyed this video&nbsp; you’re definitely going to want to check&nbsp;&nbsp; out my episode on what I think is the most&nbsp; famous British royal residence of them all;&nbsp;&nbsp; Buckingham Palace. I hope you enjoy&nbsp; it and until next time, keep learning.

over then it disappears over the horizon i can see the the glimmer from the star kind of diminished our hearts sank because we realized you know we're not going to get rescued two more hours pass and
